Make sure to include fileciteturn0file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

# **Chapter One: Something Wrong**

Junction Seven. The plaque held twenty-three names. Jhace read each one in order. Harren Corl. Bessa Tyne. Mikael Stradd. His jaw stayed tight. No ceremony. Just the reading. Just the accountability that sits in your chest and doesn't leave.

He pulled his inspector kit from his pack. Slate, chalk, cord, collar keys, wedge slips, lamp oil—each item confirmed by touch, fingers moving through the sequence without looking. Everything present.

He logged the time on his slate: Tide 0, Kindling. Wrote below it: start of route.

The grid-hum ran through everything in Saint Merro—pipes, walls, floors. Constant low vibration. Background noise you learned not to hear. Jhace pressed his palm to the main pipe at Junction Seven. The hum arrived through his skin. Normal baseline present. But something else rode with it. A second pitch. Doubled harmonic.

He shifted his hand. Different contact point, different pressure. The doubled harmonic stayed.

He leaned in. Put his ear to the metal. Cheek cooled fast against the pipe. The second pitch carried a narrow edge. He felt it in his teeth.

Dust fell from a ceiling seam. Thin strip, maybe a finger-width across. Jhace watched it fall through his lamp light. Counted his breaths. Three. Four. Five. The dust fell again. Same pattern.

He wrote on his slate: repeat interval.

The collar seam showed no active steam. Sealant bead intact. No obvious leak. He moved to Junction Nine. Pressed his palm to the pipe there. Same doubled harmonic. Same narrow edge.

He tested a side spur valve. Baseline hum changed when he closed it. The doubled harmonic didn't. It kept running underneath.

He wrote: off-local.

Copper taste arrived on his tongue. He checked his gums with his finger. No blood. The taste stayed anyway. He logged it: metal taste without injury.

A guild chalk board hung near Junction Twelve. Someone had written: keep flow, meet schedule. Jhace looked at it. Kept walking.

Two guild workers argued over a collar shift at Junction Twelve. One stepped into Jhace's path, wrench held across his chest like a boundary marker. Jhace stopped two strides back. Set his kit down. Kept his hands visible.

He read the collar's torque bias by how the seam pulled. The shift trended Downslope. Dangerous territory if it failed.

"Last tightening sequence?" he asked.

The worker with the wrench shrugged. "By feel."

"No torque marks?"

"Didn't need them."

Jhace's shoulders stayed level. His voice stayed flat. "Isolate the collar."

"Not doing that. We're behind schedule."

"Junction Seven's dead. Twenty-three names on that plaque. This collar fails, you add more."

Neither worker moved. One looked at the other. Tools hung quiet in their hands. Then one reached for the valve.

Jhace used the phrase that held weight in the tunnels: "This is the grid."

The valve turned. Baseline hum dipped. The doubled harmonic stayed. Jhace wrote: persistence confirmed.

The tunnel shuddered. Brief. Micro-movement. His lamp flame flicked. A tool cloth crept across the floor. Weight lifted for one heartbeat. Then returned hard. His knees flexed to catch it.

The workers went still. Fear showed in how they stopped talking. Jhace kept his hands steady. Wrote out contingency orders: wedge under collar, keep isolation, send runner to Bram if the hum changes.

He touched the stone wall. Not the pipe. The pulse repeated through solid rock. Foundation pressure. He logged it.

His slate held a map of the tunnel system. He marked a route to Sector Fourteen. Deeper. Where the doubled harmonic seemed strongest.

He walked at controlled pace. The harmonic grew louder. Dust fell in repeating pulses. Whatever was wrong, it came from below.

---

# **Chapter Two: Glass That Listens**

The theodolite base sat uneven. Tiffani scraped grit from under the rear foot until the bubble centered. She checked the other feet. All seated properly now.

She pulled three lens plates from their case. Inspected each edge under angled light. No micro-chips visible. She swapped the installed plate anyway. Eliminated the variable.

Her logbook opened to a fresh page. She wrote the header in order: Tide 0, Pouring. Target: Seven-Three. Instrument: theodolite, calibrated two Tides prior. Air conditions: clear, minimal haze bands. Grid-hum: baseline audible through platform grating.

The sight aligned on Seven-Three. She took the reading. Logged the angle. Checked it against her reference chart.

Offset beyond tolerance.

She reset the instrument. Repeated the measurement. Same offset. She wrote the value twice to confirm she hadn't transposed digits.

Control star read next. She swung the sight to Vela-Nine. Took that reading. Compared it to reference. Stable. Within expected range.

She wrote: instrument not source.

The glass plate bins behind her began a low resonance. Barely audible. She turned and touched the bin rim. Vibration traveled into her fingertips. Steady. Persistent. She logged: pitch change detected in storage bins.

The grid-hum shifted. A whine climbed into the higher register, then dropped to a lower baseline than normal. She felt it in her teeth before she consciously heard it.

Seven-Three stepped again. Discrete increment. Measurable. She drew projection curve segments in the logbook margin. If this rate continued—

She calculated the intersection point. Wrote the result. Underlined it once.

Footsteps on the stairs. Pella appeared at the platform edge, bread wrapped in cloth tucked under one arm.

"Stay back," Tiffani said. Eyes on the sight. "Don't talk."

Pella stopped. Set the bread on the top stair.

Metallic taste arrived on Tiffani's tongue. Copper. Sharp and immediate. She touched her mouth corner with one finger. Checked it. Copper-gold shimmer clung to her fingertip. Not blood. Something else.

She pressed the particulate against the slate edge. It stuck. Didn't brush off cleanly like normal dust. She rubbed her fingers together. The shimmer transferred between them but wouldn't fully release.

She wrote: external forcing. Underlined it hard enough the chalk snapped.

Warning protocol existed for a reason. She pulled the bell cord in the prescribed sequence: three pulls, two-count gap, two pulls. The bell tone rang flat, corrected mid-note, then stabilized wrong.

Apprentices on the grinding floor looked up. The wheel stopped. Everyone listened for the second strike that would mean evacuation.

The structure trembled.

Not ground-shake—that had stopped after the Piercing settled. This came through the platform feet as vibration first, then graduated to visible movement. The theodolite feet scraped against their base. Her logbook page lifted and slapped back down. She pinned it with her palm.

Air pressure arrived before sound. Her ribs vibrated. Breath interrupted mid-inhale. She forced herself to finish the breath. Logged: pressure wall, pre-acoustic.

The roar came after.

A dust column rose beyond the roofline. Visible over the lower buildings. Debris arced outward in widening circles. She tracked the centerline. Estimated point of origin. Marked it on her district map with one chalk stroke.

Gravity changed.

Her boots lost full contact with the platform. Stomach lifted. She counted heartbeats. One. Two. Three. Four.

Gravity returned.

Hard. Her knees buckled to absorb it. Iron taste joined the copper. She touched her gums. Blood. She spat over the platform rail and logged: gum hemorrhage, pressure differential.

Through the settling dust, something became visible. A dark spindle. Vertical. Massive. The boundary around it distorted—bent light, warped edges. She watched it for three full breaths. The distortion moved. Not wind-driven. The object itself was moving.

She wrote: bent-light. Added: object in motion, spin likely.

A scar appeared in the sky above the spindle. Visible in the gaps between dust clouds. Persistent. The dust cleared in places and the scar remained. She noted it as: atmospheric tear, non-closing.

Dust fall began in earnest. Particulate stuck to her eyelashes. Coated her lips. She tasted copper-gold with every breath. Her throat felt lined.

Pella pointed down at the street. "People are—"

"I see them." Tiffani didn't look long. She finished her notation: spindle trajectory suggests planetary exit vector.

The observatory protocols existed for cataloging events, not responding to them. Her training covered measurement, documentation, archival. Not field response.

She closed the logbook. Secured it inside her vest.

"Kit list," she said. "Chalk, cord, spare slate, my plates—the thin set, not the observatory glass—and secure that logbook."

Pella moved immediately. Gathered the items.

"Lift is banned," Tiffani said. "Grid instability makes it a drop risk. We take stairs."

She took one last look at the spindle. Still visible. Still moving. The scar above it stretched from horizon to horizon now. The Seam, she thought. That's what people would call it.

She descended at controlled speed. Pella followed two steps behind. The stairwell vibrated with wrong-pitch hum. Distant collapses punctuated their footfalls.

Whatever had just pierced Saint Merro, it had gone all the way through.

---

# **Chapter Three: The Piercing**

Jhace emerged from Sector Fourteen into full chaos.

The street had broken. Not cracked—broken. A crevasse split the paving stones in a line that ran straight through the intersection. Buildings on either side leaned inward. Glass hung in window frames like loose teeth waiting to fall.

Dust everywhere. Copper-gold particulate thick enough to taste with your mouth closed. It coated his tongue, lined his throat. The grid-hum screamed wrong. Not just off-pitch. Oscillating. Spiking and dropping in irregular intervals that made his jaw ache, made his teeth buzz.

People ran in conflicting directions. Some toward the crevasse. Some away. No coordination. Just panic. Bodies colliding. Voices layering into one continuous roar.

A woman's voice cut through. "Chloen\! CHLOEN\!"

Jhace tracked the sound. A mother stood at the crevasse edge, looking down into it. Calling a name over and over. Her voice breaking on the second syllable.

He moved toward her. Rubble shifted under his boots. He tested each step before committing weight. The street wasn't stable. Nothing was stable.

"How old?" he asked when he reached her.

"Six Turns." The mother didn't look at him. Kept staring down into the crevasse. "She was right here. We were walking and then the street opened and she—" Her voice cut off. Started again. "I can't reach her. I can't—"

"I'll look."

Jhace knelt at the edge. The crevasse dropped maybe two strides, opened into what looked like a collapsed cellar. A timber beam had fallen at an angle. Created a lean-to space underneath.

Chloen sat in that space. Dust-covered. Eyes wide. Tears cut clean tracks through the dust on her cheeks. She made small sounds—half-sobs that hitched in her chest, the kind that come after you've been screaming too long to breathe right.

"Chloen\!" Jhace called down. "Can you move?"

The girl shook her head. Small movement. Afraid to move too much. Afraid the space around her would collapse.

The beam shifted.

Not much. Maybe a finger-width. Dust puffed from the contact point where timber met rubble. The entire structure groaned.

The mother made a sound behind him. Animal. Raw. The sound that says my child is dying and I can't reach them.

Jhace assessed the beam. Old growth timber. Maybe twelve spans long, as thick around as his thigh. Resting on rubble at one end, pinned under a stone lintel at the other. The weight distribution was wrong. The rubble pile looked loose—packed dirt and broken stone, nothing solid. If it shifted even a hand-width, the beam would drop and the space underneath would collapse.

Conventional options: rope descent, brace from below, wedge the fulcrum. All required time he didn't have and materials that weren't here.

The beam shifted again. Visible this time. The rubble settling. Creating cascade.

Chloen screamed. High. Thin. The sound of a child who knows what's about to happen.

Jhace's hands moved without decision. Reached for the beam. Not to grab it—grabbing wouldn't help, timber that size would just slip through his grip. To hold it. To keep it where it was. His palms made contact with the wood.

Heat arrived.

Not external heat. Not forge heat or lamp flame or sun-warmed stone. Internal. Built in his chest like pressure, like his ribs were too small, like something wanted out. It grew fast—not gradual warmth but spike, surge, flood. His chest felt full. Too full. The pressure pushed outward. Demanded release.

It poured down his arms.

Not traveled—*poured*. Like liquid metal in channels. He felt it move through muscle, through bone, through pathways that shouldn't exist. Hot channels where blood should be. His forearms burned from the inside.

It concentrated in his palms.

The heat multiplied. His hands felt like he'd thrust them into forge coals, like skin should be blistering, blackening, peeling away. But they weren't. The heat was there but doing nothing. Just building. Accumulating. Pressure and heat with nowhere to go except—

Into the beam.

Light appeared. Not reflection. Not lamp light catching surface. His hands were producing light. Copper-gold glow that started at the contact points and spread. Threads of it. Moving into the wood grain like cracks, like roots seeking water, like veins filling with blood. The threads branched. Subdivided. Followed the stress lines in the timber.

His vision narrowed.

Not focus—constriction. Tunnel vision. Everything except the contact points went dark. The edges of his sight compressed inward until all he could see was his hands on the beam and the light spreading from them. The rest of the world—the crevasse, the girl, the mother, the chaos—gone. Eliminated. Just hands and wood and impossible light.

Sound distorted. The screaming around him became muffled, distant, like he was underwater. His own heartbeat filled his ears instead. Loud. Fast. Each beat pushed more heat down his arms, more light into the beam.

The beam stopped moving.

Not because physics stopped. Not because rubble stabilized. Because he was holding it. With light. With heat. With something that had no name, no explanation, no right to exist.

*What the fuck is this.*

The thought came sharp and clear through the sensory chaos. His mind trying to categorize the impossible. Trying to fit this into any framework he understood. Forge work didn't do this. Metallurgy didn't do this. Bodies didn't do this.

But his body was doing it anyway.

He felt the weight of the beam. Not through his muscles—those weren't straining. Through the light. Through whatever the copper-gold threads were. They carried the load. Distributed it. Showed him where the timber wanted to break, where stress concentrated, where failure would begin if he released.

The rubble shifted underneath. He felt that too. Felt the compression, the settling, the cascade beginning. The light responded. Adjusted. Held the beam stable against the shifting foundation.

Time stretched wrong. Each heartbeat took forever. Each breath felt like pulling air through cloth. His lungs worked harder than they should for just standing still. Harder than sprinting. Harder than climbing.

His hands shook. Small tremor starting in his fingers. He tried to stop it. Couldn't. The tremor persisted despite his attempts at control. It spread into his palms. Made the contact points unstable.

The light flickered.

Panic spiked. If he lost contact, if the hold failed, the beam would drop and—

He pressed harder. Locked his grip. The light stabilized.

The mother dropped into the crevasse. He registered her movement peripherally. Barely. Most of his awareness was consumed by the hold, by the heat still pouring through him, by the light that shouldn't exist but did.

She grabbed Chloen. Lifted her. Someone above pulled them both up. Hands reached down. Voices shouted. Time compressed and stretched simultaneously.

"She's clear\!" someone yelled.

The words took forever to process. To translate into meaning. To become the signal: *release*.

Jhace let go.

The light cut.

Not fade—*cut*. Instant. Like severing a rope. The heat stopped pouring. The pressure in his chest released so fast his lungs seized. He gasped. Couldn't get air. Couldn't get enough air. His chest felt hollow where the pressure had been.

Cold rushed in to replace the heat. His hands went from forge-hot to ice-cold in the span of one breath. Pins and needles erupted across his palms. Then his fingers. Then his forearms. Not numbness becoming sensation—the opposite. Sensation becoming electric. Wrong. Like nerve endings firing without pattern, without reason.

The tremor in his hands escalated. Not small oscillation anymore. Visible shaking. His fingers twitched. Curled. Uncurled. He tried to clench his fist. The fingers responded slowly. Delayed. Like the signal from brain to hand was traveling through static.

His vision expanded back to normal. The tunnel vision released. The world crashed back in—noise, chaos, screaming, dust. All of it overwhelming after the narrowed focus.

He tried to stand. His legs felt wrong. Hollow. No strength. He made it halfway up and his knees buckled. He caught himself on the crevasse edge. Stayed there. Crouched. Breathing too hard.

His forearms showed faint lines. Not veins. Something underneath the skin. Visible when he turned his arms in the light. Copper-gold traces that followed pathways that shouldn't exist. They pulsed faintly. Faded as he watched but didn't disappear completely.

*What the fuck did I just do.*

No answer came. Just tremor. Just cold. Just the hollow feeling in his chest and the electric wrongness in his hands.

The beam dropped into the crevasse behind him. The rubble pile collapsed. The space where Chloen had been sitting vanished under timber and stone.

He'd held it long enough. Barely. But enough.

His hands wouldn't stop shaking.

---

A building groaned. Close. Too close.

Jhace looked up. The structure across the street had lost its corner support. The doorway frame bulged outward. A family trapped behind it—he could see them through the window. Three people. Two children pressed against their mother's sides.

The frame wouldn't hold. The weight above it was too much. The header beam already bowing. Conventional bracing required timber, wedges, time to—

The frame buckled.

Jhace ran. His legs still felt hollow but he made them work anyway. Reached the doorway as the header beam started to drop. He grabbed a fallen support post from the rubble. Shoved it under the beam. The post sank into loose debris. Not stable. Not enough. The beam kept dropping.

His hands went to the post.

The heat returned.

Faster this time. Not building—*slamming*. Into his chest, down his arms, concentrated in his palms before he had time to process what was happening. The pressure spike made his ribs ache. Made breathing hard.

The light spread into the post. Copper-gold threads racing through the wood. His vision narrowed again. Same tunnel compression. Same sensory exclusion. Just hands and post and beam and impossible light holding impossible weight.

The family ran past him. He registered their movement as shapes in his peripheral awareness. Shadows. Abstract. Not real in the way the post was real, in the way the load was real.

The mother grabbed both children. Pulled them out the door. Their feet crunched on broken glass. Someone else helped them away. Voices calling. Hands reaching.

"Clear\!" A man's voice. Distant through the sensory distortion.

Jhace released.

The light cut again. The heat stopped. The cold returned worse this time. His entire body went cold. Not just his hands. His core. His chest. Like the heat had been the only thing keeping him warm and now it was gone and nothing was left.

He staggered. Caught himself on the doorframe. His hands left copper-gold smears on the wood where he touched it. The light fading but not gone. Residue. Evidence.

The tremor had spread. Not just his hands now. His wrists. His forearms. Visible shaking he couldn't control. He tried to make a fist. His fingers barely responded. Grip strength gone. Diminished to almost nothing.

The vein-lines on his forearms were brighter now. More defined. Still fading but slower. Taking longer to disappear.

His legs gave out. He sat down hard on the rubble. Didn't choose to sit. Just stopped being able to stand.

Breathing took effort. Each inhale required conscious decision. His chest felt tight. Compressed. Like the ribs couldn't expand fully.

*I can't do that again.*

The thought arrived clear and definite. Whatever that was—whatever he'd just done twice—he couldn't do it a third time. His body wouldn't allow it. Would break if he tried.

Someone touched his shoulder.

He jerked away—reflex, wrong person could mean threat in this chaos—then registered the touch wasn't aggressive. Turned his head. Slow. His neck felt stiff.

A woman stood there. Tall. Dust covered like everyone was dust covered. But she moved organized. Deliberate. Held slate and chalk like documentation mattered more than survival. Her eyes tracked his hands. The tremor. The faint vein-lines still visible on his forearms.

Behind her, someone screamed for help. She didn't turn. Just kept watching his hands.

"How many times have you done that?" she asked. Her voice clinical. Analytical. Like she was asking about routine procedure, not witnessing impossible light.

He stared at her. At the slate. At the chalk poised like she was about to take notes on his collapse. "What?"

"The light. The stabilization. How many times?"

"Twice." His voice came out rough. Throat lined with copper-gold dust. "Why are you—"

His hands shook harder. Made the question irrelevant. Made everything irrelevant except the fact that his body wasn't his anymore. Wasn't responding right. Wasn't working.

"Duration of contact?" she asked.

"I don't know." Sharper than he meant. Anger breaking through exhaustion. "I wasn't timing it. I was trying not to let people die."

She wrote anyway. Unbothered by his tone. Chalk moving across slate in quick precise strokes.

"You need to sit down," she said. Still writing. Not looking at him. "Actually, you're already sitting. Good. Stay there."

"Who are you?"

"Tiffani Merrow. Glassworker. Observatory guild." She gestured at the chaos around them with her chalk. At the Seam visible above. At the dust still settling. "Was documenting the stellar anomaly when this happened." She looked at him then. Direct assessment. "You're exhibiting Stage One cost markers. Tremor at rest. Vein-line persistence. Grip strength reduction. You need water. Food if you can hold it. And you need to not use that ability again until we understand what it's costing you."

"I'm not planning to use it again." The words came out before he could stop them. Truth. Definite. "Whatever that was, I'm done."

"No, you're not." She crouched next to him. Eye level now. "Because whatever just happened is going to keep happening. Structures keep failing. People keep getting trapped. And you just demonstrated you can hold things that conventional methods can't hold." She tapped her slate. Hard enough to make the chalk jump. "So we can figure out how to do it without killing you, or you can keep doing it blind until it does kill you. Your choice."

He wanted to argue. Wanted to refuse. Wanted to say this wasn't his responsibility, wasn't his problem, wasn't something he agreed to.

Twenty-three names said otherwise.

Junction Seven. The plaque he read every Tide. The accountability that sat in his chest and didn't leave. The reason he became an inspector in the first place.

If he could hold structures. If he could prevent collapses. If he could stop names from being added to plaques—

"What do you need?" The question came out before he decided to ask it.

She studied him. Measuring something. Making calculation he couldn't see. "Right now? I need you to stay conscious. Drink this." She produced a water flask from somewhere. Handed it to him.

His hands shook too hard to hold it steady. Water spilled when he tried to drink. He got some of it in his mouth anyway. Swallowed. Tasted copper underneath the water. The particulate coating everything including his throat.

"After that," she continued, "we figure out what you just did. Document it. Test it. Build protocol around it so you're not just burning yourself out every time you manifest."

"Manifest?" The word felt wrong. Technical term for something that shouldn't have terminology yet.

"The light. The stabilization." She gestured at his hands. "The ability to hold structures through direct contact. I'm calling it manifestation until we have better terminology."

"It felt like dying."

"I know." She wrote something else on her slate. "The cost markers indicate significant physiological strain. But you're still conscious. Still verbal. Still mobile. That means we're working with survivable parameters." She looked at him again. "Barely survivable. But survivable."

A structure collapsed three streets over. The sound carried. Deep grinding crash followed by secondary failures. People screaming. More dust rising.

Tiffani stood. "Can you walk?"

"Don't know."

"Try."

He tried. Got his feet under him. Stood. Swayed. His legs felt hollow but they held. Barely.

"Good enough," she said. "We're moving to clearer ground. Away from structures that might collapse. You're going to sit. I'm going to document what I observed. Then we're going to establish baseline protocols before anyone asks you to do that again."

"No one asked me the first time."

"I know." She started walking. Didn't check if he was following. Just assumed. "That's the problem. Instinct reaction. No assessment. No preparation. No understanding of cost until after activation." She looked back at him. "That's how people die. We're not doing that."

Jhace followed her. His body moved through habit more than conscious decision. The tremor persisted. The vein-lines faded further but didn't disappear completely. His hands still felt wrong. Electric. Disconnected.

They reached a clear section of street. Stone wall intact. No immediate collapse risk. Tiffani sat. Gestured for him to sit next to her.

He did. His legs grateful for the permission to stop working.

She opened her logbook. Started writing. Fast precise notation. Numbers. Observations. Measurements he didn't understand.

Jhace watched the Seam. The sky held a scar that hadn't been there at Kindling. Stretched from horizon to horizon. Persistent. The spindle was gone but the wound remained.

The grid-hum played at wrong frequency. Oscillating. Making his teeth buzz.

Copper-gold dust coated everything. Visible on every surface. Tasted on every breath.

The world had changed. He'd changed with it.

Tiffani kept writing. Clinical. Methodical. Like documentation could make sense of catastrophe. Like measurement could contain the impossible.

Maybe it could.

Maybe that was the only way forward. System. Protocol. Method built on measurement instead of instinct.

"What's your name?" she asked. Not looking up from her writing.

"Jhace Torrins. Metalworker. Inspector."

She wrote it down. "Jhace. We're going to figure this out. What you can do. What it costs. How to do it without burning you out." She looked at him then. "But I need you to not die while we're figuring it out. Can you do that?"

He looked at his hands. The tremor that wouldn't stop. The vein-lines still faintly visible. The grip strength gone.

"Yeah," he said. "I can do that."

"Good." She went back to writing. "Because if you die, I lose my primary test subject. And I hate wasting data."

Despite everything—the chaos, the fear, the wrongness in his body—Jhace almost smiled. "That's your concern? Wasting data?"

"That's my primary concern, yes." She didn't look up. Kept writing. "Your survival is functionally necessary for continued observation. So don't die."

"I'll try."

"Trying isn't protocol." She turned a page. Started a new section. "Not dying is protocol. We're establishing that now. Rule one: you don't manifest without assessment. Rule two: you don't manifest without spotter present. Rule three: you release on command. No extended holds. No heroic bullshit. Method over heroics. Always."

"I don't know what any of that means yet."

"You will." She kept writing. "We're building it as we go. But the framework starts now. Before you do that again. Before anyone else manifests. Before more names go on more plaques."

Twenty-three names. She was right. If he could prevent that. If they could figure out how to do this without killing people—

"Okay," he said. "We build the framework."

Tiffani nodded once. Kept writing. Her chalk moving across slate. Documenting the end of the old world and the beginning of whatever came next.

Jhace leaned back against the wall and let her work.

His hands still shook. The cost still sat in his body like permanent weight. But he was alive. Chloen was alive. The family was alive.

That had to count for something.

The Seam cut across the sky. The wound that wouldn't close. Evidence that the world had broken and wouldn't unbreak.

But maybe they could hold it together anyway.

One structure at a time. One protocol at a time. One life saved at a time.

Method over heroics. Always.

Tiffani kept writing. Clinical. Systematic. Turning his near-failure into framework for everyone else.

Jhace watched her work. Watched the Seam. Watched evidence that the world had changed and they'd have to change with it.

The grid-hum played wrong. The dust coated everything. Twenty-three names sat in his chest.

But alive. Still alive. Still functional enough to matter.

They'd figure it out. Somehow.

One structure at a time.

---

# **Chapter Four: After the Shudder**

Jhace woke to tremor.

Not ground-shake—that had stopped after the Piercing settled. His hands. Shaking against the work bench where he'd fallen asleep. Small oscillation. Persistent. He tried to stop it by clenching his fists. The fingers curled slowly. Grip strength diminished. The tremor didn't stop.

Stage One, Tiffani had called it. Cost markers. Evidence.

He sat up. His back protested—wrong angle, wrong surface, too many Tides sleeping wherever work stopped instead of finding proper rest. The workshop smelled like cold forge and copper dust. Gray light filtered through the high windows. Kindling phase. Tide 1\.

One Tide since the Piercing. One Tide since his body stopped being entirely his.

He flexed his hands. Watched the tremor. Counted his heartbeats while he watched. Fifteen. Twenty. The shaking persisted at constant amplitude. No improvement.

His forearms showed faint lines underneath the skin. Not the bright vein-lines that appeared during activation. These were permanent. Traces that didn't fade completely. Copper-gold pathways visible when he turned his arms in the light.

Evidence. Persistent evidence.

He stood. Walked to the water barrel. Splashed his face. The cold helped wake him but did nothing for the tremor. His reflection showed in the water's surface—hollow eyes, copper dust in his hair, jaw tight from sleeping with teeth clenched.

Twenty-three names. Plus however many he'd saved during the Piercing. The count should balance. Should feel like progress.

It didn't.

The workshop door opened. Tiffani entered carrying her logbook and a cloth bundle. She assessed him in one look. "You slept here."

"Yeah."

"That's suboptimal. Proper rest requires horizontal surface, adequate duration, and—"

"I know."

She set the bundle on the work bench. Unwrapped it. Bread. Dried fruit. Water flask. "Eat. Your body processed significant strain. Caloric intake is necessary for recovery."

Jhace looked at the food. His stomach twisted at the thought of eating. "Not hungry."

"That's not relevant. Hunger is an unreliable indicator post-activation. Metabolic demand exists regardless of appetite." She pushed the bread toward him. "Eat anyway."

He picked up the bread. Bit off a piece. Chewed. Tasted copper underneath everything. The dust got into food, into water, into lungs. No escaping it.

Tiffani opened her logbook. Wrote the date and phase at the top of a fresh page. "I need to assess your baseline status. Physical examination. Document cost progression."

"Now?"

"Yes, now. Timely documentation is critical for establishing patterns." She looked at him. "Hold out your hands."

He did. The tremor showed immediately. Visible oscillation he couldn't control.

She pulled a thin cord from her kit. Marked it at finger-widths. "Grip this. Maximum force."

Jhace wrapped his fingers around the cord. Squeezed. His grip felt weak. Wrong. Like the signal from brain to hand was traveling through interference.

She measured the compression. Wrote the value. "Thirty percent reduction from baseline. That's significant degradation for one activation sequence."

"Two activations."

"Correct. Two discrete events." She made a note. "Duration of first hold?"

"Don't know. Didn't count."

"Estimate."

Jhace tried to reconstruct the timeline. The beam. The light. The cold that came after. "Twenty heartbeats. Maybe thirty."

"Second hold?"

"Shorter. Ten. Fifteen."

She wrote both estimates. "Total activation time: approximately forty heartbeats across two events. Current cost markers: persistent tremor, thirty percent grip loss, vein-line residue, elevated metabolic demand." She looked up. "This establishes baseline degradation rate. We'll track progression."

"For what?"

"To determine if the cost is cumulative or if recovery occurs between activations." She turned a page. "If cumulative, we need to establish maximum safe frequency. If recovery occurs, we need to measure recovery rate and threshold before permanent damage."

The clinical language should have felt cold. Instead it felt like structure. Framework. Something to hold onto when his body was demonstrating it couldn't be trusted.

"What if I just don't do it again?" he asked.

Tiffani closed her logbook. Met his eyes directly. "That's not a viable option. Structures continue to fail. The Piercing destabilized load-bearing elements throughout Saint Merro. Conventional methods can't hold everything that needs holding." She gestured at his hands. "You demonstrated capacity yesterday. That capacity will be required again."

"I didn't ask for it."

"I know. That's not relevant." She reopened the logbook. "We work with what exists. You manifest. Others will manifest. We document, establish protocol, minimize cost, maximize function. That's the work now."

Jhace wanted to argue. Wanted to refuse. Wanted to say this wasn't fair, wasn't his responsibility, wasn't something he chose.

Twenty-three names said otherwise. Always did.

"What do you need me to do?" he asked.

"Finish eating. Then we test activation under controlled conditions."

His stomach twisted. Cold spread through his chest. "Now?"

"Yes, now. We need to establish whether you can manifest on demand or if threat-stimulus is required." She pulled out her slate and chalk. "If you can activate without imminent collapse, that changes protocol significantly. If you can't, we need to know that limitation immediately."

"What if I can't?"

"Then we document that result and adjust framework accordingly." She stood. "Come outside. We'll use the practice yard. I've already prepared the test structure."

Jhace followed her. His legs felt steady enough but the hollow sensation from the Piercing persisted. Like his body was running on diminished reserves with no clear path to refilling them.

The practice yard held a simple test rig—timber frame, weighted with stones, supported by a single post. Tiffani had marked the ground with chalk lines. Clear boundaries. Measured distances.

"Stand here." She indicated a mark. "Place your hands on the post. Attempt to activate. I'll observe and document."

Jhace moved to the mark. The post felt solid under his palms. Normal wood. Room temperature. No threat. No imminent collapse.

He tried to call the heat. Tried to summon whatever had poured through him during the Piercing. Reached for it mentally, physically, however it was supposed to work—

Nothing.

No heat. No pressure in his chest. No light. Just his hands on wood and copper taste in his mouth.

He tried harder. Focused on the contact points. Remembered the sensation—the building pressure, the flood down his arms, the light spreading into timber.

Still nothing.

"I can't," he said. His voice came out frustrated. Wrong. Like admitting failure.

"Try again," Tiffani said. Writing already. "Focus on the activation sequence. What triggered it during crisis?"

"I don't know. It just happened."

"That's imprecise. Reconstruct the moment. What were you thinking? Feeling? What preceded the activation?"

Jhace closed his eyes. Reconstructed the crevasse. Chloen trapped. The beam shifting. The mother's sound—animal, raw, the sound that says my child is dying.

Fear. Necessity. No time for anything except action.

He opened his eyes. Tried again. Reached for that fear, that necessity. Tried to manufacture the desperation that had triggered the light.

Nothing happened.

The post stayed inert. His hands remained normal. No heat. No light. No manifestation.

"I can't do it," he said. Definitive now. Not failure—fact. "Without the threat, without the—it doesn't work."

Tiffani wrote for ten heartbeats. Then looked up. "That's a critical data point. Manifestation requires imminent threat stimulus. You cannot activate on demand." She underlined something on her slate. "That limits tactical application but provides safety constraint. You won't accidentally activate during routine work."

"That's good?"

"That's information. Information allows protocol development." She gestured at the test rig. "Step back. We're going to test justified activation."

"What does that mean?"

"I'm going to remove the support post. The frame will collapse. You'll attempt to hold it." She met his eyes. "Controlled threat. Measured duration. I'll command release at predetermined threshold. This tests whether threat-stimulus is sufficient and establishes your current capacity post-degradation."

Jhace's hands started shaking harder. Not just rest tremor—new tremor. Fear. "That's—"

"Necessary," Tiffani finished. "We need baseline data under controlled conditions. Otherwise we're operating blind." She moved to the support post. "Are you ready?"

"No."

"That's acceptable. Ready or not, the frame will collapse when I remove this post. You'll hold it or you won't. Either result provides data." Her hand went to the post. "I'm removing support in three. Two. One."

She pulled the post free.

The frame dropped.

Jhace's hands moved automatically. Caught the header beam before conscious decision. Contact made.

The heat arrived. Fast. Not building—slamming into his chest like physical impact. The pressure expanded so fast his ribs ached. His lungs seized. Couldn't get full breath. The heat poured down his arms anyway. Flooded his hands. Concentrated in his palms.

Light spread into the beam. Copper-gold threads racing through wood grain. His vision narrowed. Everything except the contact points going dark. The world reduced to hands and beam and impossible light.

The weight arrived through the light. Not through his muscles. Through whatever the threads were. He felt the load. Felt the stone weights above trying to pull the beam down. Felt himself holding them suspended.

His hands shook. The tremor from rest escalating under activation. Making the contact points unstable. The light flickered.

"Hold," Tiffani's voice. Distant. Muffled like he was underwater.

He held. Counted heartbeats because that's what his mind did under stress. One. Two. Three. Four. Five.

The heat built higher. His chest felt compressed. Breathing harder. Each inhale required conscious effort.

Six. Seven. Eight.

"Release," Tiffani commanded.

Jhace let go.

The light cut. The heat stopped. Cold rushed in. His hands went ice-cold in one breath. The hollow feeling in his chest expanded. Worse than before. Like the activation had carved out more space and left it empty.

The frame dropped. Stones scattered. Timber hit dirt with dull impact.

Jhace staggered. His body argued with him about staying upright. Wanted to collapse completely. He forced it to hold anyway. Caught himself on the yard wall. His legs barely held. The tremor in his hands had escalated. Visible shaking. Worse than rest-state. Much worse.

Tiffani was next to him. When had she moved? "Sit."

He sat. Didn't argue. Couldn't argue. All his energy focused on breathing. On staying conscious. On not collapsing completely.

She pulled his hands toward her. Examined them. The vein-lines showed bright. Copper-gold traces clear under his skin. Pulsing faintly. Taking longer to fade than after the Piercing.

"Duration: eight heartbeats," she said. Writing with one hand while holding his wrist with the other. "Cost escalation: tremor amplitude increased, vein-line brightness elevated, recovery delay extended." She checked his pupils. "Conscious. Verbal. Mobile." She released his wrist. "You're functional."

"Don't feel functional."

"Feelings are unreliable indicators. Your body is processing strain but maintaining core systems. That's functional." She wrote more notes. "The data shows threat-stimulus is sufficient trigger. Controlled activation is possible. Cost is cumulative—second activation showed faster onset and stronger symptoms despite shorter duration."

Jhace's breathing started to normalize. The cold in his chest remained but the immediate panic passed. "How many times can I do that?"

"Unknown. We'll establish threshold through progressive testing." She looked at him. "Not today. You need recovery time. Food. Water. Rest. Then we repeat assessment and measure recovery rate."

"And then?"

"Then we build protocol that keeps you alive while using what you can do." She closed her logbook. "Method over heroics. Always."

The yard gate opened. A figure entered. Young. Maybe twenty Turns. Thin build. Work clothes covered in plaster dust. He moved hesitant. Uncertain.

His hands shook. Visible tremor. Same pattern as Jhace's.

Copper-gold traces showed on his forearms. Faint but present. Vein-lines that didn't fade completely.

The young man stopped three strides from them. His eyes went to Jhace's hands. To the tremor. To the vein-lines. Then to his own hands showing the same markers. His expression shifted—understanding, then relief. Not alone.

"You too?" he asked. His voice quiet. Almost afraid.

Jhace looked at his own hands. At the tremor he couldn't stop. At the evidence of cost he'd carry now. Then at this stranger who showed the same evidence.

"Yeah," Jhace said. "Me too."

Tiffani stood. Opened her logbook to a fresh page. "Name?"

"Kylar," the young man said. Still looking at Jhace. "Kylar Vess. I'm a mason. Was a mason. Before—" He gestured at his hands. "During the Piercing, I held a wall. Kept it from falling on people. Afterwards, my hands started shaking and they haven't stopped."

"When did you manifest?" Tiffani asked. Writing already.

"During the Piercing. Shaping phase. The wall was collapsing and I just—I put my hands on it and it stopped. Light came from my hands. Copper-gold. Like his." Kylar looked at Tiffani. "What is this? What's happening to us?"

"Unknown," Tiffani said. Clinical. Direct. "The Piercing triggered manifestation capacity in multiple individuals. You're the second confirmed case. There will be more." She gestured at the yard. "Sit. I need to document your activation event and assess your current status."

Kylar sat on the yard wall next to Jhace. His hands shook with the same persistent tremor. His eyes held the same hollowness. The same evidence that his body had done something impossible and was still paying the price.

"Does it stop?" Kylar asked. Quiet. Directed at Jhace, not Tiffani. "The shaking. Does it go away?"

Jhace looked at his own hands. One Tide since activation. The tremor unchanged. "Don't know yet."

"I'm scared," Kylar said. Simple admission. No shame. "I don't know what I did. Don't know how to control it. Don't know if I'll do it again or if it'll kill me next time."

"That's why we're here," Tiffani said. Not unkind. Just factual. "To document. To establish limits. To build protocol that prevents death." She looked between them. "You both manifested during crisis. You both show cost markers. You both survived." She wrote something. Underlined it. "That means survival is possible. Protocol makes it probable."

Jhace watched Kylar. Saw his own fear reflected. His own uncertainty. His own desperate need for framework that made sense of impossible things.

"We're figuring it out," Jhace said. Gestured at Tiffani and her logbook. "She's tracking everything. Building protocol. Measuring limits so we don't die finding them." He looked at Kylar directly. "You're not alone. And you're not dying blind."

Kylar's shoulders dropped. Relief visible in how his posture changed. "Thank you. I—thank you."

Tiffani pulled out more slate and chalk. Handed them to Kylar. "I need you to draw the wall you held. Approximate dimensions. Estimate load. Describe the activation sequence in as much detail as you can remember." She looked at Jhace. "You rest. Drink water. Eat if you can. We'll reconvene at Pouring phase."

Jhace stood. His legs held better now. The hollow feeling persisted but didn't disable. He walked to the workshop door. Stopped. Looked back.

Kylar was drawing on slate. His hands shook but he drew anyway. Tiffani watched. Wrote notes. Built framework from chaos.

Protocol emerging from necessity. Method discovered through failure. Partnership becoming functional unit.

They were figuring it out. One test at a time. One limitation learned. One person saved from dying alone without understanding what their body was doing.

Jhace entered the workshop. Sat at the bench. Picked up the bread Tiffani had brought. Forced himself to eat.

His hands shook. The cost remained. The evidence persisted.

But he wasn't alone. Kylar wasn't alone. Others would come. Others were already coming.

And when they did, there would be protocol. There would be method. There would be a way forward that didn't require dying to figure out limits.

Method over heroics. Always.

He finished the bread. Drank water. Rested his hands on the bench and watched them shake.

One Tide since the Piercing. One Tide since the world broke.

They'd hold it together anyway. Somehow.

One structure at a time.

# **Chapter Five: Where to Press**

Tiffani's logbook held three pages of data. Insufficient for projection but sufficient for hypothesis.

She sat at the observatory's lower work table. The platform above remained closed—dust accumulation made the glass plates unusable, and she had no time for cleaning when the work that mattered was here. Documentation. Pattern recognition. Framework construction.

Jhace's baseline measurements occupied the first page. Grip strength reduction: thirty percent. Tremor frequency: consistent at rest. Vein-line persistence: measurable at twenty-three heartbeats post-release. Recovery rate: unknown, required longitudinal observation.

Kylar's data filled the second page. Similar markers but different magnitudes. Grip strength: forty-five percent reduction. Tremor frequency: higher amplitude. Vein-line persistence: thirty-seven heartbeats. His activation had lasted longer—approximately fifty heartbeats by his estimate, though estimates were unreliable and she needed to witness an activation herself to establish accurate measurement.

The third page held her projections. If cost accumulated linearly, Jhace could sustain approximately twelve activations before permanent Stage Two symptoms appeared. If cost accumulated exponentially, that number dropped to six. If recovery occurred between activations, the sustainable frequency depended on recovery rate, which remained unmeasured.

Three variables. Insufficient data. Potentially fatal margins of error.

She wrote: *Urgent requirement: establish recovery rate and cost accumulation pattern. Method: serial testing with measured intervals. Risk: accelerated degradation if hypothesis incorrect.*

The workshop door opened. Jhace entered carrying water flasks. Set them on the table. "Bram says you haven't eaten since Kindling."

"That's inaccurate. I consumed bread at—" She stopped. Checked her internal timeline. Kindling phase had been how many heartbeats ago? She'd lost count somewhere between documenting Kylar's wall dimensions and calculating load distribution patterns.

"When?" Jhace asked.

"Irrelevant. Current priority is cost curve projection." She gestured at the logbook. "Sit. I need to review findings."

He sat. Picked up one of the water flasks. Drank. His hands shook enough that water spilled. He wiped it without comment.

Tiffani watched the tremor. Measured the amplitude visually. Approximately the same as Kindling assessment. No improvement. No degradation. Stable cost state after—she counted backward—roughly eight Tides since last activation.

"Your tremor amplitude is unchanged," she said.

"I know."

"That suggests cost stabilization. Persistent damage but not progressive degradation in absence of activation." She made a note. "That's a critical data point. Manifestation causes acute cost spike followed by plateau. The plateau persists but doesn't worsen without additional activation."

"Is that good?"

"It's information. Good is a value judgment. Information allows decision-making." She turned to a fresh page. Drew a graph—time on horizontal axis, cost on vertical. Marked Jhace's data points. "Linear accumulation model predicts twelve activations to Stage Two threshold. Exponential accumulation predicts six. Current data insufficient to determine which model is accurate."

Jhace studied the graph. His finger traced the projection lines. "What's Stage Two?"

"Seizure. Loss of consciousness. Respiratory compromise. Potentially fatal." She wrote the symptoms below the graph. "Stage One is tremor, grip loss, vein-line persistence. Stage Two introduces neurological compromise. Stage Three is organ failure. Stage Four is death."

His hand stopped moving. Stayed pressed against the page where her Stage Four notation sat.

"How many people make it past Stage Two?" he asked. His voice flat. Clinical. Matching her tone.

"Unknown. Sample size is two. Neither has reached Stage Two. Projections are theoretical." She met his eyes. "That's why we need protocol. To prevent progression. To establish safe operational parameters before someone dies testing limits blind."

Jhace pulled his hand back. The tremor visible. Persistent. Evidence of cost he already carried. "What parameters?"

"Duration limits. Recovery intervals. Spotter requirements. Release command authority." She pulled out a fresh slate. Started writing the framework. "Rule one: no activation without spotter present. Spotter monitors duration, issues release command at predetermined threshold. Operator obeys release command immediately. No extensions. No heroic holds."

"What if the structure still needs holding?"

"Then conventional methods take over. Wedges, posts, cord, whatever materials are available." She kept writing. "Rule two: maximum activation duration scales with operator's current cost state. Fresh operator: twenty heartbeats maximum. Stage One operator: ten heartbeats maximum. Stage Two: no activation permitted."

"That's not enough time."

"It's the time that keeps you functional for the next crisis." She underlined the rule. "Dead operators save no one. Burned-out operators save no one. Functional operators save people across multiple events. Method over heroics. Always."

Jhace was quiet. Stared at the slate. At the rules she was writing. At the framework that constrained what his body could do.

"What if I disagree?" he asked.

"With what specifically?"

"With your assessment. With your duration limits. With—" He gestured at the slate. At the rules. "With all of it."

Tiffani set down her chalk. Looked at him directly. "Then you operate without protocol and we document your degradation until you hit Stage Two, at which point you're no longer functional and someone else has to figure this out starting from zero data." She picked up the chalk again. "Or you work within framework that's designed to keep you alive and we refine the parameters as data accumulates. Your choice."

The workshop went quiet. Copper taste present on every breath. Grid-hum oscillating through wrong frequency. His hands shaking with persistent tremor.

"I don't want to die," he said. Simple statement. No performance.

"I know. That's why I'm building this." She tapped the slate. "Protocol isn't limitation. It's method. It's how we use what you can do without burning you out in the process."

He nodded once. Slow. Accepting necessity even when it constrained him.

Tiffani returned to writing. "Rule three: mandatory recovery interval between activations. Minimum eight Tides for Stage One operators. We'll adjust based on measured recovery data."

"Eight Tides? Structures won't wait eight Tides."

"Then other operators rotate in. That's why we're documenting everyone who manifests. Building a network. Distributing load across multiple people instead of burning out individuals." She wrote faster. The framework clarifying as she articulated it. "Rule four: cost assessment required before each activation. If tremor amplitude exceeds baseline by measurable margin, no activation permitted until recovery occurs."

"Who measures?"

"Spotter. Which means spotters need training. Need to recognize cost markers. Need authority to refuse activation if operator is compromised." She looked at him. "Can you refuse yourself?"

"What?"

"If your body is telling you you're compromised, can you refuse to activate even when people need help?" She waited. "Because if you can't, someone else has to refuse for you. That's what spotter authority means."

Jhace's jaw tightened. He looked at his hands. At the tremor. At the evidence that his body wasn't entirely under his control anymore.

"No," he said. Quiet. Honest. "If someone's dying, I can't refuse."

"I know. That's why spotter authority supersedes operator decision-making. When I say release, you release. When I say no activation, there's no activation. Even if you disagree. Even if someone's screaming." She held his eyes. "That's the protocol. That's how you stay alive."

He didn't argue. Didn't fight it. Just nodded once and looked away.

Tiffani wrote: *Spotter authority: absolute. Operator compliance: non-negotiable. Violation of spotter command results in immediate removal from rotation.*

Harsh. Necessary. The only way to prevent operators from killing themselves trying to save everyone.

The door opened again. Kylar entered. Behind him, a woman—maybe thirty-five Turns, broader build, work clothes stained with dye. Her hands showed the same tremor. Same vein-line traces.

"Found another one," Kylar said. Gestured at the woman. "This is Merra. She held a wall in the dye quarter. Same symptoms."

Tiffani opened her logbook to a fresh page. "Sit. Both of you. I need to document your activation events and establish baseline measurements."

Merra sat. Held out her hands without being asked. The tremor showed clearly. "How many of us are there?"

"Unknown. You're the third confirmed case. Projections suggest dozens, potentially hundreds across Saint Merro." Tiffani pulled out her measurement cord. "Grip this. Maximum force."

Merra gripped. The cord compressed. Tiffani measured. Wrote the value. Fifty-two percent reduction. Worse than Jhace, worse than Kylar.

"When did you manifest?" Tiffani asked.

"During the Piercing. Striking phase. Wall was collapsing on a group of workers. I put my hands on it and—light came. Copper-gold. The wall stopped falling." Merra's voice stayed level but her hands shook harder. "Afterwards I couldn't stop shaking. Couldn't work. Guild sent me home. Said I was compromised."

"You are compromised," Tiffani said. Clinical. Direct. "But not permanently. The damage is stable in absence of additional activation. We're establishing protocol to prevent progression."

"Can I still manifest?"

"Unknown. We'd need to test under controlled conditions. But your grip strength is severely reduced. Any activation would carry high risk of further degradation." Tiffani made notes. Calculated. "Based on your current cost state, I'd classify you as borderline Stage Two. No activation should be permitted until recovery occurs."

"How long until recovery?"

"Unknown. We're measuring that now." Tiffani gestured at Jhace. "He's eight Tides post-activation with stable cost. We'll continue monitoring to establish recovery timeline."

Merra looked at Jhace. At his shaking hands. At the evidence they shared. "Does it stop? Ever?"

"Don't know yet," Jhace said. Same answer he'd given Kylar.

The door opened a fourth time. Bram entered carrying food—bread, dried meat, fruit. Set it on the table. Looked at the three people sitting with shaking hands and copper-gold traces on their forearms.

"How many?" he asked Tiffani.

"Three confirmed. More coming." She gestured at the food. "That's for everyone. Eat. Metabolic demand post-manifestation requires increased caloric intake."

Bram distributed food. Merra and Kylar ate without argument. Jhace picked at the bread. Tiffani noted his reduced appetite—potential cost marker, required monitoring.

"Listen," Bram said. Pulled up a chair. "The Crown's sending someone. Inspector from the Reconstruction and Relief Office. Wants to talk to people who manifested. Document what happened. Establish... something. They used a lot of official words I didn't track."

Tiffani looked up from her notes. "When?"

"Tomorrow. Pouring phase. They want to meet here." Bram glanced at the three operators. "Thought you should know."

"Noted." Tiffani returned to writing. The framework expanding. "We'll provide documentation. Measured data. Established protocol. No speculation. Facts only."

"They're going to want to control this," Jhace said. "Put rules around it. Take authority."

"Let them try." Tiffani kept writing. "We have the data. We have the operators. We have methodology they don't possess. They can make demands. We'll evaluate those demands against operational requirements and accept or refuse based on evidence."

"That's not how Crown authority works."

"That's how this works." She looked at him. "You don't manifest because someone with a seal tells you to. You manifest because someone's dying and conventional methods failed. That means operator decision-making supersedes external authority. They can document. They can observe. They can coordinate resources. But they don't command activation. We do."

Bram whistled low. "You're going to tell a Crown inspector that?"

"If necessary, yes." She turned back to her logbook. "This isn't about authority. It's about keeping people alive. Both the people who need help and the people doing the helping. Protocol serves that goal. External authority that conflicts with protocol gets refused."

Jhace almost smiled. Small movement at his mouth corner. "You're going to make enemies."

"I'm going to build functional methodology. If that creates opposition, we'll manage opposition." She wrote the final protocol rule. "Rule five: operator welfare takes precedence over external pressure. No activation occurs because someone demands it. Activation occurs when assessment justifies it and protocol permits it. End of framework."

She set down her chalk. Looked at the three operators sitting at the table. Jhace with one Tide of data. Kylar with fresh manifestation. Merra with borderline Stage Two symptoms. Three people whose bodies did impossible things and paid impossible prices.

"Tomorrow someone official will arrive with questions," she said. "We'll answer with data. With methodology. With evidence-based framework. Not speculation. Not heroic narratives. Facts and protocol." She closed her logbook. "Until then, you rest. You eat. You drink water. You do not activate unless imminent collapse creates justified threat and I authorize it. Understood?"

Three nods. Quiet acceptance. They were scared. They were compromised. They were desperate for structure that made sense of what their bodies were doing.

She'd give them that structure. One rule at a time. One measurement at a time. One life saved without burning out the people doing the saving.

Method over heroics. Always.

---

# **Chapter Six: The Arch Under Strain**

Jhace stood in front of the arch and knew it was dying.

Not collapse-imminent. Not yet. But the stone showed stress patterns—hairline cracks radiating from the keystone, slight displacement in the supporting columns, mortar dust accumulating at the base. The load distribution had shifted. The arch wanted to fail. Give it time and it would.

The problem was the building above it. Three stories. Residential. Maybe forty people living in the structure. Evacuation would take Tides. Conventional bracing would require timber they didn't have and time they couldn't afford.

Tiffani stood next to him with her slate. Measuring sight-lines. Calculating angles. "The keystone has displaced three finger-widths east. Column base shows lateral movement. Load concentration is migrating to the western support." She wrote numbers. Drew force vectors. "Conventional stabilization would require—"

"Timber we don't have," Jhace finished. "And time we don't have."

"Correct." She lowered the slate. "Assessment: activation justified. But duration will be extended. This isn't a rescue hold. This is stabilization until conventional methods can be implemented. Estimate: one hundred heartbeats minimum."

Jhace felt his hands shake harder. One hundred heartbeats. His longest hold had been eight.

"That's too long," he said.

"I know. But partial stabilization is insufficient. If you activate and release before the arch is properly braced, it fails immediately. People die anyway and you've burned activation capacity for nothing." She met his eyes. "This is why we established protocol. Extended hold requires assessment and authorization. I'm assessing. You need to tell me if you can do this."

Could he? His body argued both sides. The tremor said no. The hollow feeling in his chest said no. The memory of cold after release said no.

Twenty-three names said yes anyway.

"I can do it," he said.

"That's not what I asked. I asked if you can do it safely. If you can hold for one hundred heartbeats and remain functional afterward." She didn't look away. "Because if you burn out, you're done. No more activations. No more holds. And everyone else who needs you gets nothing."

Jhace looked at the arch. At the cracks spreading from the keystone. At the building above holding forty people who didn't know their home was dying underneath them.

"I don't know," he said. Honest. "Never held that long. Don't know what happens at one hundred heartbeats."

"Then we're testing limits in real conditions. Non-optimal but necessary." She pulled chalk from her kit. Marked boundaries on the ground. "You stand here. I stand here. Spotter distance: three strides. You place hands on keystone. I count heartbeats aloud. At one hundred, I command release. You release immediately regardless of arch status."

"What if it's not stable yet?"

"Then we implement emergency evacuation and accept the building may fail." She held his eyes. "But you release. No extensions. No heroic holds. Protocol supersedes structure. Understood?"

He wanted to argue. Wanted to say the building mattered more than protocol. Wanted to refuse the constraint.

But he'd agreed to this. Agreed that Tiffani's assessment superseded his instinct. Agreed that spotter authority was absolute.

"Understood," he said.

She nodded once. Drew more chalk lines. Boundary markers. Safe zones. Collapse patterns if the activation failed. She was thinking through catastrophe even while authorizing the attempt. Planning for failure while hoping for success.

"Bram," she called.

Bram emerged from where he'd been coordinating with residents. "Yeah?"

"Evacuation plan established?"

"Residents are gathering essentials. First groups ready to move on your command."

"Good. Initiate gradual evacuation. Priority: top floor first. If the arch fails, upper collapse has longest fall distance." She looked back at Jhace. "That gives us buffer. Fewer people in the building when you activate."

Jhace moved to his mark. The arch keystone sat maybe two strides above ground level. Accessible. He'd need to place his hands and reach up slightly. Sustainable position for extended hold.

He hoped. He didn't actually know if the position mattered. If body mechanics affected manifestation or if it was all the same once the light started.

"Ready?" Tiffani asked. Chalk poised over her slate. Timer marked in twenty-heartbeat intervals.

"No."

"Acceptable. Activate anyway."

Jhace reached up. Placed his palms against the keystone. The stone felt cool. Solid. The cracks visible underneath his hands.

He waited for the heat.

Nothing.

Right. Threat stimulus required. He couldn't activate on demand. Needed to create the threat or wait for it to manifest naturally.

"The arch is compromised," Tiffani said. Clinical. Factual. "Residents are evacuating. If it fails during evacuation, people die. You're preventing that failure. That's the threat. That's the necessity."

The heat arrived.

Slower than in crisis. Building instead of slamming. Rising in his chest like water filling a container. The pressure accumulated. His ribs expanded. His lungs worked harder. The heat poured down his arms. Concentrated in his hands.

Light spread into the keystone. Copper-gold threads racing into the stone. Different from wood—stone took the light differently. The threads moved slower. Branched less. Followed the crystalline structure instead of organic grain.

His vision narrowed. The world compressed to hands and keystone and light.

"Counting," Tiffani's voice. "One."

The weight arrived. Massive. The entire arch's load concentrating in the keystone. He felt it through the light. Felt the western column trying to shift. Felt the eastern support taking too much load. Felt the stone wanting to crack further, wanting to split, wanting to fail.

The light responded. Threads thickened where stress concentrated. Reinforced the weak points. Held the load distribution stable.

"Twenty."

His hands shook. The tremor escalating. Making the contact unstable. He pressed harder. Locked his palms against the stone. The light flickered. Stabilized.

"Forty."

Breathing got harder. Each inhale required conscious effort. His chest felt compressed. The pressure that created the light was building in his lungs. Nowhere to release it except into the stone.

"Sixty."

Cold started in his fingertips. Not the cold that came after release—this was different. Cold and heat present simultaneously. His hands burning and freezing. Sensation distorting. He couldn't tell which was real.

"Eighty."

His vision compressed further. Just keystone now. Just light. Everything else—Tiffani, Bram, the building, the world—gone. Eliminated. Not present in the narrowed reality of manifestation.

Sound distorted. Tiffani's counting became muffled. Distant. Like listening through water. Like listening through stone. His heartbeat filled his ears instead. Loud. Fast. Irregular.

"Ninety."

His body argued about staying upright. Legs shaking. Not just hands anymore. Tremor spreading. Core instability. He locked his knees. Pressed against the keystone. Used the arch to hold himself up while he held the arch.

"Ninety-five."

Pain spiked behind his eyes. Sharp. Immediate. Headache arriving with pressure that felt like his skull was too small. Like his brain was expanding against bone. Like something needed to crack to release the strain.

"One hundred. Release. Now."

Jhace tried to let go.

His hands wouldn't move.

Not defiance—inability. The light had fused him to the stone somehow. Or his body had forgotten how to release. Or the command wasn't reaching his muscles through the distortion and pain.

"Jhace. Release." Tiffani's voice. Still distant. Still muffled.

He tried again. Focused on his hands. On letting go. On pulling away from the keystone.

Nothing. His palms stayed locked. The light kept running. The heat kept building.

"One hundred ten. Release command not obeyed. Jhace—you need to let go."

He knew. He was trying. His body wasn't listening. The manifestation had its own momentum now. Own logic. It didn't stop just because he wanted it to stop.

"One hundred twenty."

Panic spiked. Cold fear cutting through the heat. If he couldn't release—if this just kept going—

His vision went dark at the edges. Not compression. Actual darkness. Like someone was turning off lights one by one. The center stayed—keystone, light, hands—but everything else went black.

"Jhace\!" Different voice. Bram. Closer. "Tiffani, something's wrong—"

"I know. Jhace, listen to me. You need to release. Force it. Pull your hands away. Do it now."

He tried. Put everything into it. Every remaining thread of control. Pulled.

His hands came free.

The light cut. Instant. Total. Like severing connection.

Jhace collapsed.

Hands caught him. Bram. Lowering him to the ground instead of letting him drop. Voices around him. Tiffani saying something clinical and urgent. Bram responding. The world spinning even though he wasn't moving.

His hands shook so hard it hurt. Full-body tremor. Not just hands—arms, legs, core. Everything shaking. Uncontrollable. His teeth chattering. Body temperature wrong. Can't regulate. Can't stop shaking.

"Get him flat. Head elevated. Check pupils."

Tiffani's hands on his face. Holding his head. Light in his eyes—when had she gotten a lamp? Pupils contracting. Responding. Good sign or bad? He couldn't tell.

"Responsive. Conscious. Elevated tremor. Vein-lines—" She stopped. Pulled up his sleeve.

The vein-lines glowed. Not fading. Glowing bright copper-gold along his forearms. Pulsing with his heartbeat. Visible even in daylight.

"That's new," Bram said. Voice tight.

"Documented." Tiffani was writing somehow. One hand holding Jhace steady, other hand moving chalk across slate. "Duration: one hundred twenty-three heartbeats. Release command delayed by twenty-three heartbeats. Cost markers: full-body tremor, elevated vein-line luminescence, possible Stage Two threshold."

Jhace tried to speak. His throat didn't work right. Sound came out wrong. Slurred.

"Don't talk. Just breathe. Focus on breathing." Tiffani's hand steady on his shoulder. "Bram, get water. And send someone to check the arch."

"The arch held?"

"Check anyway. If it failed after release, we need to know immediately."

Footsteps running. Jhace focused on breathing. In. Out. In. His lungs worked. That was good. Heart still beating. Also good. Everything else felt wrong but those core systems functioned.

"Jhace." Tiffani's voice close. Clinical but not cold. "Can you hear me?"

He nodded. Small movement. Managed that much.

"You held too long. Twenty-three heartbeats past command. That's protocol violation." She wasn't angry. Just stating fact. "But you're conscious. You're breathing. You didn't seize. That means you're still functional." She adjusted her grip on his shoulder. "The arch held. Residents are evacuating safely. You did what needed doing."

The praise felt hollow. He'd violated protocol. Lost control. Couldn't release when commanded. That was failure by the rules she'd established.

"I couldn't—" His voice came out rough. Damaged. "Couldn't let go."

"I know. That's a critical data point. Extended activation reduces voluntary control. The manifestation develops autonomy." She wrote more. "That's Stage Two threshold. Loss of release control. You're borderline now." She met his eyes. "No more activation until recovery. Minimum one Anneal. Maybe two."

One Anneal. Seven Tides. Possibly fourteen.

"What if—"

"No. Non-negotiable." She was absolute. No flexibility. "You hit threshold. Any additional activation before recovery risks seizure. Seizure risks death. You're benched."

Jhace wanted to argue. Wanted to say people needed help. Structures needed holding. He couldn't just stop.

But his body was making Tiffani's argument for her. The tremor wouldn't stop. The vein-lines still glowed. His throat barely worked. He was compromised. Anyone could see that.

Bram returned with water. Tiffani helped Jhace sit up enough to drink. The water spilled. His hands shook too hard to hold the flask. Bram had to help.

Evidence. Persistent evidence that he'd pushed too far.

"The arch held," Bram reported. "Cracks are stable. Residents are out. We can implement conventional bracing now without time pressure."

"Good." Tiffani stood. Looked at Jhace. "You rest here. Don't try to stand. Don't try to work. Just recover." She gestured at Bram. "Stay with him. If tremor worsens or if he loses consciousness, send someone immediately."

"Where are you going?" Bram asked.

"To document this with the other operators. They need to know what Stage Two threshold looks like. They need to know that release control can fail. They need to understand the limit before they hit it themselves." She picked up her logbook. "This isn't just his data. This is methodology."

She walked away. Clinical. Systematic. Turning his near-failure into framework for everyone else.

Jhace leaned against the wall. His body still shaking. The vein-lines still glowing faintly. The hollow feeling in his chest worse than ever.

He'd held the arch. Saved forty people. Violated protocol doing it.

Method over heroics. Always.

He'd failed the method even while succeeding at the heroics.

Tiffani was right. He needed to bench. Needed to recover. Needed to let other operators carry the load while his body remembered how to function properly.

Twenty-three names. Plus forty more who got to live because he'd held past threshold.

The count balanced differently now. Not clean. Not simple. Just complicated math of survival where the right answer still cost more than you wanted to pay.

Bram sat next to him. Didn't talk. Just present. Just there.

Jhace closed his eyes. Listened to his heartbeat slow. Felt the tremor persist. Tasted copper on every breath.

One Anneal minimum. Seven Tides of watching others do what he couldn't. Seven Tides of being benched while his body recovered from doing what needed doing.

The arch stood. The residents lived. He'd failed protocol and succeeded anyway.

That should have felt better than it did.

---

# **Chapter Seven: Method**

They watched the Lowland Plain disappear.

Jhace stood on the Crown wall observation point with his hands pressed against the stone parapet. His tremor visible. Persistent. The vein-lines on his forearms had stopped glowing two Tides ago but the traces remained. Evidence that didn't fade.

Tiffani stood next to him with her distance lens. Measuring. Always measuring. Even when measurement was just documentation of catastrophe she couldn't affect.

The Lowland Plain sat maybe ten Leagues south. Visible on clear Tides as a dark strip between Saint Merro's elevation and the coast. Farmland mostly. Small settlements. Maybe three thousand people total across the scattered villages.

The water had been rising since Kindling.

Not surge. Not wave. Just rising. Like the ocean had decided the boundary between land and sea was negotiable and was renegotiating incrementally. The water climbed. Slow. Relentless. Visible through Tiffani's lens as a dark line advancing across fields.

"The eastern settlement's gone," Tiffani said. Her voice clinical. Factual. But something underneath. Something she wasn't naming. "Water level reached the buildings at Pouring phase. By Shaping, full submersion."

Jhace counted the settlements he could see. Four visible yesterday. Three now. The eastern one just... absent. Buildings underwater. People evacuated or drowned. He didn't know which. Distance prevented knowing.

"How fast?" he asked. His voice still rough. Still damaged from the extended hold three Tides ago. Maybe permanent. He didn't know that either.

"Approximately one stride per Tide." Tiffani lowered the lens. Marked something on her slate. "At current rate, the central settlement submerges by tomorrow. The western settlements by the Tide after."

"They're evacuating?"

"Unknown. Distance prevents assessment." She raised the lens again. "But roads are visible. No significant movement. Either evacuation completed already or population isn't moving."

Jh
