[L1] # **Chapter One: Something Wrong**
[L2] 
[L3] Junction Seven. The plaque held twenty-three names. Jhace read each one in order. Harren Corl. Bessa Tyne. Mikael Stradd. His jaw stayed tight. No ceremony. Just the reading. Just the accountability that sits in your chest and doesn't leave.
[L4] 
[L5] He pulled his inspector kit from his pack. Slate, chalk, cord, collar keys, wedge slips, lamp oil—each item confirmed by touch, fingers moving through the sequence without looking. Everything present.
[L6] 
[L7] He logged the time on his slate: Tide 0, Kindling. Wrote below it: start of route.
[L8] 
[L9] The grid-hum ran through everything in Saint Merro—pipes, walls, floors. Constant low vibration. Background noise you learned not to hear. Jhace pressed his palm to the main pipe at Junction Seven. The hum arrived through his skin. Normal baseline present. But something else rode with it. A second pitch. Doubled harmonic.
[L10] 
[L11] He shifted his hand. Different contact point, different pressure. The doubled harmonic stayed.
[L12] 
[L13] He leaned in. Put his ear to the metal. Cheek cooled fast against the pipe. The second pitch carried a narrow edge. He felt it in his teeth.
[L14] 
[L15] Dust fell from a ceiling seam. Thin strip, maybe a finger-width across. Jhace watched it fall through his lamp light. Counted his breaths. Three. Four. Five. The dust fell again. Same pattern.
[L16] 
[L17] He wrote on his slate: repeat interval.
[L18] 
[L19] The collar seam showed no active steam. Sealant bead intact. No obvious leak. He moved to Junction Nine. Pressed his palm to the pipe there. Same doubled harmonic. Same narrow edge.
[L20] 
[L21] He tested a side spur valve. Baseline hum changed when he closed it. The doubled harmonic didn't. It kept running underneath.
[L22] 
[L23] He wrote: off-local.
[L24] 
[L25] Copper taste arrived on his tongue. He checked his gums with his finger. No blood. The taste stayed anyway. He logged it: metal taste without injury.
[L26] 
[L27] A guild chalk board hung near Junction Twelve. Someone had written: keep flow, meet schedule. Jhace looked at it. Kept walking.
[L28] 
[L29] Two guild workers argued over a collar shift at Junction Twelve. One stepped into Jhace's path, wrench held across his chest like a boundary marker. Jhace stopped two strides back. Set his kit down. Kept his hands visible.
[L30] 
[L31] He read the collar's torque bias by how the seam pulled. The shift trended Downslope. Dangerous territory if it failed.
[L32] 
[L33] "Last tightening sequence?" he asked.
[L34] 
[L35] The worker with the wrench shrugged. "By feel."
[L36] 
[L37] "No torque marks?"
[L38] 
[L39] "Didn't need them."
[L40] 
[L41] Jhace's shoulders stayed level. His voice stayed flat. "Isolate the collar."
[L42] 
[L43] "Not doing that. We're behind schedule."
[L44] 
[L45] "Junction Seven's dead. Twenty-three names on that plaque. This collar fails, you add more."
[L46] 
[L47] Neither worker moved. One looked at the other. Tools hung quiet in their hands. Then one reached for the valve.
[L48] 
[L49] Jhace used the phrase that held weight in the tunnels: "This is the grid."
[L50] 
[L51] The valve turned. Baseline hum dipped. The doubled harmonic stayed. Jhace wrote: persistence confirmed.
[L52] 
[L53] The tunnel shuddered. Brief. Micro-movement. His lamp flame flicked. A tool cloth crept across the floor. Weight lifted for one heartbeat. Then returned hard. His knees flexed to catch it.
[L54] 
[L55] The workers went still. Fear showed in how they stopped talking. Jhace kept his hands steady. Wrote out contingency orders: wedge under collar, keep isolation, send runner to Bram if the hum changes.
[L56] 
[L57] He touched the stone wall. Not the pipe. The pulse repeated through solid rock. Foundation pressure. He logged it.
[L58] 
[L59] His slate held a map of the tunnel system. He marked a route to Sector Fourteen. Deeper. Where the doubled harmonic seemed strongest.
[L60] 
[L61] He walked at controlled pace. The harmonic grew louder. Dust fell in repeating pulses. Whatever was wrong, it came from below.
[L62] 
[L63] ---
[L64] 
[L65] # **Chapter Two: Glass That Listens**
[L66] 
[L67] The theodolite base sat uneven. Tiffani scraped grit from under the rear foot until the bubble centered. She checked the other feet. All seated properly now.
[L68] 
[L69] She pulled three lens plates from their case. Inspected each edge under angled light. No micro-chips visible. She swapped the installed plate anyway. Eliminated the variable.
[L70] 
[L71] Her logbook opened to a fresh page. She wrote the header in order: Tide 0, Pouring. Target: Seven-Three. Instrument: theodolite, calibrated two Tides prior. Air conditions: clear, minimal haze bands. Grid-hum: baseline audible through platform grating.
[L72] 
[L73] The sight aligned on Seven-Three. She took the reading. Logged the angle. Checked it against her reference chart.
[L74] 
[L75] Offset beyond tolerance.
[L76] 
[L77] She reset the instrument. Repeated the measurement. Same offset. She wrote the value twice to confirm she hadn't transposed digits.
[L78] 
[L79] Control star read next. She swung the sight to Vela-Nine. Took that reading. Compared it to reference. Stable. Within expected range.
[L80] 
[L81] She wrote: instrument not source.
[L82] 
[L83] The glass plate bins behind her began a low resonance. Barely audible. She turned and touched the bin rim. Vibration traveled into her fingertips. Steady. Persistent. She logged: pitch change detected in storage bins.
[L84] 
[L85] The grid-hum shifted. A whine climbed into the higher register, then dropped to a lower baseline than normal. She felt it in her teeth before she consciously heard it.
[L86] 
[L87] Seven-Three stepped again. Discrete increment. Measurable. She drew projection curve segments in the logbook margin. If this rate continued—
[L88] 
[L89] She calculated the intersection point. Wrote the result. Underlined it once.
[L90] 
[L91] Footsteps on the stairs. Pella appeared at the platform edge, bread wrapped in cloth tucked under one arm.
[L92] 
[L93] "Stay back," Tiffani said. Eyes on the sight. "Don't talk."
[L94] 
[L95] Pella stopped. Set the bread on the top stair.
[L96] 
[L97] Metallic taste arrived on Tiffani's tongue. Copper. Sharp and immediate. She touched her mouth corner with one finger. Checked it. Copper-gold shimmer clung to her fingertip. Not blood. Something else.
[L98] 
[L99] She pressed the particulate against the slate edge. It stuck. Didn't brush off cleanly like normal dust. She rubbed her fingers together. The shimmer transferred between them but wouldn't fully release.
[L100] 
[L101] She wrote: external forcing. Underlined it hard enough the chalk snapped.
[L102] 
[L103] Warning protocol existed for a reason. She pulled the bell cord in the prescribed sequence: three pulls, two-count gap, two pulls. The bell tone rang flat, corrected mid-note, then stabilized wrong.
[L104] 
[L105] Apprentices on the grinding floor looked up. The wheel stopped. Everyone listened for the second strike that would mean evacuation.
[L106] 
[L107] The structure trembled.
[L108] 
[L109] Not ground-shake—that had stopped after the Piercing settled. This came through the platform feet as vibration first, then graduated to visible movement. The theodolite feet scraped against their base. Her logbook page lifted and slapped back down. She pinned it with her palm.
[L110] 
[L111] Air pressure arrived before sound. Her ribs vibrated. Breath interrupted mid-inhale. She forced herself to finish the breath. Logged: pressure wall, pre-acoustic.
[L112] 
[L113] The roar came after.
[L114] 
[L115] A dust column rose beyond the roofline. Visible over the lower buildings. Debris arced outward in widening circles. She tracked the centerline. Estimated point of origin. Marked it on her district map with one chalk stroke.
[L116] 
[L117] Gravity changed.
[L118] 
[L119] Her boots lost full contact with the platform. Stomach lifted. She counted heartbeats. One. Two. Three. Four.
[L120] 
[L121] Gravity returned.
[L122] 
[L123] Hard. Her knees buckled to absorb it. Iron taste joined the copper. She touched her gums. Blood. She spat over the platform rail and logged: gum hemorrhage, pressure differential.
[L124] 
[L125] Through the settling dust, something became visible. A dark spindle. Vertical. Massive. The boundary around it distorted—bent light, warped edges. She watched it for three full breaths. The distortion moved. Not wind-driven. The object itself was moving.
[L126] 
[L127] She wrote: bent-light. Added: object in motion, spin likely.
[L128] 
[L129] A scar appeared in the sky above the spindle. Visible in the gaps between dust clouds. Persistent. The dust cleared in places and the scar remained. She noted it as: atmospheric tear, non-closing.
[L130] 
[L131] Dust fall began in earnest. Particulate stuck to her eyelashes. Coated her lips. She tasted copper-gold with every breath. Her throat felt lined.
[L132] 
[L133] The guild bell rang evacuation sequence. Apprentices scattered. Tools clattered to the floor. Pella grabbed the bread and ran.
[L134] 
[L135] Tiffani stayed at the instrument. Took three more readings. Logged each one. The offset continued. Seven-Three was moving. Not measurement error. Actual stellar displacement.
[L136] 
[L137] She packed the theodolite with deliberate speed. Each component secured in its case slot. Lens plates wrapped. Adjustment knobs locked. The evacuation bell kept ringing but her hands stayed methodical.
[L138] 
[L139] The platform shook again. She gripped the rail. Waited for it to pass. Then finished packing.
[L140] 
[L141] She descended the stairs with the case under one arm and her logbook pressed against her chest. The copper-gold taste intensified. Each breath coated her tongue.
[L142] 
[L143] At ground level, people ran in multiple directions. No coordination. No systematic evacuation. Just fear-driven scattering.
[L144] 
[L145] She marked the spindle's location on her district map. Drew a radius around it. Calculated which structures would be in the collapse zone if it fell.
[L146] 
[L147] Then she walked toward it.
[L148] 
[L149] ---
[L150] 
[L151] # **Chapter Three: First Contact**
[L152] 
[L153] The collapse zone smelled like shattered stone and copper-gold. Jhace pushed through the dust haze. His lamp did almost nothing. The particulate hung too thick.
[L154] 
[L155] A building leaned at thirty degrees. Support beams visible through the broken facade. The grid-hum screamed from inside—wrong pitch, wrong resonance, sustained note that made his molars ache.
[L156] 
[L157] People trapped inside. He could hear them. Voices calling for help. Movement-sounds. Someone coughing in the deep interior.
[L158] 
[L159] The building shifted. Settling or pre-collapse, he couldn't tell from outside. Dust poured from new cracks. The lean increased another degree.
[L160] 
[L161] Jhace circled the structure. Looked for stable entry points. Found none. Every doorway compromised. Every window frame bent. The whole thing balanced on partial support, ready to finish falling.
[L162] 
[L163] A woman ran past him toward the entrance. "My son's in there! Third floor!"
[L164] 
[L165] Jhace caught her arm. "Structure's unstable. You go in, it collapses."
[L166] 
[L167] "Then get him out!"
[L168] 
[L169] "Can't. Not without—" He stopped. Looked at the building. At the visible stress fractures spreading through the remaining supports. At the people inside who would die when it fell.
[L170] 
[L171] He'd seen this before. Junction Seven. Twenty-three names. The plaque where he read their deaths every shift.
[L172] 
[L173] His chest tightened. Breathing got harder. Not panic—recognition. This was the moment. The choice. Go in and maybe save them, probably die trying. Or stand here and watch them die anyway.
[L174] 
[L175] The woman pulled against his grip. "Let me go! That's my son!"
[L176] 
[L177] A voice came from behind him. Clinical. Precise. "Structural integrity below survivable threshold. Entry without support guarantee produces additional casualties."
[L178] 
[L179] Jhace turned. A woman stood there with a logbook and a district map. Dust coated her hair. Blood on her lip from the pressure differential. She looked at the building like it was a measurement problem.
[L180] 
[L181] "Can you hold it?" she asked.
[L182] 
[L183] "What?"
[L184] 
[L185] "The building. Can you stabilize it long enough for evacuation?"
[L186] 
[L187] Jhace stared at her. "I'm a tunnel inspector. I check pipes."
[L188] 
[L189] "You're a metalworker. Guild certification visible on your belt. Metalworkers manifest structural reinforcement." She pointed at the building. "I calculated the collapse window. Approximately four hundred heartbeats remain. Can you hold it for that duration?"
[L190] 
[L191] "I've never—I don't know how to—"
[L192] 
[L193] "Estimated capacity exceeds requirement. You're young. No visible cost accumulation. First activation carries lowest threshold risk." She pulled out chalk. Drew something on her slate. "I'll coordinate evacuation. You prevent collapse. Division of function optimizes outcome."
[L194] 
[L195] The woman with the trapped son looked between them. Hope and desperation fighting in her expression.
[L196] 
[L197] Jhace looked at the building again. At the visible stress points. At the people he could hear but couldn't see. His hands started shaking. Not tremor—fear. Clean, simple terror at what this woman was suggesting.
[L198] 
[L199] "I don't know what I'm doing," he said.
[L200] 
[L201] "Neither do I. But the alternative is documented." She gestured at the building. "Collapse in—" she checked her slate "—three hundred seventy heartbeats. We attempt intervention or we watch."
[L202] 
[L203] Jhace's breath came shallow. He counted his heartbeats. Felt them hammering against his ribs. Thought about Junction Seven. About standing outside reading names when he could have been inside preventing them.
[L204] 
[L205] "Tell me what to do," he said.
[L206] 
[L207] The woman with the logbook stepped closer. "Touch the structure. Focus on the stress points. Will the material to hold. Manifestation follows intent backed by understanding." She paused. "Cost will accumulate. Tremor first. Possibly vein-lines. Accept it or walk away now."
[L208] 
[L209] Jhace looked at his hands. Steady. Unmarked. He thought about what they'd look like after. What he'd carry forever if this worked.
[L210] 
[L211] He walked to the building. Pressed both palms against the damaged support beam. The wrong grid-hum vibrated through his skin. He could feel the stress. The material screaming. The point where it would snap.
[L212] 
[L213] "Now what?"
[L214] 
[L215] "Manifest," the woman said. "Hold it."
[L216] 
[L217] Jhace closed his eyes. Thought about the structure. About metal and stone and the forces trying to tear them apart. He thought: *stay together*.
[L218] 
[L219] Nothing happened.
[L220] 
[L221] He tried again. Concentrated harder. Pushed his intent into the building like he could force it to obey.
[L222] 
[L223] Still nothing.
[L224] 
[L225] "It's not working," he said.
[L226] 
[L227] "You're trying to command it. That's not how manifestation operates." The woman came closer. "You understand material stress. You've worked metal for Turns. You know how forces distribute through structure. Use that knowledge. Guide the forces. Don't fight them."
[L228] 
[L229] Jhace opened his eyes. Looked at the beam under his hands. Saw where the load concentrated. Where the material compressed. Where it would fail.
[L230] 
[L231] He thought about forge work. About how heat moves through metal. How force distributes when you hammer it. How you work with the material's nature instead of against it.
[L232] 
[L233] He breathed. Let his understanding spread through the structure. Felt where the stress concentrated. Where support remained. Where collapse would begin.
[L234] 
[L235] Then he thought: *distribute the load*. Not a command. A suggestion. A guide for forces that wanted to flow if given a path.
[L236] 
[L237] Heat built in his chest. Pressure behind his sternum. Something gathering. Something responding.
[L238] 
[L239] Light threaded from his hands into the beam. Copper-gold. Thin filaments following the material's stress lines. The light moved into the structure. Found the weak points. The failing connections. The places about to break.
[L240] 
[L241] The light wrapped around them. Reinforced them. Held them.
[L242] 
[L243] Jhace gasped. The pressure in his chest expanded. Heat climbed his arms. The light intensified. More filaments. More reinforcement. The building's scream quieted. The lean stopped progressing.
[L244] 
[L245] "It's holding," the woman said. "Maintain that. Don't increase output. Stable support sufficient."
[L246] 
[L247] The building stopped settling. The grid-hum stabilized at the wrong pitch but stopped climbing. Jhace kept his hands on the beam. Kept the light flowing. Felt the cost starting.
[L248] 
[L249] Heat in his joints. Vibration in his fingertips. Something changing in his chest—hollow feeling, like capacity being used.
[L250] 
[L251] The woman moved. He heard her shouting orders. Coordinating evacuation. Directing people out of the building. Her voice stayed clinical even while organizing a rescue.
[L252] 
[L253] People ran past him. Families. Workers. Someone carrying a child. They didn't look at him. Just fled the building he was holding together with light he didn't understand.
[L254] 
[L255] His hands started shaking. Not fear anymore. Tremor. Actual physical manifestation of cost. He watched it develop. Watched his fingers vibrate against the beam.
[L256] 
[L257] "Evacuation seventy percent complete," the woman reported. "Maintain current output."
[L258] 
[L259] Jhace's breath came harder. The hollow feeling in his chest deepened. Like something essential draining away. Like paying a price that couldn't be returned.
[L260] 
[L261] More people escaped. The building groaned but held. The light kept flowing from his hands into the structure.
[L262] 
[L263] "Final group emerging," the woman said. "Ten heartbeats."
[L264] 
[L265] A man ran out carrying two children. A woman followed with an elderly person supported between her and another worker. They cleared the entrance. Kept running.
[L266] 
[L267] "Clear," the woman said. "Release the manifestation."
[L268] 
[L269] Jhace tried. The light kept flowing. His hands stayed locked to the beam.
[L270] 
[L271] "I can't—"
[L272] 
[L273] "You're holding because you're afraid it will collapse. Let go. The evacuation is complete."
[L274] 
[L275] "It'll fall—"
[L276] 
[L277] "Correct. Let it fall. The people are safe. Your job is finished."
[L278] 
[L279] Jhace's arms shook. The tremor intensified. His vision blurred at the edges. The hollow feeling in his chest had gone deep—past discomfort into something that felt like damage.
[L280] 
[L281] He thought about releasing. About pulling his hands back. About letting the building collapse now that everyone was out.
[L282] 
[L283] The light cut off.
[L284] 
[L285] Jhace staggered backward. His legs gave out. He caught himself on his knees. The tremor spread from his hands up his arms. His breath wouldn't regulate. The hollow feeling stayed.
[L286] 
[L287] The building collapsed. Slow at first, then accelerating. The support beam snapped where he'd been holding it. The structure folded into itself. Dust billowed outward. The crash echoed through the district.
[L288] 
[L289] Jhace knelt in the street. Watched his hands shake. Felt the cost settling into his body. The tremor wouldn't stop. He pressed his palms against his thighs. The vibration continued through the contact.
[L290] 
[L291] The woman with the logbook appeared beside him. She pulled out measuring cord. Checked the amplitude of his tremor without asking permission.
[L292] 
[L293] "Stage One cost accumulation," she said. "Within expected parameters for first manifestation. Duration was two hundred forty heartbeats. Output moderate. Tremor should stabilize within one Tide. Possibly persist indefinitely at low amplitude." She wrote something in her log. "What's your name?"
[L294] 
[L295] "Jhace." His voice came rough. The copper-gold taste coated his tongue.
[L296] 
[L297] "Tiffani." She helped him stand. "You prevented twelve deaths. Cost: permanent tremor, possibly minor vein-line development. That exchange rate is sustainable."
[L298] 
[L299] Jhace looked at his shaking hands. At the woman—Tiffani—with her logbook and her clinical assessment. At the collapsed building where twelve people had been trapped.
[L300] 
[L301] He'd manifested. Held a structure. Saved lives. And now his hands wouldn't stop shaking.
[L302] 
[L303] Tiffani handed him a slate. "We need to document this. I have questions about the activation sequence, the sensory experience, and the cost progression timeline. Will you answer them?"
[L304] 
[L305] Jhace took the slate. The tremor made his grip uncertain. "Right now?"
[L306] 
[L307] "Data quality degrades with time. Immediate documentation provides accurate baseline." She pulled out chalk. "First question: what did the manifestation feel like before the light appeared?"
[L308] 
[L309] Jhace sat on the rubble. Started answering her questions. Tried not to think about how his hands kept shaking.
[L310] 
[L311] Tried not to think about Junction Seven. About whether he could have done this four Turns ago. About twenty-three names that maybe didn't need to be on that plaque.
[L312] 
[L313] Tiffani wrote everything down. Measured everything. Turned his first manifestation into data points and documentation.
[L314] 
[L315] The tremor continued. The cost was already permanent.
[L316] 
[L317] But twelve people walked away. That math worked. It had to work.
[L318] 
[L319] Otherwise this cost meant nothing.
[L320] 
[L321] ---
[L322] 
[L323] # **Chapter Four: Protocol Development**
[L324] 
[L325] Jhace's workshop smelled like metal oil and coal dust. Tiffani sat at his work bench with her logbook open. She'd spent the last three Tides documenting collapse patterns. Jhace had spent them manifesting.
[L326] 
[L327] Five structures held. Five successful evacuations. His tremor had stabilized but never stopped. Thin vein-lines now showed on his forearms—copper-gold traces visible under the skin.
[L328] 
[L329] Tiffani measured them with cord. Wrote the length and distribution pattern. "Stage One progression confirmed. Vein-lines correlate with manifestation duration. Longer holds produce more extensive marking."
[L330] 
[L331] Jhace looked at his arms. At the proof of cost literally written on his skin. "They getting worse?"
[L332] 
[L333] "They're accumulating. Different metric." She drew comparison sketches. "First manifestation: two hundred forty heartbeats, minimal vein-lines. Yesterday's hold: four hundred heartbeats, significant line development. The correlation is consistent."
[L334] 
[L335] "So shorter holds produce less cost."
[L336] 
[L337] "Correct. Which suggests optimal deployment strategy: multiple operators rotating to prevent individual threshold crossing." She wrote parameters. "If we limited single-operator duration to two hundred heartbeats, cost accumulation would decrease substantially."
[L338] 
[L339] Jhace rubbed his forearm. Felt the vein-lines under his fingertips. They were slightly raised. Slightly warm. "We don't have multiple operators. Just me."
[L340] 
[L341] "Currently accurate. But manifestation capacity isn't unique to you. Other metalworkers exist. Other craftspeople with material understanding." She pulled out her district survey. "I identified fourteen potential operators within Saint Merro. We recruit them. Build a rotation system. Distribute the cost."
[L342] 
[L343] "They won't do it. Not once they see this." He held up his shaking hands.
[L344] 
[L345] "We present the math. Show them the exchange rate. Demonstrate that limited activation prevents severe cost accumulation." She started writing recruitment protocol. "Information creates choice. Choice allows participation."
[L346] 
[L347] Jhace watched her write. Watched her reduce his permanent tremor and vein-lines to data points in an optimization equation. Part of him wanted to be angry about it. The rest recognized she was right.
[L348] 
[L349] "What if they still refuse?"
[L350] 
[L351] "Then we document their refusal and continue with current capacity. But refusal based on accurate information is different than refusal based on fear." She looked up from her writing. "We show them the cost. We show them the results. We let them decide."
[L352] 
[L353] The workshop door opened. A man stood there. Older. Soot-stained hands. Metalworker by the callus pattern.
[L354] 
[L355] "You're the one holding buildings?" he asked.
[L356] 
[L357] Jhace stood. "That's me."
[L358] 
[L359] The man came closer. Looked at Jhace's tremor. At his vein-lines. "Heard about the Harbor Street collapse. Heard you held it long enough to get everyone out."
[L360] 
[L361] "Information is correct," Tiffani said. "Manifestation duration: three hundred seventy heartbeats. Evacuation: seventeen people. Zero casualties. Operator cost: tremor progression, vein-line development within Stage One parameters."
[L362] 
[L363] The man ignored her. Kept looking at Jhace. "My daughter lives on Harbor Street. Third floor. You held that building, you saved her life."
[L364] 
[L365] Jhace's chest tightened. He didn't know what to say.
[L366] 
[L367] "Can you teach me?" the man asked. "Can you show me how to do what you did?"
[L368] 
[L369] Tiffani pulled out a fresh slate. "Name?"
[L370] 
[L371] "Corren."
[L372] 
[L373] "Prior manifestation experience?"
[L374] 
[L375] "None."
[L376] 
[L377] "Craft specialization?"
[L378] 
[L379] "Metalwork. Twenty Turns in the foundries."
[L380] 
[L381] "Material understanding sufficient. Age presents elevated threshold risk but not prohibitive." She wrote assessment notes. "We can begin training. First activation will be supervised. Limited duration. Cost monitoring throughout. Participation contingent on accepting documented risk profile."
[L382] 
[L383] Corren looked at Jhace. "She always like this?"
[L384] 
[L385] "Always," Jhace said. "But she's right about the risk. You manifest, you'll get what I have. Tremor at minimum. Probably vein-lines. Possibly worse if you hold too long."
[L386] 
[L387] "My daughter's alive because you were willing to risk that. I can carry a tremor."
[L388] 
[L389] Tiffani handed him the slate. "Read this. Shows cost progression timeline, Stage One symptoms, threshold boundaries, and termination criteria. If you still want to participate after reading, we proceed with training."
[L390] 
[L391] Corren read. Took his time. His expression stayed steady even when he got to the severe cost descriptions.
[L392] 
[L393] He handed the slate back. "When do we start?"
[L394] 
[L395] ---
[L396] 
[L397] # **Chapter Five: First Network**
[L398] 
[L399] The grain warehouse on Tallow Street had four floors. All of them compromised when the aftershock hit. The structure leaned. Support columns cracked. Roof beam sagging in the middle. Two hundred people worked inside.
[L400] 
[L401] Jhace stood in the street with Corren and Tiffani. His hands shook. His vein-lines had darkened over the last Anneal. Stage One holding steady but accumulated cost visible.
[L402] 
[L403] "Two operators," Tiffani said. She'd drawn the building schematic on her slate. Marked the critical support points. "Jhace takes the main column. Corren stabilizes the roof beam. Combined duration target: one hundred eighty heartbeats per operator. Evacuation window: three hundred heartbeats estimated."
[L404] 
[L405] Corren looked at the building. At the visible damage. "Never manifested before. You want me to start with this?"
[L406] 
[L407] "Optimal learning occurs under controlled crisis conditions. I'll monitor both activation sequences. Provide real-time guidance. Terminate either operator if cost crosses acceptable parameters." She held up her measuring instruments. "This is supervised training with safety controls. Not unsupported deployment."
[L408] 
[L409] Jhace pressed his palm against the main column. Felt the stress. The wrongness in how the load distributed. "Corren takes the roof beam. I'll handle the column. Tiffani coordinates."
[L410] 
[L411] "Confirmed." Tiffani pulled the evacuation bell. Started the prescribed sequence. Inside the warehouse, workers looked up. Saw the building state. Started moving toward exits.
[L412] 
[L413] "Now," Tiffani said.
[L414] 
[L415] Jhace manifested. The heat built in his chest. Light threaded from his palms into the column. He found the fractures. The weak points. Wrapped reinforcement around them. The column stabilized.
[L416] 
[L417] Across from him, Corren pressed both hands to the roof beam. Nothing happened.
[L418] 
[L419] "Manifest," Tiffani said. "Use your understanding. Guide the forces."
[L420] 
[L421] Corren's jaw clenched. Sweat appeared on his forehead. Still nothing.
[L422] 
[L423] "I can't—"
[L424] 
[L425] "You're forcing it. Stop commanding. Start guiding." Tiffani checked her evacuation count. "Two hundred heartbeats remaining. Activate now or I abort the attempt."
[L426] 
[L427] Corren's breath came faster. Jhace watched him struggle. Recognized the pattern. The same wall he'd hit during his first manifestation.
[L428] 
[L429] "Feel where it's breaking," Jhace said. "Where the beam wants to split. Then show the forces a different path."
[L430] 
[L431] Corren closed his eyes. His breathing slowed. Then copper-gold light threaded from his hands into the beam. Thin filaments. Uncertain. But present.
[L432] 
[L433] The beam stopped sagging.
[L434] 
[L435] "Maintain that exact output," Tiffani said. "Don't increase. Stable support sufficient."
[L436] 
[L437] People flooded from the warehouse exits. Families. Workers carrying supplies. Someone with a child on their shoulders. The evacuation proceeded in organized waves. No panic. Just systematic departure.
[L438] 
[L439] Jhace felt the cost building. Heat in his joints. The hollow feeling in his chest deepening. His tremor intensified. New vein-lines formed—he could feel them threading through his forearms like hot wires under his skin.
[L440] 
[L441] Across from him, Corren gasped. His light flickered. Then stabilized.
[L442] 
[L443] "One hundred heartbeats," Tiffani called. "Corren's cost acceptable. Jhace showing elevated accumulation. Both continue."
[L444] 
[L445] The warehouse kept emptying. Eighty people out. One hundred. One hundred fifty.
[L446] 
[L447] "One hundred eighty heartbeats. Terminate in coordinated sequence." Tiffani's voice stayed level. "Corren first. Then Jhace after three-count."
[L448] 
[L449] Corren's light cut off. He staggered back. Caught himself against a cart. His hands showed tremor. Fresh. Visible. He stared at them.
[L450] 
[L451] "Three. Two. One. Jhace terminate."
[L452] 
[L453] Jhace released the manifestation. The light died. He dropped to one knee. His tremor had spiked—hands shaking so hard he couldn't hold his slate. The vein-lines on his arms glowed copper-gold. Bright enough to see through his shirt.
[L454] 
[L455] The warehouse groaned. The column cracked further. The roof beam sagged again. But it held long enough.
[L456] 
[L457] "Final group clear," Tiffani reported. "Two hundred nineteen people evacuated. Zero casualties. Collapse imminent. Retreat to minimum safe distance."
[L458] 
[L459] They moved back. The warehouse shuddered. Then collapsed. Slower than the first building Jhace had held. The structure folded methodically. Columns failed in sequence. Roof beam split. The whole thing settled into its own footprint.
[L460] 
[L461] Dust rolled outward. Jhace breathed through it. Tasted copper-gold. Felt his new vein-lines burning under his skin.
[L462] 
[L463] Corren sat against a wall. Staring at his shaking hands. "It won't stop."
[L464] 
[L465] "Correct. Stage One tremor is permanent for active manifestors." Tiffani measured the amplitude. "Yours is minimal. Well within functional parameters. You retained manual dexterity. That's optimal outcome for first activation."
[L466] 
[L467] "This is optimal?"
[L468] 
[L469] "Compared to Stage Two or Stage Three progression? Absolutely." She wrote documentation. "You held for one hundred eighty heartbeats. Prevented approximately one hundred ten deaths. Cost: permanent tremor, minor vein-line development. That exchange rate validates the methodology."
[L470] 
[L471] Jhace leaned against the wall next to Corren. Both of them shaking. Both marked with copper-gold traces. He looked at Tiffani with her measuring instruments and her clinical assessment.
[L472] 
[L473] She was documenting their permanent damage like it was data. Like their bodies were experimental results.
[L474] 
[L475] She was also right.
[L476] 
[L477] Two hundred nineteen people walked away. Two operators paid the cost. The math worked.
[L478] 
[L479] "We need more people," Jhace said.
[L480] 
[L481] Tiffani nodded. "Confirmed. I have six candidates identified. We begin recruitment tomorrow. Build systematic rotation. Prevent individual threshold crossing through distributed load."
[L482] 
[L483] Corren looked at Jhace. "This is what you've been doing? Collecting people willing to carry tremor?"
[L484] 
[L485] "Building a network. People who understand the cost and choose to carry it anyway." Jhace held up his shaking hands. Showed Corren what they'd both become. "We rotate. We limit duration. We document everything. And we keep people alive."
[L486] 
[L487] Corren studied his own tremor. The new vein-lines just starting to show under his skin. "My daughter's alive because you did this. Now I'm doing it so someone else's daughter survives." He stood. "All right. Who else are we recruiting?"
[L488] 
[L489] Tiffani pulled out her candidate list. Started explaining her selection criteria. Turned recruitment into methodology.
[L490] 
[L491] Jhace watched his vein-lines glow. Felt the hollow space in his chest where something essential had been consumed. The cost was permanent. But so was the network.
[L492] 
[L493] Two operators became three. Three would become six. Six would become a system that could sustain itself.
[L494] 
[L495] Small scale. Distributed cost. Rotation prevents catastrophic threshold crossing.
[L496] 
[L497] The math worked. It had to work.
[L498] 
[L499] Because otherwise they were just collecting people with permanent tremor for nothing.
[L500] 
[L501] ---
[L502] 
[L503] # **Chapter Six: The Shard**
[L504] 
[L505] Brother Corran found them at the training ground. Tall man. Precise movements. Voice that carried authority without volume. He wore no rank marks but everyone knew him—the Crown's investigator. The one who documented manifestation events.
[L506] 
[L507] "Jhace. Tiffani." He gestured toward the collapsed practice structure they'd been using for threshold testing. "We need to talk."
[L508] 
[L509] Tiffani closed her logbook. "Regarding?"
[L510] 
[L511] "Controlled operator network. Unauthorized training program. Systematic manifestation deployment without Crown oversight." He pulled out his own documentation. "You've activated eight operators in the last Anneal. Built rotation protocol. Established cost-monitoring systems. All without requesting permission or reporting to institutional authority."
[L512] 
[L513] Jhace's hands tightened. "People were dying. We stopped that."
[L514] 
[L515] "You prevented immediate casualties. You also created eight permanently compromised individuals. That's consequential action requiring oversight."
[L516] 
[L517] "We documented everything," Tiffani said. She handed him her records. "Cost progression timelines. Threshold boundaries. Termination criteria. Stage One parameters. All systematically tracked."
[L518] 
[L519] Corran read through her logs. His expression didn't change. "This is comprehensive documentation of unauthorized experimentation on human subjects."
[L520] 
[L521] "No," Jhace said. "This is documentation of people choosing to help. Everyone who manifested knew the cost. Saw the evidence. Made informed decisions."
[L522] 
[L523] "After you recruited them. After you demonstrated the process. After you created social pressure through rescue operations." Corran set down the logbook. "I'm not saying you acted with malice. I'm saying you built a system with permanent human cost without institutional framework or ethical review."
[L524] 
[L525] Tiffani pulled out her evacuation records. "Eight operators. Forty-three activation events. Six hundred seventy-two lives saved. Zero operator deaths. Eight permanent tremor cases. Seventeen vein-line progressions. All within Stage One parameters. The cost-to-outcome ratio validates the methodology."
[L526] 
[L527] "The Crown doesn't validate methodology through death counts. We validate through controlled study, ethical frameworks, and institutional oversight." Corran sat on the practice structure rubble. "I'm not here to stop you. I'm here because someone needs to witness what you're building. Because when this scales—and it will scale—someone needs to have documented the foundation."
[L528] 
[L529] Jhace looked at him. At the Crown investigator who could shut them down with one report. "You want to observe?"
[L530] 
[L531] "I want to understand. You've discovered something significant. Systematic manifestation deployment with cost management. That's never existed before." He pulled out a fresh slate. "But you're also operating without guard rails. Without external review. Without anyone checking whether your 'acceptable cost parameters' are actually acceptable."
[L532] 
[L533] "We check ourselves," Tiffani said. "Every activation monitored. Every cost measured. We terminate operators who approach Stage Two threshold."
[L534] 
[L535] "You created the thresholds. You defined the parameters. You determined what's acceptable." Corran wrote something. "What happens when someone crosses Stage Two anyway? When your methodology fails? When an operator manifests too long and suffers catastrophic cost? Do you have protocols for that?"
[L536] 
[L537] Silence.
[L538] 
[L539] Jhace felt the question sit in his chest. Felt the weight of what they'd built without considering failure modes. "We prevent that. We rotate. We limit duration."
[L540] 
[L541] "You prevent it until you don't. Until crisis exceeds capacity. Until someone chooses to cross threshold because the alternative is watching people die." Corran looked at both of them. "I've seen manifestation events. I've documented severe cost accumulation. I know what Stage Three looks like. Do you?"
[L542] 
[L543] "No," Tiffani said. Quiet. Factual. "We have theoretical projections based on Stage One and Stage Two progression patterns. No direct observation data for Stage Three outcomes."
[L544] 
[L545] "Then let me provide that data." Corran opened his documentation. Showed them sketches. Descriptions. Records of manifestors who'd crossed catastrophic thresholds. "Stage Three: complete autonomic compromise. Can't release manifestation voluntarily. Body locks into activation state. Cost accelerates until vessel failure or external intervention. Survival rate: approximately twelve percent. Recovery to functional state: three percent."
[L546] 
[L547] The descriptions were clinical. Detailed. Brutal.
[L548] 
[L549] Jhace read about organ shutdown. About manifestation burning through a person's capacity until nothing remained. About bodies found still manifesting long after the person had died.
[L550] 
[L551] "This is what you're preventing," Corran said. "This is why your rotation system matters. This is also what will happen eventually because crisis doesn't respect your careful planning."
[L552] 
[L553] "Then we build better protocols," Tiffani said. "Add external monitoring. Establish forced termination procedures. Create intervention methods for locked activation states."
[L554] 
[L555] "Good. Do that. Document it. Let me witness it." Corran stood. "The Crown wants institutional control. Wants to regulate manifestation, license operators, establish oversight frameworks. That's coming. It's inevitable. But before they arrive with regulations and restrictions, someone should document what actually works. How operators organize themselves. How they manage cost without institutional interference."
[L556] 
[L557] "You're saying let us continue?" Jhace asked.
[L558] 
[L559] "I'm saying I'll observe and document. I'll provide Stage Three data. I'll share crisis patterns I've witnessed elsewhere. And when the Crown arrives demanding oversight, I'll have comprehensive records showing what you built and how it functioned." He looked at both of them. "You're creating methodology from necessity. Someone should preserve that before it gets destroyed by regulations designed by people who've never manifested anything."
[L560] 
[L561] Tiffani pulled out a fresh slate. "What data do you have on manifestation triggers? We've identified material understanding as primary prerequisite but correlation isn't complete."
[L562] 
[L563] Corran smiled. Small. Genuine. "I have seventeen Turns of observation records. Let's compare notes."
[L564] 
[L565] They sat in the rubble. Shared documentation. Compared cost progression timelines. Discussed threshold boundaries and termination criteria and intervention methods.
[L566] 
[L567] Corran wasn't there to stop them. He was there to learn from them.
[L568] 
[L569] And to make sure someone remembered what they'd built before the institutions arrived to control it.
[L570] 
[L571] ---
[L572] 
[L573] # **Chapter Seven: Crisis Point**
[L574] 
[L575] The Merchant Hall collapse happened during Pouring. Four hundred people inside for the seasonal contracts assembly. The building shuddered. Support columns cracked. The roof began its slow terrible descent toward the crowd below.
[L576] 
[L577] Tiffani pulled the emergency bell. Eight operators responded. She'd drilled them for this. Coordinated manifestation deployment. Multiple operators, distributed load, managed duration.
[L578] 
[L579] "Positions," she called. "Jhace: main column. Corren: roof beam center. Senne: eastern support. Daved: western support. Kess: northern column. Ralla: foundation stabilization. Merra: evacuation coordination. Kylar: backup capacity."
[L580] 
[L581] They moved without questions. Everyone knew their roles. Everyone understood the system.
[L582] 
[L583] Tiffani held her slate. Monitored eight operators simultaneously. Measured their tremor amplitude. Watched for cost acceleration. Ready to terminate anyone who approached threshold.
[L584] 
[L585] "Activate," she said.
[L586] 
[L587] Eight manifestations lit up the collapsing building. Copper-gold light threaded from palms into failing structure. The columns stabilized. The roof stopped descending. The whole building locked into temporary suspension.
[L588] 
[L589] "Evacuation commencing," Merra reported. She stood at the main entrance directing people out in organized waves. No panic. Just systematic departure.
[L590] 
[L591] Tiffani counted heartbeats. Thirty. Sixty. Ninety.
[L592] 
[L593] Jhace's tremor spiked. She watched the amplitude increase. Watched new vein-lines forming on his arms.
[L594] 
[L595] "Jhace showing elevated cost accumulation," she called. "Prepare Kylar for substitution."
[L596] 
[L597] "Holding steady," Jhace responded. His voice strained but controlled.
[L598] 
[L599] One hundred twenty heartbeats. The evacuation continued. Two hundred people out. Two hundred fifty.
[L600] 
[L601] Senne's manifestation flickered. Tiffani saw it immediately.
[L602] 
[L603] "Senne, report status."
[L604] 
[L605] "Getting hollow," Senne said. "Chest feels empty. Light's... harder to maintain."
[L606] 
[L607] Stage Two warning signs. Tiffani checked her protocols. Senne was approaching threshold.
[L608] 
[L609] "Terminate Senne. Kylar substitute eastern support. Three-count transition."
[L610] 
[L611] Senne's light cut off. He dropped to his knees. Kylar moved immediately. Pressed his palms to the eastern support. Manifested. The support held through the operator change.
[L612] 
[L613] Three hundred heartbeats. Evacuation seventy percent complete.
[L614] 
[L615] Merra's voice came tight. Urgent. "We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can't reach main exits."
[L616] 
[L617] Tiffani's chalk stopped on the slate. Fifty people. Upper floors. No exit path.
[L618] 
[L619] "How long to clear alternate route?" she asked.
[L620] 
[L621] "Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred."
[L622] 
[L623] The operators had been manifesting for three hundred twenty heartbeats. Approaching duration limits. Cost accumulating across all active manifestors.
[L624] 
[L625] Tiffani calculated. Standard evacuation window assumed clear exit paths. Blocked stairs changed the entire equation.
[L626] 
[L627] "We need to hold longer," Merra said.
[L628] 
[L629] "Negative. Operators approaching threshold. Extended duration produces Stage Two crossing." Tiffani looked at her slate. At the cost measurements. At the people still trapped inside.
[L630] 
[L631] She had protocols for this. Clear termination criteria. Safety boundaries.
[L632] 
[L633] She also had fifty people about to die.
[L634] 
[L635] Her hands tightened on the slate. The chalk snapped between her fingers.
[L636] 
[L637] *Protocol says terminate. Protocol prevents catastrophic cost. Protocol keeps operators functional.*
[L638] 
[L639] *Protocol also lets fifty people die while we stand here following rules.*
[L640] 
[L641] Tiffani's breath caught. She looked at the building. At the eight operators holding it. At Merra trying to reach the trapped people through debris.
[L642] 
[L643] The numbers didn't work. She couldn't save everyone. She had to choose.
[L644] 
[L645] Operators or civilians. Method or rescue. Protocol or catastrophe.
[L646] 
[L647] Her chest felt tight. Breathing got harder. She'd built these systems specifically to avoid this choice. Specifically to make decisions mathematical instead of moral.
[L648] 
[L649] The math didn't care about fifty people.
[L650] 
[L651] But she did.
[L652] 
[L653] "Continue holding," she said. Voice rough. "All operators maintain current output. I'm... calculating extended duration parameters."
[L654] 
[L655] She wasn't calculating. She was abandoning protocol. Letting operators cross threshold because the alternative was unspeakable.
[L656] 
[L657] Jhace looked at her. Saw something in her expression. "Tiffani?"
[L658] 
[L659] "Forty-seven people still inside," she said. Not explanation. Statement.
[L660] 
[L661] Jhace's jaw tightened. Then he nodded. Kept manifesting. Kept paying cost.
[L662] 
[L663] Four hundred heartbeats. Corren's tremor spiked. His vein-lines glowed bright enough to see through his sleeves.
[L664] 
[L665] Five hundred heartbeats. Kess started bleeding from her nose. Stage Two symptom. Threshold crossing in progress.
[L666] 
[L667] Tiffani wrote it all down. Documented the cost. Watched her methodology fail in real time.
[L668] 
[L669] Six hundred heartbeats. Merra got the debris cleared. The trapped people started moving.
[L670] 
[L671] "Alternate route open," Merra called. "Final evacuation commencing."
[L672] 
[L673] Seven hundred heartbeats. Daved's manifestation flickered. His light dimmed. Then surged brighter. Autonomic response. Body trying to compensate. Stage Two progression.
[L674] 
[L675] Tiffani's hands shook while writing. Not tremor. Fear. Clean simple terror at what she'd done. What she was doing. What she'd keep doing until those fifty people escaped.
[L676] 
[L677] Eight hundred heartbeats. The last person cleared the building.
[L678] 
[L679] "Complete evacuation confirmed," Merra said. "All civilians safe."
[L680] 
[L681] "Terminate all operators," Tiffani said. "Immediate coordinated shutdown."
[L682] 
[L683] The lights cut off. Eight operators collapsed. Jhace dropped to both knees. Corren caught himself against a wall. Senne lay flat on the ground shaking. Kess bled from her nose and mouth.
[L684] 
[L685] Daved didn't release his manifestation.
[L686] 
[L687] His light kept burning. His hands stayed locked to the western support. His eyes showed white all around. Breath coming in gasps.
[L688] 
[L689] "Daved, terminate," Tiffani said.
[L690] 
[L691] Nothing. The manifestation continued.
[L692] 
[L693] "Daved!" Jhace crawled toward him. "Let it go. Everyone's out. You can stop."
[L694] 
[L695] Daved's manifestation intensified. His whole body glowed copper-gold. The light burned brighter. Hotter. The western support began to warp from the energy flowing through it.
[L696] 
[L697] Stage Three. Locked activation. Autonomic failure.
[L698] 
[L699] Tiffani ran to him. Grabbed his arms. Tried to pull his hands away from the support.
[L700] 
[L701] The light burned her. She felt her palms blister. Kept pulling anyway.
[L702] 
[L703] "Jhace, help me!"
[L704] 
[L705] Jhace grabbed Daved's shoulders. Together they wrenched him away from the support. His hands came free. The manifestation finally cut off.
[L706] 
[L707] Daved collapsed. Unconscious. His tremor visible even while he was out. His entire body covered in vein-lines. Glowing. Pulsing with residual energy.
[L708] 
[L709] Tiffani pressed her fingers to his throat. Found his pulse. Rapid. Irregular. Weak.
[L710] 
[L711] "He's Stage Three," she said. Quiet. Factual. "Autonomic compromise. Possible organ damage. Uncertain survival probability."
[L712] 
[L713] The building collapsed behind them. The supports failed now that no one held them. The structure folded into itself with a roar that matched the sound in Tiffani's chest.
[L714] 
[L715] Four hundred people saved. Eight operators compromised. One crossed into Stage Three.
[L716] 
[L717] She'd broken protocol. Pushed operators past safe thresholds. Done exactly what her systems were designed to prevent.
[L718] 
[L719] Fifty people lived. Daved might die.
[L720] 
[L721] The math didn't work anymore.
[L722] 
[L723] Tiffani looked at her burned palms. At Daved's unconscious body. At the other operators struggling to recover from threshold crossing.
[L724] 
[L725] She'd built methodology to avoid this exact outcome.
[L726] 
[L727] And when crisis came, she'd abandoned it anyway.
[L728] 
[L729] "Get him to the medical ward," she said. "Monitor his vital signs. Document all symptoms. If he survives, we'll have Stage Three recovery data. If he doesn't..."
[L730] 
[L731] She couldn't finish the sentence.
[L732] 
[L733] Jhace and Corren carried Daved. The other operators followed. Everyone shaking. Everyone marked with new cost.
[L734] 
[L735] Tiffani stayed behind. Looked at her slate. At the protocols she'd violated. At the cost measurements that had crossed every safe boundary.
[L736] 
[L737] Four hundred people saved.
[L738] 
[L739] One operator critically compromised.
[L740] 
[L741] She didn't know if that math validated anything.
[L742] 
[L743] Didn't know if saving people was worth destroying her methodology.
[L744] 
[L745] Didn't know if she'd make the same choice again.
[L746] 
[L747] She wrote in her logbook: *Protocol insufficient for crisis exceeding capacity. Methodology failure documented. Revision required.*
[L748] 
[L749] Then she walked to the medical ward. To witness what her choices had cost.
[L750] 
[L751] To see if Daved would survive.
[L752] 
[L753] To understand if her method could survive this too.
[L754] 
[L755] ---
[L756] 
[L757] # **Chapter Eight: Recovery**
[L758] 
[L759] Daved opened his eyes three Tides after crossing Stage Three threshold. Tiffani sat beside his bed with measuring instruments and documentation slate. She'd been there every Tide. Taking readings. Recording symptoms. Waiting.
[L760] 
[L761] "You're conscious," she said. "That's unexpected. Survival rate for Stage Three crossing is twelve percent. Conscious recovery: three percent. You're statistically anomalous."
[L762] 
[L763] Daved tried to speak. His jaw wouldn't coordinate properly. The attempt produced sounds but no words.
[L764] 
[L765] Tiffani handed him a slate and chalk. "Motor control compromised. Try writing."
[L766] 
[L767] He gripped the chalk. His hand shook so violently the chalk skittered across the slate. He tried again. Pressed harder. Managed to write: *cant stop shaking*
[L768] 
[L769] "Correct. Stage Three tremor is severe and permanent. Your autonomic nervous system experienced catastrophic disruption. Recovery is occurring but will be incomplete." She measured his tremor amplitude. Wrote the value. "You crossed eight hundred seventy heartbeats of continuous manifestation. Your body locked into activation state. We had to physically separate you from the structure to terminate manifestation."
[L770] 
[L771] Daved wrote: *remember*
[L772] 
[L773] "What do you remember?"
[L774] 
[L775] *everything* He underlined it three times. *felt myself disappearing. couldnt stop. body kept going. i was just witness*
[L776] 
[L777] Tiffani's chalk stopped on her slate. She looked at what he'd written. Read it twice.
[L778] 
[L779] "That's valuable data," she said. Quiet. "Stage Three progression described from internal perspective. No one's documented that before."
[L780] 
[L781] Daved's expression showed something between anger and despair. He wrote: *im not data*
[L782] 
[L783] "You're a person who crossed catastrophic threshold and survived. That makes you both." She set down her measuring instruments. "I pushed you past safe duration. Violated my own protocols. You paid the cost of my choice. I'm... documenting the consequences."
[L784] 
[L785] *worth it?*
[L786] 
[L787] "Fifty people lived because you held that support. Cost-to-outcome ratio validates the decision from population perspective." She looked at her documentation. "From individual operator perspective, you suffered permanent severe disability to save people you didn't know. I can't determine if that's worth it. Only you can assess that exchange."
[L788] 
[L789] Daved stared at his shaking hands. Watched the tremor that wouldn't stop. The vein-lines that covered his arms. The evidence that his body would never function normally again.
[L790] 
[L791] He wrote: *would do it again*
[L792] 
[L793] Then crossed it out.
[L794] 
[L795] Wrote: *maybe*
[L796] 
[L797] Crossed that out too.
[L798] 
[L799] Finally wrote: *dont know. ask me when tremor stops.*
[L800] 
[L801] "The tremor won't stop. That's permanent cost." Tiffani measured his vital signs. "But you're conscious. Breathing without assistance. Communicating effectively despite motor compromise. You achieved functional recovery from Stage Three. That's significant."
[L802] 
[L803] *what do i do now*
[L804] 
[L805] "You recover as much as capacity allows. We document your progression. Establish Stage Three recovery baselines. Determine if you retain manifestation ability or if threshold crossing destroyed that capacity entirely." She pulled out a fresh slate. "Then we decide if you can return to operator rotation or if your crossing permanently disabled you."
[L806] 
[L807] Daved's jaw tightened. He wrote: *want to help*
[L808] 
[L809] "Wanting doesn't determine capacity. Function determines capacity. We assess your recovery. Test your capabilities. Make decisions based on evidence."
[L810] 
[L811] *cold*
[L812] 
[L813] "Accurate. I'm clinical about permanent disability because emotion doesn't change outcomes. You crossed Stage Three. That produces consequences. I document those consequences and work with what remains."
[L814] 
[L815] She meant it practically. Daved heard it as: *you're broken now. let's measure how broken.*
[L816] 
[L817] He closed his eyes. The tremor continued behind his eyelids. The vein-lines glowed bright enough to see through closed lids. His body had become proof of catastrophic cost.
[L818] 
[L819] Tiffani stood. "I'll return tomorrow. We'll begin mobility assessments. Determine what function remains and what's permanently compromised."
[L820] 
[L821] She left. Clinical. Methodical. Turning his disability into data points.
[L822] 
[L823] Daved lay in the medical ward bed. Tried to stop his hands from shaking. Failed.
[L824] 
[L825] Tried to remember why holding that structure felt necessary. Why fifty strangers mattered more than his motor control.
[L826] 
[L827] Couldn't remember. The choice had felt obvious in the moment. Now it felt impossible to justify.
[L828] 
[L829] His body kept shaking. The cost kept accumulating. The methodology had failed him.
[L830] 
[L831] And somewhere, fifty people walked around alive because his nervous system died keeping them safe.
[L832] 
[L833] The math still didn't work.
[L834] 
[L835] ---
[L836] 
[L837] # **Chapter Nine: The Shard-Bearer**
[L838] 
[L839] Brother Corran found Jhace at the practice ground. "There's someone you need to meet. Someone who's been manifesting differently."
[L840] 
[L841] They walked to the northern district. To a collapsed housing block where repair crews worked. Corran pointed to a man lifting a support beam. No crew. No equipment. Just one person and a massive weight.
[L842] 
[L843] The man's arms glowed. Not just hands—entire limbs wrapped in copper-gold light. The light extended into the beam. The beam lifted. Controlled. Precise. Impossible.
[L844] 
[L845] The manifestation cut off. The beam settled into place. The man wiped his forehead. Showed no tremor. No vein-lines. No visible cost.
[L846] 
[L847] "Mobel," Corran said. "Watch."
[L848] 
[L849] Mobel moved to another beam. Manifested again. Lifted tonnage that should require multiple operators. Set it exactly where support structure needed it. Released the manifestation. Walked to the next beam.
[L850] 
[L851] Still no tremor. Still no visible cost accumulation.
[L852] 
[L853] Jhace counted his own tremor cycles. Watched his vein-lines glow. Compared his permanent disability to Mobel's clean skin.
[L854] 
[L855] "How?" Jhace asked.
[L856] 
[L857] "He has something embedded in his chest. Shard of the Piercing object. Fragment of whatever punctured the world." Corran gestured at Mobel. "It changes manifestation capacity. Increases output. Reduces cost. Makes him stronger than your entire network combined."
[L858] 
[L859] Jhace watched Mobel work. Watched one man do the work of eight operators. Watched him do it without paying price.
[L860] 
[L861] "What's the actual cost?" Jhace asked.
[L862] 
[L863] "Unknown. He won't let me examine the shard. Won't discuss side effects. Won't submit to monitoring." Corran pulled out his documentation. "But he's been manifesting for three Anneals. Output increasing. Cost invisible. Something's wrong with the pattern but I can't identify what."
[L864] 
[L865] Mobel noticed them watching. Set down his current beam. Walked over. Up close, Jhace saw it—copper-gold shimmer in his eyes. Faint. Easy to miss. But present.
[L866] 
[L867] "You're Jhace," Mobel said. "The one building the operator network. Training people to manifest."
[L868] 
[L869] "That's me."
[L870] 
[L871] "How many operators now?"
[L872] 
[L873] "Eight. Well, seven. One's in Stage Three recovery."
[L874] 
[L875] Mobel's expression shifted. Something like pain. "You're teaching people to destroy themselves."
[L876] 
[L877] "We're teaching people to save others. Cost happens. We manage it."
[L878] 
[L879] "You manage it by collecting people with permanent tremor. I prevent cost by manifesting alone." Mobel touched his chest. Where the shard sat embedded. "One person paying price versus eight people suffering disability. My approach is more efficient."
[L880] 
[L881] "Your approach scales to one person's capacity," Tiffani said. She'd appeared silently. Slate already out. Measuring Mobel's output against environmental factors. "You save hundreds per activation. We save similar numbers through coordinated deployment. Your individual capacity exceeds ours. Our network sustainability exceeds yours."
[L882] 
[L883] "I don't need sustainability. I need output." Mobel gestured at the collapsed buildings around them. "People are dying. Buildings are falling. I can fix more than your network can hold."
[L884] 
[L885] "Currently accurate. Long-term uncertain." Tiffani wrote calculations. "Your shard enhances manifestation capacity. That enhancement requires energy source. Where does that energy come from?"
[L886] 
[L887] "The shard provides it."
[L888] 
[L889] "Incorrect. Energy conservation applies universally. The shard doesn't create energy. It draws from somewhere. Your body. The environment. Something." She looked at the copper-gold shimmer in his eyes. "You're paying cost. You just don't recognize it yet."
[L890] 
[L891] Mobel's jaw tightened. "I've saved two thousand people in three Anneals. How many has your network saved?"
[L892] 
[L893] "Six hundred seventy-two documented rescues across eight operators and forty-three activation events," Tiffani said. "Your individual output exceeds our network total. Your cost progression is also completely undocumented. You could be approaching catastrophic threshold and wouldn't know until failure occurs."
[L894] 
[L895] "Or I could keep saving thousands while you carefully rotate people with tremor." Mobel turned to Jhace. "I respect what you're building. Protocol. Methodology. Systematic survival. But it's too slow. Too limited. People need immediate intervention, not careful planning."
[L896] 
[L897] Jhace looked at Mobel. At the man who saved thousands without tremor. Then at his own shaking hands. At the vein-lines covering his arms. At the permanent cost he carried for saving hundreds.
[L898] 
[L899] "Maybe you're right," Jhace said. "Maybe your approach saves more people. But when your shard fails—when whatever cost you're accumulating crosses threshold—who saves people then? Your capacity dies with you. Our network continues."
[L900] 
[L901] "If I die, you'll still be here rotating operators and saving dozens at a time. That's not sufficient."
[L902] 
[L903] "It's sustainable. That's different than sufficient. But it's what remains when heroes burn out." Jhace held up his tremor. "We're not competing with you. We're building something that survives you."
[L904] 
[L905] Mobel looked at them. At the network they'd built from permanent disability. At the methodology that valued survival over output.
[L906] 
[L907] "Good luck," he said. "You're going to need it when the next crisis exceeds your capacity."
[L908] 
[L909] He walked away. Back to the collapsed building. Started manifesting again. Lifting impossibilities. Saving lives. Paying costs he couldn't see accumulating.
[L910] 
[L911] Corran watched him go. "He's going to die. The shard's burning him from the inside. I can document symptoms but he won't acknowledge them."
[L912] 
[L913] "Can you stop him?" Tiffani asked.
[L914] 
[L915] "No. He's saving too many people too effectively. The Crown won't intervene while he's producing results." Corran wrote something. "I can only witness. Document what happens when manifestation capacity exceeds human tolerance. Hope someone learns from it."
[L916] 
[L917] Jhace counted his tremor. Watched Mobel lift another impossible weight.
[L918] 
[L919] Two approaches. Both saving people. Both paying costs.
[L920] 
[L921] One built for longevity. One burning bright.
[L922] 
[L923] Neither was wrong. Neither was sufficient.
[L924] 
[L925] Both would continue until capacity ran out.
[L926] 
[L927] ---
[L928] 
[L929] # **Chapter Ten: Institutional Pressure**
[L930] 
[L931] The Crown delegation arrived during Striking. Five officials. All with documentation. All with authority to regulate manifestation deployment.
[L932] 
[L933] Bram Tallow led them. Crown administrator. Face like worked leather. Voice that assumed compliance.
[L934] 
[L935] "Unregulated manifestation operations," he said. "Eight operators. No licensing. No institutional oversight. No accountability framework."
[L936] 
[L937] Tiffani handed him her logs. "Comprehensive documentation. Cost progression timelines. Threshold boundaries. Termination criteria. Six hundred seventy-two confirmed rescues. One Stage Three crossing with documented recovery. All systematically recorded."
[L938] 
[L939] Bram read through her logs. His expression didn't soften. "This is evidence of systematic human experimentation without ethical review or Crown authorization."
[L940] 
[L941] "This is evidence of crisis response operating within developed protocols," Jhace said. "People were dying. We prevented that."
[L942] 
[L943] "You created permanently disabled individuals to prevent deaths. That's consequential action requiring Crown approval before implementation."
[L944] 
[L945] "Crown approval requires proposal, review, committee assessment, and institutional authorization. That timeline produces death counts while we wait for permission." Tiffani pulled out her evacuation records. "We operated on immediate need. Developed protocols through practice. Proved methodology through results."
[L946] 
[L947] "You operated without authority. That's illegal regardless of results." Bram set down the logs. "The Crown is implementing licensing requirements for all manifestation operators. Mandatory training. Regular cost assessments. Deployment limits. Institutional oversight."
[L948] 
[L949] "We already do all that," Jhace said.
[L950] 
[L951] "You do it without Crown supervision. That's the problem." Bram pulled out official documentation. "Effective immediately, all operators must register with Crown authority. Submit to regular medical monitoring. Limit manifestation to Crown-approved crisis events. Violations result in operator suspension and possible legal consequences."
[L952] 
[L953] Tiffani wrote notes. "Crown-approved crisis events. Define approval criteria."
[L954] 
[L955] "Crisis severity. Available resources. Risk assessment. Crown authority determines deployment authorization."
[L956] 
[L957] "Crown authority determines while people die waiting for authorization," Jhace said. "We respond immediately. Structure fails, we manifest. Your system adds decision layers that produce casualties."
[L958] 
[L959] "Our system prevents operators from destroying themselves for marginal outcomes. Your Daved crossed Stage Three saving fifty people. Crown assessment would have determined those fifty lives didn't justify one Stage Three crossing." Bram met Jhace's eyes. "You let operators decide. We prevent them from making choices that produce catastrophic cost."
[L960] 
[L961] "You prevent them from saving people," Tiffani said. "Cost assessment is operator decision. They understand risk. They choose participation. Crown intervention removes agency."
[L962] 
[L963] "Crown intervention prevents exploitation. Prevents people like you from recruiting vulnerable individuals into permanent disability for your methodology." Bram stood. "Register your operators or cease operations. Those are your options."
[L964] 
[L965] He left. The other officials followed. Documentation and authority and systematic control.
[L966] 
[L967] Jhace looked at Tiffani. "They'll shut us down."
[L968] 
[L969] "They'll try. Registration might work. Might preserve network function while satisfying institutional oversight." She wrote calculations. "Or it might produce exactly what Bram wants—regulated, controlled, limited manifestation that prioritizes institutional approval over immediate response."
[L970] 
[L971] "We register anyway?"
[L972] 
[L973] "We register. We document everything. We prove methodology works better than their oversight." Tiffani pulled out fresh slates. "Then when their system fails, we'll have evidence showing what effective crisis response requires."
[L974] 
[L975] They spent the next Tide registering operators. Submitting documentation. Proving their network to people who wanted to control it.
[L976] 
[L977] The Crown wanted safety. Wanted limits. Wanted to prevent catastrophic cost through regulation.
[L978] 
[L979] Jhace and Tiffani wanted survival. Wanted response capacity. Wanted to save people even when it cost them everything.
[L980] 
[L981] Both approaches claimed to protect operators.
[L982] 
[L983] Neither trusted the other to actually do it.
[L984] 
[L985] ---
[L986] 
[L987] # **Chapter Eleven: The Hunger**
[L988] 
[L989] Daved could walk now. Slowly. His tremor made each step uncertain. But he could move.
[L990] 
[L991] Tiffani ran assessment tests. Had him grip measuring cord. Had him attempt fine motor tasks. Documented his recovery progression with clinical precision.
[L992] 
[L993] "Twenty percent function retained in precision grip. Eighty percent compromise permanent. Fine motor control insufficient for pre-manifestation craft work." She wrote the assessment. "You can't return to your previous trade. Permanent disability confirmed."
[L994] 
[L995] Daved stared at his shaking hands. At the evidence that his life had fundamentally changed. "What can I do then?"
[L996] 
[L997] "Mentorship. Training. Documentation sharing. You have Stage Three experience. That's data no one else possesses. You can teach operators to recognize warning signs. Can share what threshold crossing feels like from internal perspective. Can help prevent others from making your choice." Tiffani pulled out role assignments. "We build around your remaining capacity. Find what you can contribute."
[L998] 
[L999] "I can't manifest anymore?"
[L1000] 
[L1001] "Unknown. We'd need to test under controlled conditions. But your motor compromise is severe. Any activation would carry extreme threshold risk. Current assessment: you're non-operational for manifestation. Permanently."
[L1002] 
[L1003] Daved nodded. Accepted the verdict without protest.
[L1004] 
[L1005] Jhace sat with him after Tiffani left. Both of them shaking. Both marked with copper-gold traces. Both permanently changed by methodology they'd helped build.
[L1006] 
[L1007] "You regret it?" Jhace asked.
[L1008] 
[L1009] Daved looked at his tremor. Considered. "Don't know. I saved fifty people. Cost me everything I was. That math should work. Should feel justified. Doesn't though. Just feels like I'm broken now."
[L1010] 
[L1011] "You're not broken. You're differently functional."
[L1012] 
[L1013] "That's Tiffani's clinical phrasing for permanent disability."
[L1014] 
[L1015] "It's also accurate." Jhace held up his own tremor. "I can't do forge work anymore. Hands shake too much for precision metalwork. But I can manifest. Can coordinate operators. Can teach methodology. My capacity changed. Didn't disappear."
[L1016] 
[L1017] Daved tried to grip a tool. His hand shook too hard to maintain hold. The tool clattered to the floor.
[L1018] 
[L1019] "That's what I mean," he said. "Everything I trained for. Everything I built my life around. Gone because I held a structure too long."
[L1020] 
[L1021] "You held it because fifty people needed you to. That's not nothing."
[L1022] 
[L1023] "It's also not enough. Not enough to justify this. Not enough to make the cost feel worth it." Daved looked at Jhace. "Do you ever want more? Want to manifest bigger? Hold longer? Save more people even when you know the cost?"
[L1024] 
[L1025] Jhace's chest tightened. Because yes. Because every collapse he witnessed, every structure he couldn't reach, every person who died while he rotated operators according to protocol—all of it sat in him like hunger. Like capacity wanting to be used.
[L1026] 
[L1027] "Every Tide," Jhace said. Quiet. Honest. "Every crisis where methodology prevents me from just—doing more. Holding everything. Saving everyone. The protocols make sense. The rotation prevents catastrophic cost. But it doesn't satisfy the wanting."
[L1028] 
[L1029] "What do you do with that? With the hunger to cross threshold even when you know you shouldn't?"
[L1030] 
[L1031] "I carry it. I acknowledge it exists. I don't let it drive me toward threshold violations." Jhace looked at the practice ground. At the network they'd built. "Mobel feeds that hunger. Saves thousands. Crosses thresholds without apparent cost. Part of me wants to be him. Wants to abandon methodology and just—manifest until capacity runs out."
[L1032] 
[L1033] "Why don't you?"
[L1034] 
[L1035] "Because when my capacity runs out, the network still functions. When Mobel's capacity runs out, two thousand people lose their only intervention option." Jhace met Daved's eyes. "I want more. But I can live with less. That's the difference."
[L1036] 
[L1037] Daved nodded. Understanding settling into acceptance. He'd never manifest again. Never save lives through direct intervention. But he could teach. Could share. Could help prevent others from crossing Stage Three.
[L1038] 
[L1039] Differently functional. Not broken. Not optimal. Just—adapted.
[L1040] 
[L1041] The workshop door opened. A new recruit entered. Young. Uncertain. Asking about manifestation training.
[L1042] 
[L1043] Daved picked up a slate with shaking hands. Started explaining threshold warnings. Started teaching from experience.
[L1044] 
[L1045] Turning his catastrophic cost into prevention.
[L1046] 
[L1047] Small scale. Sustainable. Different than he wanted.
[L1048] 
[L1049] But possible. That counted for something.
[L1050] 
[L1051] ---
[L1052] 
[L1053] # **Chapter Twelve: Method**
[L1054] 
[L1055] Tiffani found Jhace on the Crown wall observation point at dark. Same place he'd stood after too many collapses. Same overlook where counting failures felt like the only honest accounting.
[L1056] 
[L1057] She didn't speak. Just measured the Seam width with her distance lens. Documented expansion rate. Calculated time to catastrophic threshold.
[L1058] 
[L1059] "It's wider," Jhace said.
[L1060] 
[L1061] "Confirmed. Forty-three percent increase since initial formation. Rate of expansion accelerating." She lowered the lens. "The Piercing's consequences continue. Geography destabilizes. Ocean claims land. Sky-wound expands. All ongoing. All beyond our capacity to prevent."
[L1062] 
[L1063] They stood quiet. Just watching. Just witnessing what they couldn't fix.
[L1064] 
[L1065] "Mobel's still out there," Jhace said.
[L1066]

[L1072] 
[L1073] Jhace almost smiled. "That's not comforting."

[L1078]
