[USER]
# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

## Chapter One: The World Before

The heat-grid risers hummed at baseline frequency.

Jhace had lived under that sound for three Turns. It told him when a joint seated clean and when thermal expansion started to make connections drift. It told him when a beam took load properly and when stress patterns shifted wrong.

Tonight it told him the forge was running true.

He worked alone on the terrace-side bench, fitting collar joints for a residential grid expansion in the Upper Terraces. The work was simple. Repetitive. The kind that let his hands move by memory while his mind stayed quiet.

Collar joint. Alignment check. Weld bead. Cool. Repeat.

He had forty-eight joints to complete before the end of Crucible. The contractor needed them installed before winter phase when the Upper Terraces pulled maximum heating load. Late delivery meant contract penalties. Penalties meant no work next Cast.

Simple rules. Follow them or fail.

The weld bead ran clean along the seam. He pulled back and checked the heat distribution with his fingertips near the metal—not touching, just reading the air. Even spread. No hot spots. Good penetration.

Collar joint number fourteen. Thirty-four remaining.

He set it aside to cool and reached for the next blank.

The workshop door opened.

Bram entered carrying a manifest folder and the expression that meant someone else's emergency was about to become Jhace's problem.

"Contractor for the Southslope water reclamation project just sent a runner," Bram said. "They need eight expansion sleeves by end of Anneal."

Jhace looked at the collar joints on his bench. "I am already committed."

"They are offering premium rate. Forty percent over standard."

"I have a delivery deadline."

"They have a regulatory deadline. If their system is not operational by next Tide, the Waterworks Guild fines them per-Tide until completion. They will pay whatever it costs."

Jhace wiped his hands on a rag. Looked at Bram. "How many sleeves?"

"Eight. Custom diameter for their pressure specifications."

"That is three Tides of work minimum."

"Which is why they are offering premium rate."

Jhace walked to the design wall where project timelines hung on brass clips. He pulled down the collar joint schedule. Looked at the calendar marks. Ran the numbers.

If he worked both projects simultaneously, he could complete the collar joints with two Tides of buffer time. The sleeves would require late shifts and precision work when he was already tired. Tired meant errors. Errors meant scrapped work and wasted material.

But forty percent premium meant he could cover material costs for next Crucible and have funds remaining. That meant stability. That meant not scrambling for contracts when seasonal work dried up.

Simple calculation. Risk against reward.

"Tell them I can deliver six sleeves by end of Anneal," Jhace said. "The remaining two by mid-following Anneal. Non-negotiable on timeline."

Bram nodded once. "I will send the terms."

He left. The door closed. The heat-grid hum continued unchanged.

Jhace returned to collar joint number fifteen.

His hands moved through familiar sequence. Position. Align. Weld. Check. The rhythm was mechanical. Reliable. The kind of work that did not demand innovation or genius. Just competence applied consistently.

He liked that.

He had tried innovation once. At Junction Seven. He had flagged a structural risk in the support grid layout. He had documented it. He had submitted it to the crew lead. The crew lead had filed it as "noted but non-critical" and kept the construction timeline moving.

Three Anneals later, Junction Seven collapsed during a grid stress test. Twenty-three people died when the main support beam failed at exactly the connection point he had flagged.

The investigation cleared him of fault. His documentation proved he had identified the risk. He had followed protocol. He had done everything correctly.

Twenty-three people were still dead.

After that, he stopped trying to improve systems. He followed specifications. He delivered on time. He did not flag risks unless they were severe enough to halt work entirely.

That kept him employed. That kept him from being responsible when things went wrong.

Collar joint number eighteen. Thirty remaining.

The weld bead cooled. He set it on the rack and reached for number nineteen.

Outside, Saint Merro's terrace bells rang for end of Shaping phase. The sound carried through stone and glass, marking the transition into Striking phase when workshops closed and people moved to evening routines.

Jhace kept working.

He would finish collar joints through number twenty-four tonight. That put him on schedule. Tomorrow he would start material calculations for the expansion sleeves. The Tides would follow each other in predictable sequence. The work would get done.

Simple. Mechanical. Reliable.

The world made sense when you followed the rules.

The heat-grid hum stayed constant. The weld beads ran true. His hands moved through practiced motions.

Collar joint number twenty. Twenty-eight remaining.

---

## Chapter Two: The Calculation

Tiffani adjusted the primary lens for the fourth interval that Tide.

The spindle had entered observable range seven Tides ago. At first it appeared as a trajectory anomaly—something moving too fast through the orbital approach corridor to be standard debris. She had flagged it in her observation log and continued standard measurement protocols.

By the second Tide, the trajectory had refined. The object was not slowing. It was not altering course. It was coming directly toward Te'Oga on a path that intersected planetary mass with precision that suggested either intent or catastrophic coincidence.

She preferred coincidence. Intent required assumptions about unknown actors with unknown capabilities.

Coincidence only required math.

She had spent the last five Tides calculating impact vectors. The Observatory lens array could track objects beyond atmospheric boundary by using geometric compensation for planetary curvature. The technique was not new. She had refined it during her training under Master Kellis, improving the calculation precision by implementing iterative correction for atmospheric distortion.

The spindle was currently 840,000 spans out and closing at velocity that made her throat tight when she converted the numbers to real-world scale.

It would reach atmospheric boundary in three Tides.

Impact in four.

She had filed reports with the Astronomical Guild. She had sent calculations to the Regional Research Office. She had documented projected impact sites, energy release estimates, and probable damage radius.

The responses had been polite acknowledgment and requests for continued observation.

No one believed the numbers.

The spindle was too large. The velocity was too high. The trajectory was too precise. The energy release on impact would be—

She stopped that calculation before completing it. The result had not changed in forty-three iterations. Running it again would not make it less catastrophic.

Tiffani returned to the observation bench and pulled her current slate. The tracking data showed consistent deceleration—minimal, but present. The object was interacting with Te'Oga's outer atmospheric layers. Friction should be converting velocity to heat.

The spindle's surface temperature had not increased.

That was wrong.

Everything she understood about atmospheric entry physics said the object should be radiating heat as it decelerated. Instead, the thermal signature remained constant at ambient temperature for its current distance from the sun.

She made a note in the margin: *Thermal properties inconsistent with standard material composition. Recommend material analysis if retrieval becomes possible.*

Retrieval would not be possible.

The impact would vaporize anything within 200 spans of the strike site. The shockwave would flatten structures within 2,000 spans. The seismic propagation would cause failures across the entire regional terrace system.

She had calculated casualty estimates. She had filed them with the proper authorities. She had done everything protocol required.

The spindle kept coming.

Tiffani walked to the western observation port and looked out across Saint Merro's terrace levels. The city descended in geometric precision from the Crown heights down through residential and commercial districts to the industrial Underface. Millions of people lived in those terraces. They moved through their Tides following predictable patterns. They trusted that the world would continue operating by comprehensible rules.

The spindle was going to prove them wrong.

She returned to the lens array and made another measurement. The trajectory had refined further. The projected entry point was shifting—not by much, but enough to matter for calculating which regions would experience primary impact effects versus secondary collapse.

Current projection: atmospheric entry over the eastern ocean, 4,000 spans from the coastal shelf. Trajectory angled to pass through Te'Oga's mantle and exit somewhere in the Crown region.

Exit.

She had tried not to think about that part.

If the spindle maintained structural integrity through planetary transit, it would punch completely through Te'Oga and continue into space. The exit wound would be—

She stopped.

Made herself breathe.

Returned to documentation.

*Tide 7, Shaping phase: Object now 720,000 spans out. Velocity 8,200 spans per set. Trajectory stable. Atmospheric entry projected for Tide 11, Kindling phase. Planetary transit duration estimated at 84-92 sets depending on mantle density variations.*

*Current impact site: Eastern ocean entry, Crown region exit.*

*Saint Merro sits approximately 400 spans from projected exit trajectory.*

Four hundred spans.

Close enough to experience catastrophic secondary effects. Far enough that direct impact would not vaporize the entire city.

She filed the updated report. Sent it to the Guild. Sent it to the Research Office. Sent it to the Regional Emergency Authority.

The responses would be the same: acknowledged, under review, continue observation.

No one was evacuating millions of people based on calculations from a single observer.

Tiffani understood the logic. She disagreed with the conclusion. But she understood it.

She pulled a fresh slate and began calculating structural failure probabilities for Saint Merro's terrace support system under extreme seismic stress. The numbers were ugly. The terraces were built for stability, not for the kind of shockwave that would propagate through planetary crust when something punched a hole through the mantle.

The lower terraces would fail first. The Underface districts would experience immediate collapse. The residential mid-terraces would crack and buckle. The Crown heights might survive if their foundation anchors held.

Might.

She documented everything. Drew failure progression diagrams. Marked high-risk zones. Calculated evacuation routes that would remain passable during partial collapse.

Then she filed all of it and returned to the lens.

The spindle was still there. Still coming. Still on course.

Three Tides remaining.

Tiffani set up the long-observation protocol. She would track continuously now until impact. Someone needed to document this. Someone needed to record what happened when an object this large moving this fast encountered planetary mass.

Even if no one would read the report until after.

She adjusted the lens. Made another measurement. Updated her calculations.

The world continued around her. Saint Merro's bells rang phase transitions. People moved through their Tides. The heat-grids hummed. Life proceeded by normal rules.

For three more Tides.

Then everything would change.

Tiffani documented that too.

---

## Chapter Three: The Piercing

### SEGMENT A — JHACE (Underface Forge)

The heat-grid hum changed pitch at boundary between Shaping and Striking phase.

Jhace heard it shift a quarter-tone flat. Then it corrected. Then it dropped again and held there.

That was wrong.

Heat-grids did not hunt for pitch. They ran at stable frequency or they failed. Hunting meant something in the distribution network was fluctuating. Load variance, thermal expansion mismatch, pressure differential—any of those would cause drift. But this was not drift. This was active correction that kept failing.

He set down the collar joint he had been fitting and walked to the grid access panel.

Bram looked up from the materials inventory. "Problem?"

"Grid frequency is unstable."

"I do not hear anything."

"Listen."

They both went quiet. The hum filled the space between them—sustained, but wrong. The pitch wavered by fractions. Not enough to be obvious. Enough to make Jhace's jaw ache from the dissonance.

Bram frowned. "That is new."

"That is wrong."

Jhace pulled the access panel and checked the pressure readout. Normal range. Temperature nominal. Flow rate consistent. Every metric read clean.

The hum stayed wrong.

"Could be transmission issue," Bram said. "Connector drift in one of the terrace junction boxes."

"Should not affect this district. We are on dedicated Underface circuit."

"Should I file a maintenance request?"

Jhace considered. Grid maintenance would mean inspection crews. Inspection meant potential shutdown. Shutdown meant lost production time. He had thirty collar joints remaining and expansion sleeves to start.

"Monitor it," he said. "If frequency drops another quarter-tone, we file."

Bram nodded and returned to inventory.

Jhace returned to the bench. He picked up collar joint twenty-one and positioned it for welding. The hum bothered him. Not because it indicated immediate failure. Because it indicated something changing in a system that should be stable.

He started the weld bead.

The floor jumped.

Not a tremor. Not settling. A sharp vertical displacement that lifted his boots by a finger-width and dropped them again.

Jhace released the welding torch and stepped back. Looked at Bram. "You felt that."

"Yes."

Another jump. Stronger this time. The hanging chain over the main bench swung. Tools rattled on their racks.

The heat-grid hum cut out entirely.

Silence hit like a physical pressure. Jhace had never heard the forge without grid hum. The absence made his ears ring.

Then sound returned—but wrong.

A deep rumble came up through the stone. Not from any specific direction. From beneath. From inside the structure itself. The pitch was too low to parse cleanly. It made his chest vibrate. Made his teeth ache.

Bram moved toward the main arch. "We should—"

The world tilted.

Not metaphor. Not sensation. Actual geometric displacement.

Down shifted by fifteen degrees to the left.

Jhace's weight redistributed through his feet without his legs moving. His inner ear screamed. His stomach lurched. He widened his stance to compensate but the floor kept tilting.

Bram grabbed the arch support. "What the fuck—"

The axis corrected. Down returned to normal for two sets. Then it shifted again—ten degrees the other direction.

The forge was not moving. Te'Oga was moving.

The planet's axial tilt was changing.

That was not possible.

The rumble increased. The floor shook continuously now. Not rhythmic. Not settling into a pattern. Just sustained violence that made standing difficult.

Dust poured through the ceiling seams.

Not normal stone dust. This was fine particulate that caught lamplight wrong—copper-gold in color, almost luminous. It coated every surface. It coated Jhace's hands. It coated his tongue when he breathed.

The taste was metal. Pure. Like touching a charged contact point with your whole mouth.

A roof brace cracked.

The sound was not sharp. It was a long fracture tone that climbed in pitch until it became physically painful. The brace dropped by a hand-width and held. Stone dust fell in sheets.

"Under the arch," Bram shouted. "Now."

Jhace moved. The floor tilted again mid-step and he stumbled. Caught himself on a workbench. The copper-gold dust made his palm slip on the metal surface.

Another brace failed completely.

A support beam dropped from its mounting and crashed into the quench trough. Water exploded outward in a sheet that should have fallen in a normal arc. Instead it rose as a solid mass, hovered for one heartbeat, then slammed back down.

Gravity was wrong.

Down was still changing.

Jhace made it to the arch. Pressed both hands against the load-bearing stone. The vibration in it was chaotic—multiple frequencies overlapping without pattern. The rumble continued beneath everything. His molars ached. His chest hurt from the sustained low-frequency pressure.

"What is happening," Bram said. Not a question. A statement of incomprehension.

Jhace had no answer.

The tremors increased. The forge supports cracked in sequence—one brace, then another, then three at once. The ceiling started to collapse in sections. Stone hit stone. Tools clattered and vanished under debris.

The copper-gold dust kept falling. It was everywhere now. Coating skin. Filling lungs. Making every breath taste like charged metal.

Then the exit wound opened in the sky.

Jhace could not see it from the Underface. But he felt it.

A pressure wave slammed through the atmosphere and into the stone. The whole city corridor snapped sideways by a full span. Not a slide. A displacement. The geometry changed and then stabilized in the new configuration.

Down shifted again. Harder this time. Twenty degrees. Thirty.

Jhace's weight drove through his feet at an angle that made his knees buckle. He held the arch stone and tried to stay upright.

The rumble peaked. The sound was no longer sound—it was vibration that bypassed ears entirely and arrived through bone. His vision blurred. His hands shook against the stone.

Then his palms started to glow.

Copper-gold light threaded out from beneath his fingers where they pressed against the arch. Not bright. Not flaring. Just present. Like the stone itself was illuminated from within the exact places he touched.

Jhace stared at his hands.

The light was not reflection. It was not lamplight catching dust. It was coming from his skin.

That was not possible.

He lifted one hand. The glow remained on the stone for two sets, then faded. He pressed his palm back down. The light returned immediately.

"Boss," Bram said. His voice was flat. Shocked. "Your hands—"

"I see it."

"What is that?"

"I do not know."

The light spread. Thin threads traced out from his contact points and followed the stress fractures in the stone. Where the light touched, the cracks stopped propagating. The arch stopped cracking.

Jhace pressed harder.

The glow increased. The threads pushed deeper into the stone. The arch stabilized completely.

His hands started to shake. Not from fear. From something inside his joints that did not match the motion of the floor. A tremor that had its own rhythm. His fingers jittered even when he tried to lock them still.

The rumble began to fade.

The tremors decreased. The axis shifts slowed. Down was still wrong—Te'Oga was tilted nearly upright now instead of its normal inclination—but it had stopped actively changing.

The collapse continued in diminishing waves. More braces fell. More stone hit stone. But the catastrophic acceleration had passed.

Jhace kept his hands on the arch until the glow faded completely. When he finally pulled them away, his palms were unmarked. No burns. No damage. Just the tremor that would not stop.

Bram stared at him. "What did you just do?"

"I do not know."

"That was not—that is not—"

"I know."

Outside, Saint Merro screamed.

The sound carried through stone—thousands of voices blending into sustained noise without pattern. Collapse tones. Bells ringing wrong. Human voices calling for help.

Jhace looked at his shaking hands. At the copper-gold dust coating his sleeves. At the forge that had partially survived because he had held an arch with glowing palms.

Nothing made sense.

The world had rules. Physics. Predictable cause and effect.

Those rules had just changed.

His hands kept shaking.

Bram moved toward the door. "We need to help. People are—"

"Yes."

They left the forge and entered Saint Merro's broken terraces.

---

### SEGMENT B — TIFFANI (Glass Observatory Rim)

The spindle exited through the Crown region at Kindling phase plus forty-seven sets.

Tiffani saw it through the primary lens—a geometric impossibility moving at velocity that should not permit visual tracking. But she tracked it anyway. She had been tracking it for eight Tides.

She watched it leave.

The exit wound it left behind was visible as atmospheric distortion—a seam in the sky where air pressure and thermal gradients had been catastrophically disrupted. The distortion held stable instead of dispersing. It marked the exact trajectory the spindle had followed.

A scar across the world.

Tiffani documented the exit angle. The velocity. The duration of atmospheric transit. She recorded everything even though her hands shook and her instruments were breaking around her.

The pressure wave hit six sets after visual confirmation of exit.

The observatory frame imploded.

Glass panels shattered inward and then exploded outward in the same motion. The sequence was wrong. The physics were wrong. Sound arrived after force. Light arrived after impact.

The rules had changed.

Tiffani dropped flat and tried to protect her measurement slates. Shards cut the air above her. Metal fittings snapped out of their sockets and became projectiles. Her primary lens case slammed into the observation table and shattered.

The floor jumped.

Down shifted by twenty degrees to the left. Her stomach lurched. Her inner ear could not compensate. She lost orientation completely for three sets.

Then down shifted again. Harder. Thirty degrees the other direction.

The planet's axis was tilting.

Te'Oga was nearly upright now. The orbital mechanics were catastrophically disrupted. Climate patterns would collapse. Ocean currents would reverse. Atmospheric circulation would—

The instrument frame fell.

A metal support beam dropped from its ceiling mount and pinned her lower leg against the stone observation platform lip. The pain arrived sharp and clarifying. Her breath stopped once. She forced it back in.

The collapse continued in waves. The Crown terrace system cracked. Bell towers failed. Sections of residential structure dropped by full spans. Dust rose—copper-gold in color, luminous in failing lamplight.

It coated everything. It coated her tongue. It made her throat raw when she breathed.

Tiffani tried to shift the beam by leverage. The angle was wrong. The weight was too much. Her hands slipped on copper-gold dust. The beam did not move.

She heard footsteps on the stairs.

Fast. Uneven. Someone moving by compulsion instead of caution.

A man appeared in the broken doorway. Soot-stained vest. Inspector markings. Hands covered in the same copper-gold dust that coated everything.

His hands were shaking. Not a full-body tremor. Just his fingers. Continuous. Involuntary.

He looked at her leg. At the beam. At the stone lip. Then at her face.

"Where is it worst," he said.

Not comfort. Assessment.

Tiffani's breath caught. Not from pain. From recognition of competence expressed as a question.

She pointed to the bind point where metal had hooked against stone. "The frame is caught. You cannot pull straight. You need to rotate the contact angle."

He nodded once.

He set his hands on the beam where she indicated. He adjusted his stance. He used the stone lip as leverage point.

His hands glowed.

Copper-gold light threaded out from beneath his palms and traced the beam's stress lines. Where the light touched, the metal became easier to move. Not soft. Not weakened. Just—compliant.

He rotated the frame by the exact angle required. The beam lifted.

Tiffani pulled her leg free. The pain increased when pressure released. She bit down on the sound and stopped it.

The man caught her under the shoulders before she fell onto shattered glass.

"Can you stand," he asked.

"No. Leg compromised. Carry required."

He did not argue. He lifted her with one arm under knees and one behind back. His hands shook continuously against her vest. The tremor was getting worse.

Tiffani looked past his shoulder as he moved toward the stairs.

The city was collapsing in stages. The Underface was gone. The mid-terraces were cracking. The Crown heights were—

The sky had a wound in it.

A visible seam where the spindle had exited. Atmospheric distortion holding stable when it should disperse.

The world had changed.

Physics had rules. Cause and effect. Predictable outcomes.

The man carrying her had glowing hands.

Magic had just entered reality.

Tiffani started documenting in her head. She could not write. Her slates were gone. Her instruments were broken. But she could observe. She could remember.

Observation: Subject's hands emit copper-gold luminescence during contact with structural materials. Luminescence correlates with material compliance effect. Duration and intensity vary. Side effect: progressive tremor in subject's hands.

The man navigated collapsed corridors with precision. He listened to the structure. He chose routes based on load assessment. He moved like someone who understood engineering through touch.

"Name," Tiffani said.

"Jhace."

"Occupation."

"Metalworker. Underface forge district."

"The light in your hands. When did it start."

His grip tightened slightly. "During the collapse. I do not know what it is."

"Neither do I."

They descended through Saint Merro's broken terraces. Thousands were dead. More were dying. The world had tilted. The sky had a wound. Magic existed.

Tiffani kept observing.

Because observation was the only control still available.

Because someone needed to document what happened when the rules changed.

The Piercing had lasted ninety-two sets.

Its consequences had only started.

[ASSISTANT]
Your current Chapter 3 is vague for two reasons:

1) **It spends too many words “explaining” the event (pitch, metrics, degrees, axis math) instead of making the reader live through it.**  
2) **It breaks your own canon:** Jhace’s **first** manifestation happens at **Chloen’s pin** (beam contact + dust), not as a calm “arch stabilization” moment.

Below is a **drop-in replacement Chapter 3** that follows your handover brief: two POV segments, correct sensory assault, correct first Soulpulse activation, correct first meeting + rescue, lexicon-compliant (no inches/feet/miles, no “magic spell,” no divine/meta, 1–9 spelled, 10+ numeric). Narration stays hard/physical and avoids similes/metaphor.

```md
## Chapter Three: The Piercing

### SEGMENT A — JHACE (Underface Forge)
**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Shaping

The forge started shaking before the sound arrived.

Jhace felt it in the bench first. The collar joint blank rattled against the clamp. The hanging chain over the quench trough swung without settling into a cycle. The movement did not repeat clean. It changed direction inside the same set.

Bram looked up from the inventory slate. Chloen stopped mid-step near the trough, both hands on the rim.

Jhace put his palm to the nearest riser casing.

The heat-grid was still warm. The hum that usually lived inside the pipe was unstable. It did not hold a steady cadence. It thinned for one heartbeat, returned, then thinned again. The rhythm did not match any load cycle he knew.

Bram watched him. “What is it.”

“Something upstream is forcing correction.” Jhace kept his palm on the casing. “It keeps failing.”

The tremor increased. The floor did not just vibrate. It displaced. A lift by a finger-width, then drop. Tools clicked on their rack. The lamp flame flattened and then snapped upright again.

Chloen swallowed. “That is not normal settling.”

“No,” Jhace said. “It is not.”

The deep rumble arrived.

It came up through the stone and into bone. It sat under hearing. It made his molars ache. It made his ribs feel tight. The rumble held steady instead of peaking and passing.

Then the pull of gravity shifted.

Not enough to throw him. Enough to force his weight into a different part of his foot without his legs moving. His stomach lurched. His inner ear failed to find a stable reference. He widened his stance. The stance did not match the next shift.

Bram braced one hand on the main arch column. “Under the arch. Now.”

Jhace moved. The floor shuddered and the corridor walls flexed. Dust fell from the ceiling seams in thin sheets. The dust was not just stone powder. Copper-gold particulate mixed in it, catching lamplight. It coated Jhace’s tongue the moment he inhaled. The taste was metal and grit.

Chloen coughed once and tried to stop herself from coughing again.

A roof brace cracked.

The sound was long and rising. It lasted too many heartbeats. The brace dropped by a hand-width and held. Another brace dropped and did not hold. Stone fell in a slab. The quench trough water surged and slapped the rim. The water did not settle into normal ripples. It rose, hovered for one heartbeat, then slammed down again.

The axis shift landed harder.

Down changed angle. Then changed again. A fraction each time. Enough to make boots slide on dust. Enough to make knees want to fold.

Bram grabbed Jhace by the collar and yanked him under the main arch. The move saved him from the next falling shelf. Iron and tools crashed where his head had been.

Chloen screamed.

A beam had dropped across the quench trough corner. It pinned her lower leg against the stone lip. Her shin was trapped under a metal edge that had turned copper-hot from the heat blooming through the structure. The beam vibrated under load. The vibration did not match the tremors. It had its own chaotic oscillation.

Jhace saw Junction Seven.

Not as memory. As pressure in his throat and chest. Twenty-three dead. A flagged risk ignored. A system kept running. Bodies under stone anyway.

He did not form a plan. He moved.

Bram caught him once. “Jhace—”

Jhace tore free. He dropped to both knees in dust and reached for the beam.

It did not move.

He shifted grip. Tried again. The beam flexed by a fraction and then locked. The load path was wrong. The weight above them kept changing as the planet finished correcting its tilt.

Chloen’s breath went short. “It hurts.”

“I know,” Jhace said.

Bram crouched beside him. “Do not break your hands. You will not win leverage.”

Jhace stared at the trapped point where metal bit into flesh and stone.

He had one working truth left: contact. Intent. Load. Duration. He did not have a crew. He did not have wedges. He did not have time.

He had his hands and a single need.

Hold. Just hold long enough.

He pressed both palms flat on the copper-hot beam.

Copper-gold dust coated his skin. The beam was coated in the same dust. Sweat turned it into paste against his palms. Pain hit immediately. Not burn-through pain. Contact pain that made nerves scream information.

His intent narrowed until nothing else existed.

Hold.

Copper-gold light threaded out from beneath his palms.

It did not flare outward. It stayed tight to contact. It traced the bend line where the beam had torqued. It traced hairline fractures in the metal that had started to propagate under the shifting load.

The fractures stopped spreading.

The beam’s vibration changed. The chaotic oscillation organized into a stable shiver that matched his pressure.

Jhace pushed.

The beam moved one finger-width.

Chloen’s leg came free with a wet sound. She screamed again and then bit her sleeve hard enough to leave teeth marks in cloth.

Jhace hauled her backward by her vest strap. Bram caught her shoulders and dragged her under the arch.

The light cut off.

Jhace’s hands started shaking.

Not the aftershock shake. Not fear. A tremor inside the joints, independent of the floor movement. He tried to clamp his fingers into a fist. The tremor persisted.

He stared at his palms. No burns. No blisters. Dust and sweat and tremor.

Bram stared too. “What did you do.”

“I do not know.”

The rumble began to fade. The axis stopped shifting. The tremors did not stop, but they reduced from sustained violence into pulsing waves. The forge continued collapsing in stages. Stone fell. A brace dropped. The corridor beyond the arch groaned.

Outside, Saint Merro screamed.

The sound came through stone in layers. Bells rang out of sequence. Human voices overlapped into a sustained roar. The city was failing above them. Not one failure. Hundreds.

Jhace’s throat tightened.

He saved one.

He could not save the rest.

The thought arrived as weight behind the eyes and under the ribs. His hands shook harder. He swallowed and tasted copper-gold dust and blood from gums torn by pressure.

He could not sit in that weight. Stillness felt like choosing deaths.

Bram tightened the strap around Chloen’s leg. “We move. Ladder shaft. Upslope.”

Jhace nodded once. He took Chloen under her shoulders again. Bram took her other side.

They carried her toward the ladder shaft while the Underface corridor continued to shed stone and dust.

Jhace’s hands would not stop trembling.

He kept moving anyway.

---

### SEGMENT B — TIFFANI (Glass Observatory Rim)
**Tide:** 0 | **Phase:** Shaping → Quench

Tiffani had the trajectory marked before the sky admitted it was real.

She had been tracking the spindle for eight Tides. She had refined the entry line, the projected transit, the exit region. Her calculations said: eastern ocean entry, Crown region exit, Saint Merro close enough for catastrophic secondary effects.

She did not stop measuring.

Stopping felt like abandoning the only thing she controlled.

The seam appeared first.

A visible distortion along the projected line. The lens showed refraction values that did not match atmospheric behavior. The distortion held in place instead of dispersing.

Then the spindle became fully visible through the primary array.

It did not slow.

It did not flare with entry heat. The thermal signature stayed wrong. The velocity stayed constant beyond what her models permitted.

She documented anyway. Angle. Transit time. Distortion width. Every value she could capture before impact conditions destroyed her instruments.

The exit occurred at Kindling plus 47 sets.

She saw the spindle breach through the Crown region sky as a straight line crossing her lens view in too few sets. The air around the exit line warped. The seam brightened for one heartbeat, then stabilized as a persistent atmospheric scar.

Her first thought was measurement.

Her second thought was correction: her “far” had been wrong by 400 spans.

The pressure wave hit six sets later.

The observatory frame failed.

Glass did not crack first. It imploded inward. Panels shattered into the room. Then the remaining panels blew outward. The sequence did not match normal physics. Force arrived before sound. Light arrived in the wrong order. Her instruments became projectiles.

Tiffani dropped flat and pulled her slates tight to her chest.

Metal fittings snapped free. A lens housing slammed into the table and shattered. The floor displaced under her body by a finger-width and then by more.

Gravity shifted.

Down changed angle. Her stomach lurched. Her inner ear failed to stabilize. She counted heartbeats to force orientation. The count did not help.

The axis correction landed again. Harder.

The observatory support frame collapsed.

A metal strut dropped across her lower leg and pinned it against the stone lip. Pain arrived sharp and total. Breath stopped once. She forced it back in.

She tried to move the strut by leverage. Her hands slipped on dust.

Copper-gold particulate poured through every crack.

It coated her teeth. It coated her tongue. The taste was metal and grit. Her gums bled when she inhaled too hard. She swallowed and tasted blood mixed with copper-gold dust.

Outside, Saint Merro screamed.

Tiffani’s first thought was not help.

It was error.

The frame should have held under the load she rated. If it did not, she had missed a stress path or a material defect or a capacity mismatch.

Personal failure had rules. Random catastrophe did not.

She chose personal failure as the more controllable option.

Footsteps hit the stairs. Fast. Uneven. Not a runner cadence. Someone moving by compulsion.

A man appeared in the broken doorway with dust in his hair and copper-gold residue on his sleeves. His hands shook with an involuntary tremor.

He did not ask if she was fine.

He looked at her leg. Looked at the pin point. Looked at the angle.

“Where is it worst,” he said.

Not comfort. Diagnosis.

Tiffani forced one clean breath. “Here.” She pointed to the bind point where metal hooked against stone. “Do not pull straight. Rotate the contact angle.”

He nodded once.

He did not use brute force.

He set his hands where she indicated. He shifted stance to match the geometry. He levered off the stone lip and rotated the strut by a fraction.

The strut lifted enough.

Tiffani pulled her leg free. Pain spiked as pressure released. She made a short sound and stopped it.

The man caught her under the shoulders before she fell onto glass.

“Can you stand,” he asked.

“No.” She kept her voice level. “Leg compromised. Carry required.”

He lifted her. One arm under knees. One behind back. He kept her injured leg stable against his torso.

His hands shook continuously against her vest fabric. The tremor amplitude increased when he tightened grip.

Tiffani did not ask why he was shaking. She observed it as data.

They moved downslope through a stairwell that still existed.

The city was failing in stages. Towers leaned. Bell structures collapsed first. Dust rose and hung. The sky seam remained visible along the entry-exit line as persistent atmospheric distortion.

Tiffani kept her eyes on the failures.

Observation was the only control she had left.

“Name,” she said.

“Jhace,” he answered.

“Occupation.”

“Metalworker.”

The tremor in his hands continued as he carried her. Copper-gold dust coated both of them. The taste stayed in her mouth and did not fade.

They reached the next terrace landing. The screaming got louder. The collapse tones multiplied.

Jhace kept moving.

Tiffani kept observing.

The Piercing had lasted under 90 sets.

Its consequences were still unfolding.

---
```

- Chloen’s pin + broken leg,  
- Jhace’s tremor beginning immediately,  
- Tiffani documenting “contact → compliance effect → tremor,”  
without naming Soulpulse yet (let them name it later through Corran/Tiffani’s doctrine work).

---

# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

## Chapter One: The World Before

**Tide:** -4 | **Phase:** Striking

The heat-grid risers ran at stable frequency.

Jhace had lived under that hum for three Turns. Consistent pitch. Predictable thermal expansion patterns. The sound told him when a joint seated clean and when stress started to redistribute wrong. It told him when a beam carried load properly and when fracture lines began.

Tonight it told him the forge was running true.

He worked the terrace-side bench alone, fitting collar joints for an Upper Terrace residential grid expansion. Repetitive work. The kind where his hands moved by practiced sequence while his mind stayed quiet.

Position blank. Align seam. Weld bead. Cool. Check penetration. Repeat.

Forty-eight joints total. Contractor needed them before winter phase when Upper Terrace heating demand peaked. Late delivery meant contract penalties. Penalties meant no work next Cast.

Simple rules. Follow them or do not eat.

The weld bead ran clean. He checked heat distribution with his palm held near the metal—not touching, reading the air gradient. Even spread. No hot spots that meant incomplete fusion.

Joint fourteen complete. Thirty-four remaining.

He set it on the cooling rack and reached for blank fifteen.

The workshop door opened. Bram entered carrying a manifest folder and wearing the expression that meant someone else's crisis was becoming Jhace's schedule problem.

"Southslope water reclamation contractor sent a runner." Bram set the folder on the inspection table. "They need eight custom expansion sleeves by end of Anneal."

Jhace looked at his collar joint queue. "I am committed to current contract."

"They are offering premium rate. Forty percent over standard."

"I have delivery deadline in six Tides."

"They have regulatory deadline. If their reclamation system is not operational by next Tide, Waterworks Guild fines them per-Tide until completion." Bram tapped the folder. "They will pay whatever it costs."

Jhace wiped his hands on a rag and walked to the project timeline wall where schedules hung clipped to brass rails. He pulled the collar joint chart. Studied the Tide markers. Ran numbers.

If he worked both contracts in parallel, collar joints finished with two Tides buffer. The sleeves required precision work during late shifts when fatigue made errors more likely. Errors meant scrapped material and wasted Tides.

But forty percent premium meant material costs covered for next Crucible plus reserve funds. Reserve meant not scrambling for contracts when seasonal work contracted.

Risk against stability. Simple calculation.

"Tell them six sleeves delivered end of Anneal," Jhace said. "Remaining two by mid-following Anneal. Timeline is not negotiable."

Bram nodded once and left.

The door closed. The heat-grid hum continued unchanged.

Jhace returned to joint fifteen.

His hands moved through familiar rhythm. Position. Align. Weld. The sequence was mechanical. Reliable. Work that required competence applied consistently, not innovation or inspiration.

He preferred it that way.

He had attempted innovation once. Junction Seven expansion project, three Turns ago. He had flagged a structural risk in the support grid geometry—documented the load path mismatch, submitted the assessment to crew lead. Crew lead filed it as "noted, non-critical" and maintained the construction schedule.

Three Anneals later during stress testing, Junction Seven collapsed. Main support beam failed at the exact connection he had flagged. Twenty-three people died in the cascade failure.

The investigation cleared him. His documentation proved he had identified the risk. He had followed protocol. Done everything correctly by regulatory standard.

Twenty-three people remained dead.

After Junction Seven, he stopped proposing system improvements. He followed specifications. Delivered on schedule. Only flagged risks severe enough to halt work entirely—the kind that would make his non-compliance the liability.

That kept him employed. That kept him from being responsible when systems failed anyway.

Joint eighteen complete. Thirty remaining.

The weld cooled. He racked it and reached for nineteen.

Outside, Saint Merro's terrace bells rang end of Shaping phase. The sound carried through stone and glass, marking transition into Striking when workshops typically closed and people moved to evening routines.

Jhace kept working. He would complete through joint twenty-four tonight. That maintained schedule. Tomorrow he would run material calculations for the expansion sleeves. The Tides would proceed in sequence. Work would complete.

Simple. Systematic. Reliable.

The world made sense when you followed the rules and delivered what you promised.

Heat-grid hum stayed constant at baseline frequency. Weld beads ran true. His hands executed practiced motions without conscious direction.

Joint twenty complete. Twenty-eight remaining.

The forge made sense. Metal behaved predictably under known conditions. Apply heat, pressure, time in correct proportion and joints fused clean. Fail to apply them correctly and joints failed. Cause and effect. No mysteries.

He liked that.

He reached for blank twenty-one and positioned it in the clamp assembly.

---

## Chapter Two: The Calculation

**Tide:** -1 | **Phase:** Kindling

Tiffani adjusted the primary lens array for the sixth time that Watch.

The object had entered long-range observation seven Tides ago—initially logged as trajectory anomaly moving too fast through the orbital approach corridor to match debris classification. She had documented it in her observation record and continued standard measurement protocol.

By Tide two, trajectory had refined. The object was not decelerating. Not altering vector. It was approaching Te'Oga on intercept course with precision that suggested either deliberate targeting or catastrophic coincidence.

She preferred coincidence. Targeting required assumptions about unknown actors with capabilities beyond current engineering framework.

Coincidence only required mathematics.

She had spent five Tides calculating impact vectors. The Observatory lens array tracked objects beyond atmospheric boundary using geometric compensation for planetary curvature—technique refined during her training under Master Kellis. She had improved calculation precision by implementing iterative atmospheric distortion correction.

The spindle was currently 720,000 spans out and closing at velocity that made her throat constrict when she converted values to real-world scale.

Atmospheric boundary contact in two Tides.

Impact in three.

She had filed reports with Astronomical Guild. Sent calculations to Regional Research Office. Documented projected impact coordinates, energy release estimates, probable damage radius patterns.

Responses had been polite acknowledgment and requests for continued observation.

No one believed the numbers.

The spindle was too large. Velocity too high. Trajectory too precise. The energy release on impact would be—

She stopped that calculation path. The result had not changed across fifty-three iterations. Running it again would not make the outcome less catastrophic.

Tiffani returned to the observation bench and pulled her current slate. Tracking data showed minimal deceleration—the object was interacting with Te'Oga's outer atmospheric layers. Friction should be converting velocity to thermal output.

The spindle's surface temperature had not increased.

That violated atmospheric entry physics. The object should be radiating heat as it decelerated through atmospheric resistance. Instead, thermal signature remained constant at ambient solar distance temperature.

She made margin notation: *Thermal properties inconsistent with standard material composition. Material analysis recommended if retrieval becomes possible.*

Retrieval would not be possible.

Impact would vaporize anything within 200 spans of strike coordinates. Shockwave would flatten structures within 2,000 spans. Seismic propagation would trigger failures across entire regional terrace architecture.

She had calculated casualty estimates. Filed them with proper authorities. Completed everything protocol required.

The spindle continued approach.

Tiffani walked to the western observation port and looked across Saint Merro's terrace levels. The city descended in geometric precision from Crown heights through residential and commercial districts down to industrial Underface. Millions of people occupied those terraces. They moved through their Tides following predictable patterns. They trusted the world would continue operating by comprehensible physical laws.

The spindle was going to demonstrate they were wrong.

She returned to the lens array and recorded another measurement. Trajectory had refined further. Projected entry coordinates were shifting—not significantly, but enough to matter for calculating which regions would experience primary impact effects versus secondary structural collapse.

Current projection: atmospheric entry over eastern ocean, 4,000 spans from coastal shelf. Trajectory angled to transit through Te'Oga's mantle and exit somewhere in Crown region.

Exit.

She had been avoiding that calculation.

If the spindle maintained structural integrity through planetary passage, it would punch completely through Te'Oga and continue into space. The exit wound would be—

She stopped. Made herself breathe properly. Returned to documentation.

*Tide -1, Kindling phase: Object 720,000 spans out. Velocity 8,200 spans per set. Trajectory stable. Atmospheric entry projected Tide 0, Kindling phase. Planetary transit duration estimated 84-92 sets depending on mantle density variation.*

*Impact site current projection: Eastern ocean entry, Crown region exit.*

*Saint Merro location: approximately 400 spans from projected exit trajectory.*

Four hundred spans.

Close enough to experience catastrophic secondary effects. Far enough that direct impact would not vaporize the city entirely.

She filed the updated report. Transmitted to Guild. Transmitted to Research Office. Transmitted to Regional Emergency Authority.

Responses would be identical: acknowledged, under review, continue observation.

No one was evacuating millions based on calculations from single observer.

Tiffani understood the institutional logic. She disagreed with the conclusion. But she understood it.

She pulled fresh slate and began calculating structural failure probability for Saint Merro's terrace support architecture under extreme seismic stress. The numbers were catastrophic. Terraces were engineered for stability under normal load conditions, not for shockwave propagation through planetary crust when something punched hole through the mantle.

Lower terraces would fail first. Underface districts would experience immediate collapse. Residential mid-terraces would fracture and buckle. Crown heights might survive if foundation anchors held.

Might.

She documented everything. Drew failure progression diagrams. Marked high-risk zones. Calculated evacuation routes that would remain passable during partial structural collapse.

Then filed everything and returned to the lens.

The spindle was still there. Still approaching. Still on course.

Two Tides remaining.

Tiffani initiated continuous observation protocol. She would track without interruption until impact now. Someone needed to document this. Someone needed to record what occurred when object this massive moving this fast encountered planetary mass.

Even if no one read the report until after.

She adjusted lens focus. Recorded another measurement. Updated calculations.

The world continued around her. Saint Merro's bells rang phase transitions. People moved through Tides. Heat-grids hummed at stable frequency. Life proceeded according to normal rules.

For two more Tides.

Then everything would change in ways she could calculate but not prevent.

Tiffani documented that assessment as well.

Her hands were already raw from chalk dust and repeated slate notation. She had forty-seven Watches of continuous observation remaining before atmospheric entry.

She would complete all of them.

Because stopping felt like abandoning the only thing she still controlled: accurate measurement of approaching catastrophe.

The spindle closed distance at 8,200 spans per set.

Tiffani recorded the value and prepared for the next measurement cycle.

---

## Chapter Three: The Piercing

[Use the corrected version you provided - that's the canonical Chapter 3]

[ASSISTANT]
Good bones. It’s already closer to “field-real” than your earlier version. But it **does not align cleanly with the canonical Chapter 3** yet, and there are a couple lexicon/continuity landmines.

## What’s off (and how to fix it)

### 1) Phase mismatch in Chapter 1
You label Ch1 **“Striking”** but then say the bells mark **end of Shaping → transition into Striking**. That’s a straight continuity error.

**Fix:** Make Ch1 **Shaping**, and let it end as the bells push into Striking. Or keep it Striking and remove the Shaping→Striking line.

### 2) Location drift vs canonical Ch3
Canonical Ch3 starts with **Jhace already in the Underface forge** with Bram + Chloen present. Your Ch1 reads like he’s on an “Upper Terrace bench.”

**Fix:** Keep him in Underface. He can still be fabricating Upper Terrace parts, but the physical setting should be the Underface shop.

### 3) “By Tide two” is ambiguous / wrong sign
In Ch2 you’re in **Tide -1**, then you say **“By Tide two”** as if time is moving forward. That breaks your negative Tide framing.

**Fix:** Use “By the second Tide of tracking” or “By the second Tide after initial observation.” Don’t number it like it’s on the main timeline.

### 4) The tone is still a little “report-shaped” in Ch2
Not fatal, but you can add **one human crack** without breaking voice: fatigue behaviors, chalk-stiff fingers, refusing to leave lens, etc. You already started doing that near the end—do it earlier once.

---

### Chapter Two — revised (time phrasing fixed, one more human edge)
```md
## Chapter Two: The Calculation

**Tide:** -1 | **Phase:** Kindling

Tiffani adjusted the primary lens array for the sixth time that Watch.

The object had entered long-range observation seven Tides ago—initially logged as trajectory anomaly moving too fast through the orbital approach corridor to match debris classification. She had documented it and continued standard measurement protocol.

By the second Tide of tracking, the vector stopped behaving like accident. The object did not decelerate. It did not alter course. It approached Te’Oga on an intercept line with precision that suggested either deliberate targeting or catastrophic coincidence.

She preferred coincidence. Targeting required assumptions about unknown actors with capabilities beyond current engineering framework.

Coincidence only required mathematics.

She had spent five Tides calculating impact vectors. The Observatory lens array tracked objects beyond atmospheric boundary using geometric compensation for planetary curvature, refined under Master Kellis. She had improved the correction loop for atmospheric distortion. The work did not change the conclusion. It only reduced uncertainty.

The spindle was currently 720,000 spans out and closing at velocity that tightened her throat when she converted values to real-world scale.

Atmospheric boundary contact in two Tides.

Impact in three.

She had filed reports with the Astronomical Guild. Sent calculations to the Regional Research Office. Documented projected strike coordinates, energy release estimates, probable damage radius patterns.

Responses had been polite acknowledgment and requests for continued observation.

No one moved a city on one observer’s numbers.

The spindle was too large. Velocity too high. Trajectory too clean. The energy release on impact would be—

She stopped that path. The value did not change across 53 iterations. Running it again would not reduce the outcome.

The deceleration reading arrived. Minimal. Present.

Friction should have converted velocity to heat.

The spindle’s thermal signature remained constant.

Tiffani wrote: *Thermal properties inconsistent with standard material composition. Recommend material analysis if retrieval becomes possible.*

Retrieval would not be possible.

She pressed her thumb into the chalk callus at the base of her index finger until the dull pain steadied her breathing. Then she returned to the lens and took the next measurement.

Trajectory refinement continued. Entry point stabilized over the eastern ocean, 4,000 spans from the coastal shelf. Transit line predicted mantle passage with exit in the Crown region.

Exit.

She made herself write the word instead of avoiding it.

If the spindle maintained integrity through planetary transit, it would punch completely through Te’Oga and continue into space. The exit wound would be catastrophic even without a direct strike on Saint Merro.

She documented anyway.

*Tide -1, Kindling: Object 720,000 spans. Velocity 8,200 spans per set. Trajectory stable. Atmospheric entry projected Tide 0, Kindling. Planetary transit estimated 84–92 sets depending on mantle density variation.*

*Impact line: Eastern ocean entry, Crown region exit.*

*Saint Merro location: approximately 400 spans from projected exit trajectory.*

Four hundred spans.

Close enough for catastrophic secondary effects. Far enough to avoid direct vaporization.

She filed the report. Transmitted to Guild. Transmitted to Research Office. Transmitted to Regional Emergency Authority.

Acknowledged. Under review. Continue observation.

She understood the logic. She disagreed with the conclusion.

She pulled a fresh slate and began failure probability for Saint Merro’s terrace supports under extreme seismic propagation. The numbers were catastrophic. Terraces were engineered for stability, not for mantle transit shock and axis correction forces.

Lower terraces fail first. Underface districts collapse. Mid-terraces fracture and buckle. Crown anchors might hold.

Might.

She drew progression diagrams. Marked high-risk zones. Calculated evacuation routes that would remain passable during partial collapse.

Then she returned to the lens.

The spindle was still there. Still closing. Still indifferent to protocol.

Two Tides remaining.

Tiffani initiated continuous observation protocol. She would track without interruption until impact. Someone needed to record what happened when a structure this massive moving this fast intersected planetary mass.

Even if no one read the report until after.

She adjusted lens focus. Recorded another measurement. Updated calculations.

Saint Merro’s bells rang phase transitions. People moved through their Tides. Heat-grids hummed at stable frequency. Life proceeded according to normal rules.

For two more Tides.

Then everything would change in ways she could calculate but not prevent.

Tiffani documented that assessment as well.
```

---

# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

## Chapter One: The World Before



The heat-grid risers ran at stable frequency.

Jhace had lived under that hum for three Turns. Consistent pitch. Predictable thermal expansion patterns. The sound told him when a joint seated clean and when stress started to redistribute wrong. It told him when a beam carried load properly and when fracture lines began.

Tonight it told him the forge was running true.

He worked the terrace-side bench alone, fitting collar joints for an Upper Terrace residential grid expansion. Repetitive work. The kind where his hands moved by practiced sequence while his mind stayed quiet.

Position blank. Align seam. Weld bead. Cool. Check penetration. Repeat.

Forty-eight joints total. Contractor needed them before winter phase when Upper Terrace heating demand peaked. Late delivery meant contract penalties. Penalties meant no work next Cast.

Simple rules. Follow them or do not eat.

The weld bead ran clean. He checked heat distribution with his palm held near the metal—not touching, reading the air gradient. Even spread. No hot spots that meant incomplete fusion.

Joint fourteen complete. Thirty-four remaining.

He set it on the cooling rack and reached for blank fifteen.

The workshop door opened. Bram entered carrying a manifest folder and wearing the expression that meant someone else's crisis was becoming Jhace's schedule problem.

"Southslope water reclamation contractor sent a runner." Bram set the folder on the inspection table. "They need eight custom expansion sleeves by end of Anneal."

Jhace looked at his collar joint queue. "I am committed to current contract."

"They are offering premium rate. Forty percent over standard."

"I have delivery deadline in six Tides."

"They have regulatory deadline. If their reclamation system is not operational by next Tide, Waterworks Guild fines them per-Tide until completion." Bram tapped the folder. "They will pay whatever it costs."

Jhace wiped his hands on a rag and walked to the project timeline wall where schedules hung clipped to brass rails. He pulled the collar joint chart. Studied the Tide markers. Ran numbers.

If he worked both contracts in parallel, collar joints finished with two Tides buffer. The sleeves required precision work during late shifts when fatigue made errors more likely. Errors meant scrapped material and wasted Tides.

But forty percent premium meant material costs covered for next Crucible plus reserve funds. Reserve meant not scrambling for contracts when seasonal work contracted.

Risk against stability. Simple calculation.

"Tell them six sleeves delivered end of Anneal," Jhace said. "Remaining two by mid-following Anneal. Timeline is not negotiable."

Bram nodded once and left.

The door closed. The heat-grid hum continued unchanged.

Jhace returned to joint fifteen.

His hands moved through familiar rhythm. Position. Align. Weld. The sequence was mechanical. Reliable. Work that required competence applied consistently, not innovation or inspiration.

He preferred it that way.

He had attempted innovation once. Junction Seven expansion project, three Turns ago. He had flagged a structural risk in the support grid geometry—documented the load path mismatch, submitted the assessment to crew lead. Crew lead filed it as "noted, non-critical" and maintained the construction schedule.

Three Anneals later during stress testing, Junction Seven collapsed. Main support beam failed at the exact connection he had flagged. Twenty-three people died in the cascade failure.

The investigation cleared him. His documentation proved he had identified the risk. He had followed protocol. Done everything correctly by regulatory standard.

Twenty-three people remained dead.

After Junction Seven, he stopped proposing system improvements. He followed specifications. Delivered on schedule. Only flagged risks severe enough to halt work entirely—the kind that would make his non-compliance the liability.

That kept him employed. That kept him from being responsible when systems failed anyway.

Joint eighteen complete. Thirty remaining.

The weld cooled. He racked it and reached for nineteen.

Outside, Saint Merro's terrace bells rang end of Shaping phase. The sound carried through stone and glass, marking transition into Striking when workshops typically closed and people moved to evening routines.

Jhace kept working. He would complete through joint twenty-four tonight. That maintained schedule. Tomorrow he would run material calculations for the expansion sleeves. The Tides would proceed in sequence. Work would complete.

Simple. Systematic. Reliable.

The world made sense when you followed the rules and delivered what you promised.

Heat-grid hum stayed constant at baseline frequency. Weld beads ran true. His hands executed practiced motions without conscious direction.

Joint twenty complete. Twenty-eight remaining.

The forge made sense. Metal behaved predictably under known conditions. Apply heat, pressure, time in correct proportion and joints fused clean. Fail to apply them correctly and joints failed. Cause and effect. No mysteries.

He liked that.

He reached for blank twenty-one and positioned it in the clamp assembly.

---

## Chapter Two: The Calculation



Tiffani adjusted the primary lens array for the sixth time that Watch.

The object had entered long-range observation seven Tides ago—initially logged as trajectory anomaly moving too fast through the orbital approach corridor to match debris classification. She had documented it in her observation record and continued standard measurement protocol.

By Tide two, trajectory had refined. The object was not decelerating. Not altering vector. It was approaching Te'Oga on intercept course with precision that suggested either deliberate targeting or catastrophic coincidence.

She preferred coincidence. Targeting required assumptions about unknown actors with capabilities beyond current engineering framework.

Coincidence only required mathematics.

She had spent five Tides calculating impact vectors. The Observatory lens array tracked objects beyond atmospheric boundary using geometric compensation for planetary curvature—technique refined during her training under Master Kellis. She had improved calculation precision by implementing iterative atmospheric distortion correction.

The spindle was currently 720,000 spans out and closing at velocity that made her throat constrict when she converted values to real-world scale.

Atmospheric boundary contact in two Tides.

Impact in three.

She had filed reports with Astronomical Guild. Sent calculations to Regional Research Office. Documented projected impact coordinates, energy release estimates, probable damage radius patterns.

Responses had been polite acknowledgment and requests for continued observation.

No one believed the numbers.

The spindle was too large. Velocity too high. Trajectory too precise. The energy release on impact would be—

She stopped that calculation path. The result had not changed across fifty-three iterations. Running it again would not make the outcome less catastrophic.

Tiffani returned to the observation bench and pulled her current slate. Tracking data showed minimal deceleration—the object was interacting with Te'Oga's outer atmospheric layers. Friction should be converting velocity to thermal output.

The spindle's surface temperature had not increased.

That violated atmospheric entry physics. The object should be radiating heat as it decelerated through atmospheric resistance. Instead, thermal signature remained constant at ambient solar distance temperature.

She made margin notation: *Thermal properties inconsistent with standard material composition. Material analysis recommended if retrieval becomes possible.*

Retrieval would not be possible.

Impact would vaporize anything within 200 spans of strike coordinates. Shockwave would flatten structures within 2,000 spans. Seismic propagation would trigger failures across entire regional terrace architecture.

She had calculated casualty estimates. Filed them with proper authorities. Completed everything protocol required.

The spindle continued approach.

Tiffani walked to the western observation port and looked across Saint Merro's terrace levels. The city descended in geometric precision from Crown heights through residential and commercial districts down to industrial Underface. Millions of people occupied those terraces. They moved through their Tides following predictable patterns. They trusted the world would continue operating by comprehensible physical laws.

The spindle was going to demonstrate they were wrong.

She returned to the lens array and recorded another measurement. Trajectory had refined further. Projected entry coordinates were shifting—not significantly, but enough to matter for calculating which regions would experience primary impact effects versus secondary structural collapse.

Current projection: atmospheric entry over eastern ocean, 4,000 spans from coastal shelf. Trajectory angled to transit through Te'Oga's mantle and exit somewhere in Crown region.

Exit.

She had been avoiding that calculation.

If the spindle maintained structural integrity through planetary passage, it would punch completely through Te'Oga and continue into space. The exit wound would be—

She stopped. Made herself breathe properly. Returned to documentation.

*Tide -1, Kindling phase: Object 720,000 spans out. Velocity 8,200 spans per set. Trajectory stable. Atmospheric entry projected Tide 0, Kindling phase. Planetary transit duration estimated 84-92 sets depending on mantle density variation.*

*Impact site current projection: Eastern ocean entry, Crown region exit.*

*Saint Merro location: approximately 400 spans from projected exit trajectory.*

Four hundred spans.

Close enough to experience catastrophic secondary effects. Far enough that direct impact would not vaporize the city entirely.

She filed the updated report. Transmitted to Guild. Transmitted to Research Office. Transmitted to Regional Emergency Authority.

Responses would be identical: acknowledged, under review, continue observation.

No one was evacuating millions based on calculations from single observer.

Tiffani understood the institutional logic. She disagreed with the conclusion. But she understood it.

She pulled fresh slate and began calculating structural failure probability for Saint Merro's terrace support architecture under extreme seismic stress. The numbers were catastrophic. Terraces were engineered for stability under normal load conditions, not for shockwave propagation through planetary crust when something punched hole through the mantle.

Lower terraces would fail first. Underface districts would experience immediate collapse. Residential mid-terraces would fracture and buckle. Crown heights might survive if foundation anchors held.

Might.

She documented everything. Drew failure progression diagrams. Marked high-risk zones. Calculated evacuation routes that would remain passable during partial structural collapse.

Then filed everything and returned to the lens.

The spindle was still there. Still approaching. Still on course.

Two Tides remaining.

Tiffani initiated continuous observation protocol. She would track without interruption until impact now. Someone needed to document this. Someone needed to record what occurred when object this massive moving this fast encountered planetary mass.

Even if no one read the report until after.

She adjusted lens focus. Recorded another measurement. Updated calculations.

The world continued around her. Saint Merro's bells rang phase transitions. People moved through Tides. Heat-grids hummed at stable frequency. Life proceeded according to normal rules.

For two more Tides.

Then everything would change in ways she could calculate but not prevent.

Tiffani documented that assessment as well.

Her hands were already raw from chalk dust and repeated slate notation. She had forty-seven Watches of continuous observation remaining before atmospheric entry.

She would complete all of them.

Because stopping felt like abandoning the only thing she still controlled: accurate measurement of approaching catastrophe.

The spindle closed distance at 8,200 spans per set.

Tiffani recorded the value and prepared for the next measurement cycle.

---

## Chapter Three: The Piercing

[Use the corrected version you provided - that's the canonical Chapter 3]

[ASSISTANT]
You’re close. The voice fits, lexicon mostly holds, and both chapters set up the kind of “systems-first” brain that makes Chapter 3 hit harder.

Two real problems to fix before you lock them:

## What’s off (and why it matters)

### 1) **Chloen comes out of nowhere in Chapter 3**
In the canonical Chapter 3, she’s present and gets pinned. In your Chapter 1, she doesn’t exist yet. That makes her feel like a plot tool instead of a living apprentice.

**Fix:** Seed her in Chapter 1 with one clean, low-stakes moment. One line of competence. One line of youth. That’s it.

### 2) **Timeline language in Chapter 2 is internally inconsistent**
You’re mixing “Tide two,” “Tide -1,” “two Tides remaining,” and “Watch counts” like they’re the same system. It reads like the math is drifting even before the spindle shows up.

**Fix:** Use “first/second Tide of observation” (relative) and avoid “Tide -1” entirely. Anchor the projection as:  
- *Entry projected: Tide 0, Kindling*  
- *Transit: 84–92 sets*  
- *Exit: Crown region*  
Everything else can stay relative.

### 3) Minor style rule: **don’t give agency to the object**
“The spindle was going to demonstrate…” is mild anthropomorphizing. Easy to fix.

# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS

## Chapter One: The World Before

The heat-grid risers ran at stable frequency.

Jhace had lived under that hum for three Turns. Consistent pitch. Predictable thermal expansion patterns. The sound told him when a joint seated clean and when stress started to redistribute wrong. It told him when a beam carried load properly and when fracture lines began.

He worked the terrace-side bench in the Underface forge, fitting collar joints for an Upper Terrace residential grid expansion. Repetitive work. The kind where his hands moved by practiced sequence while his mind stayed quiet.

Position blank. Align seam. Weld bead. Cool. Check penetration. Repeat.

Forty-eight joints total. Contractor needed them before cold phase when Upper Terrace heating demand peaked. Late delivery meant contract penalties. Penalties meant no work next Cast.

Simple rules. Follow them or do not eat.

The weld bead ran clean. He checked heat distribution with his palm held near the metal—not touching, reading the air gradient. Even spread. No hot spots that meant incomplete fusion.

Joint fourteen complete. Thirty-four remaining.

He set it on the cooling rack and reached for blank fifteen.

Chloen crossed the work floor with a bundle of cord and a slate board. Apprentice stride. Fast on purpose. She stopped at the quench trough and checked the catch pan without being told. She kept her hands clear of the hot rim. She did not look at Jhace for permission.

Competent. Young. Still learning not to rush.

Jhace did not correct her. He watched once, then went back to the clamp.

The workshop door opened. Bram entered carrying a manifest folder and wearing the expression that meant someone else's crisis was becoming Jhace's schedule problem.

"Southslope water reclamation contractor sent a runner." Bram set the folder on the inspection table. "They need eight custom expansion sleeves by end of Anneal."

Jhace looked at his collar joint queue. "I am committed to current contract."

"They are offering premium rate. Forty percent over standard."

"I have delivery deadline in six Tides."

"They have regulatory deadline. If their reclamation system is not operational by next Tide, Waterworks Guild fines them per-Tide until completion." Bram tapped the folder. "They will pay whatever it costs."

Jhace wiped his hands on a rag and walked to the project timeline wall where schedules hung clipped to brass rails. He pulled the collar joint chart. Studied the Tide markers. Ran numbers.

If he worked both contracts in parallel, collar joints finished with two Tides buffer. The sleeves required precision work during late shifts when fatigue made errors more likely. Errors meant scrapped material and wasted Tides.

But forty percent premium meant material costs covered for next Crucible plus reserve funds. Reserve meant not scrambling for contracts when seasonal work contracted.

Risk against stability. Simple calculation.

"Tell them six sleeves delivered end of Anneal," Jhace said. "Remaining two by mid-following Anneal. Timeline is not negotiable."

Bram nodded once and left.

The door closed. The heat-grid hum continued unchanged.

Jhace returned to joint fifteen.

His hands moved through familiar rhythm. Position. Align. Weld. The sequence was mechanical. Reliable. Work that required competence applied consistently, not invention.

He preferred it that way.

He had attempted invention once. Junction Seven expansion project, three Turns ago. He had flagged a structural risk in the support grid geometry—documented the load path mismatch, submitted the assessment to crew lead. Crew lead filed it as "noted, non-critical" and maintained the construction schedule.

Three Anneals later during stress testing, Junction Seven collapsed. Main support beam failed at the exact connection he had flagged. Twenty-three people died in the cascade failure.

The investigation cleared him. His documentation proved he had identified the risk. He had followed protocol. Done everything correctly by regulatory standard.

Twenty-three people remained dead.

After Junction Seven, he stopped proposing improvements. He followed specifications. Delivered on schedule. Only flagged risks severe enough to halt work entirely—the kind that forced the decision out of his hands.

That kept him employed. That kept him from being the one with a signature next to bodies.

Joint eighteen complete. Thirty remaining.

Outside, Saint Merro's terrace bells rang end of Shaping phase. The sound carried through stone and glass, marking transition into Striking when workshops typically closed and people moved to evening routines.

Jhace kept working.

Chloen moved to the supply rack and started sorting blanks by gauge without being asked. Bram’s training showed in her hands. Not in her confidence yet.

Joint twenty complete. Twenty-eight remaining.

The forge made sense. Metal behaved predictably under known conditions. Apply heat, pressure, time in correct proportion and joints fused clean. Fail to apply them correctly and joints failed.

Cause and effect.

No mysteries.

He reached for blank twenty-one and positioned it in the clamp assembly.
