Junction Fifteen was two cables from the workshop. Jhace walked it in 400 heartbeats. Found a crew standing back from a valve cluster. Steam venting in controlled bursts. Hissing. Pressure release.

One of the crew saw him. Gestured.

“Seal’s gone. We isolated it but we need a new pressure collar fabricated. Standard size-three mount. Can you do it here?”

Jhace looked at the failed valve. At the vent pattern. At the crew keeping safe distance.

“Portable forge?” he asked.

“Got one in the equipment bay. Two cables down.”

“Bring it. I need stock metal. Quarter-bar thickness. Two spans length.”

The crew moved. Jhace set down his kit. Pulled out measuring cord. Checked the valve mount specifications while he waited.

Standard size-three. He’d made dozens. Knew the dimensions without reference. Knew the heat sequence. Knew the joint pattern that prevented premature seal failure.

The portable forge arrived. The stock metal arrived. Jhace set up his work space in the corridor. Lit the forge. Waited for the coals to heat properly.

The crew watched him work. He ignored them. Focused on the metal. On the heat. On the count.

12 heartbeats. 13. 14.

The metal heated to orange. Even distribution. No cold spots.

He pulled it. Started hammering. The corridor rang with the sound. Metal against metal. Forge work in a maintenance tunnel. Emergency fabrication because a seal failed and systems needed repair now, not next Anneal.

This was the work. This was what prevented names on plaques. This was response that mattered more than deadline brackets.

Jhace shaped the collar. Folded the joint. Set the angle. Checked it against his template.

Correct.

He quenched it. Let it cool. Tested the fit on the valve mount.

Solid.

“That’ll hold,” one of the crew said.

Jhace handed them the collar. “Install it properly. Follow torque sequence. Don’t rush the seal compression.”

“Understood.”

The crew moved to install the replacement. Jhace packed his portable forge. Checked the junction one more time. The isolation held. The repair would work. The system would function.

He walked back to the workshop.

Chloen had kept the forge properly banked. The coals glowed red. Ready to work.

Jhace pulled another blank. Set it in the coals. Started counting.

51 brackets by end of Shaping. 52 by end of Striking. 53 by Quench.

The work continued.

The heat-grid hummed.

Normal ended next Tide.

---

**END CHAPTER ONE**

[USER]
# **Chapter Three: The Piercing**

## PART ONE: ENTRY

Tiffani's hands were steady on the theodolite mount.

**Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide two — Kindling phase. Fourteen sets past phase start.**

The object filled her lens field now. Not a distant point. A visible mass. Shape resolving as it closed the final distance.

Spindle. Elongated. Tapered points at both ends. The surface reflected no light. Absorbed everything. A void-shape cutting through star field.

She logged: **Visual confirmation: length approximately 4,400 cables. Diameter at center approximately 670 cables. Surface composition: unknown. Reflectivity: zero.**

The sky deformation had spread. Stars bent in a wide curve along the approach path. The distortion looked wrong. Looked like someone had pulled the sky taut and pressed their thumb into it.

Tiffani's throat was dry. She swallowed. Tasted copper already. Atmospheric contamination beginning.

The object entered upper atmosphere.

No heat bloom. No friction glow. Just passage. Clean. Impossible. The air should have been on fire around it.

She wrote: **Atmospheric entry confirmed. Thermal signature: still absent. Friction effects: none detected. Object maintains structural integrity.**

Pressure changed. Her ears popped. The air felt thinner. Harder to pull into her lungs.

She forced the next breath to complete. Kept her eye to the lens.

The sky tore.

Not metaphor. Actual visible rupture. The atmosphere split along the object's path. A seam opened. Dark line against blue. The edges didn't heal. Didn't close. Just stayed open. Bleeding.

Tiffani's hands shook. She locked them down. Wrote: **Sky seam formation confirmed. Atmospheric wound persistent. Non-closing tear along entry trajectory.**

The object kept descending. Velocity constant. No deceleration. No course change. Just approach.

She checked her clock. Twenty-two sets since Kindling start.

Impact in approximately forty sets.

The pressure kept dropping. Her vision blurred at the edges. She blinked. Forced focus. The lens field stayed clear.

Copper taste intensified. Metallic. Sharp. Coating her tongue and throat. She spat over the platform rail. The saliva came out faintly gold.

Contamination.

She logged it: **Atmospheric particulate detected. Copper-gold coloration. Taste: metallic. Appears in saliva and respiratory system.**

The observatory platform vibrated. Low frequency. Felt through boot soles before heard. The stone trembled.

Tiffani grabbed the theodolite mount. Steadied herself. The vibration increased.

Seismic precursor. Pressure wave traveling through bedrock ahead of impact.

She wrote: **Ground vibration detected. Frequency increasing. Seismic activity commencing.**

The object hit ocean.

She didn't see the impact. Too far. Wrong angle. But she felt it.

The pressure wave arrived first. Slammed into her chest. Knocked breath out. She gasped. Couldn't refill her lungs for three heartbeats.

Then sound.

Not sound. Violence. Acoustic assault that made her jaw ache and her vision shake and her bones rattle inside her skin.

The platform bucked. Stone grinding. Glass cracking somewhere below. The theodolite mount shifted. She grabbed it with both hands. Held on.

The sky seam widened. Spread like a wound tearing further. The darkness inside it pulsed.

Tiffani's hands were shaking now. Not fear. The platform shaking her. The world shaking everything.

She forced her grip to stay locked on the mount. Forced her eye back to the lens.

The object had entered ocean. Started planetary transit.

She checked her clock. Impact confirmed at forty-seven sets past Kindling start.

Transit duration: eighty-four to ninety-two sets estimated.

She had that long to document. That long before exit. That long before Saint Merro found out what four hundred spans from the exit line meant.

The copper taste got worse. She spat again. More gold in the saliva. The particulate was falling. Microscopic. Coating everything.

Tiffani pulled out a clean slate. Set up backup documentation. Primary and secondary recording. Redundancy in case one failed.

The platform kept shaking. The seismic activity wasn't stopping. Wasn't aftershocks. Continuous vibration as the object traveled through planetary mass.

She wrote: **Ocean impact confirmed. Planetary transit commenced. Seismic activity continuous. Atmospheric contamination increasing. Documentation continues.**

Her logbook pages fluttered. Wind rising. Wrong direction. Wrong pressure. Air moving toward the impact site. Being pulled in.

She weighted the pages with her chalk box. Kept writing.

The sky seam pulsed. The edges glowed faintly copper-gold. The wound was bleeding the same contamination the object shed.

Tiffani's hands cramped from gripping the mount. She flexed her fingers. Locked them back down.

Transit had begun.

Saint Merro had eighty-four to ninety-two sets before exit.

She settled in to count them.

---

## PART TWO: TRANSIT

The heat-grid hum changed pitch.

Jhace stopped mid-strike. Hammer raised. The hum that had run constant through every Watch of his eight Castings working this forge was wrong.

Not louder. Not quieter. Different. Harmonic shifted. Secondary vibration riding underneath the baseline.

He set down his hammer. Pressed his palm flat to the workshop floor.

The vibration traveled through stone into his bones. Two frequencies. The normal grid-hum and something else. Something deeper. Something that made his teeth ache.

Chloen looked up from her anvil. "You feel that?"

"Correct." Jhace stood. Moved to the wall. Pressed his ear to the stone.

The doubled harmonic was clearer there. The second pitch carried an edge. Sharp. Wrong.

He pulled back. Looked at the forge. The coals glowed normal. The heat stayed consistent. But the hum underneath everything had changed.

"Probably grid maintenance," Chloen said. "They do valve work sometimes. Changes the resonance."

Jhace's jaw tightened. "Valve work gets scheduled. Gets announced. Gets logged."

"Maybe they—"

The floor lurched.

Not vibration. Movement. Actual displacement. The stone dropped a finger-width and slammed back up.

Jhace's knees flexed. Caught the motion. His hammer fell off the anvil. Hit the floor with a clang that rang wrong in the shifted acoustics.

Chloen grabbed her anvil. Eyes wide. "What was that?"

"Ground-shake." Jhace moved to the doorway. Structural protocol. Stand in the frame. Most stable point during seismic activity.

The floor lurched again. Harder. His shoulder hit the frame. Stone ground against stone somewhere in the walls.

Dust fell from ceiling seams. Thin cascade. Jhace watched it fall through forge light. The pattern was wrong. Falling at an angle. Gravity pulling different.

No. Not gravity. The floor was tilting.

"Chloen. Frame. Now."

She moved. Got to the doorway opposite him. Pressed her back to the frame.

The grinding intensified. Stone on stone. Load shifting. Supports redistributing force they weren't designed to carry.

A crack appeared in the workshop ceiling. Hair-width. Then finger-width. Then hand-width.

Jhace counted. Watched the crack propagate. Followed the stress line across plaster and stone. Tracked where it would fail first.

The eastern support beam. Same failure pattern as Junction Seven.

His chest tightened. Breath stopped.

Not again. Not the same. Not—

The crack reached the beam. Stone fractured. The beam shifted.

"Out." Jhace's voice came flat. "Now. Through the corridor. Not the stairs."

Chloen moved. Fast. Controlled. Good apprentice. Followed direction without questioning.

Jhace grabbed his tool kit. Left the brackets. Left the forge. Followed Chloen into the corridor.

Behind them, the workshop ceiling collapsed.

Stone and plaster and beam supports crashed down onto anvils and cooling racks and fifty-three finished brackets. The sound rolled through the corridor. Dust billowed out the doorway.

Jhace kept moving. Away from the collapse. Toward the main junction. The floor kept lurching. The walls kept grinding.

They reached Junction Fifteen. The valve cluster he'd repaired last Tide was holding. The pressure collar seated properly. No steam leak. Good work under crisis.

A crew huddled near the isolation valves. Same workers who'd called him for the emergency repair. One of them saw Jhace. Relief visible in how their shoulders dropped.

"Workshop collapsed?" the crew chief asked.

"Correct. Eastern beam failed." Jhace set down his kit. Looked at the junction. "Grid status?"

"Pressure's fluctuating. Harmonic's wrong. We've got doubled resonance through the main feed." The chief pointed at a pressure gauge. The needle jumped. Settled. Jumped again. "Never seen it do that."

Jhace pressed his palm to the main pipe. The doubled harmonic vibrated through his hand. Baseline present. Secondary pitch riding over it. The secondary was getting stronger.

"Isolate non-critical branches," he said. "Reduce load on the main feed. We need to see what the baseline does without the full network pulling."

The crew moved. Started closing valve sequences. The grid-hum changed as branches isolated. The baseline stayed. The secondary pitch got louder.

"It's not load-dependent," Jhace said. "It's coming from outside the system."

The floor lurched again. Harder than before. Jhace's knees buckled. He caught himself against the pipe. Hot metal. He pulled back. Palm stinging.

A scream came from deeper in the Underface. High. Sustained. Terror-sound.

Chloen's face went white. "Someone's trapped."

"Correct." Jhace grabbed his kit. Started toward the sound.

The crew chief caught his arm. "Structure's failing. You go in there, you might not come back out."

Jhace looked at the man's hand on his arm. At the grip. At the fear underneath the warning.

"People are trapped," Jhace said. "That means response."

He pulled free. Moved toward the screaming.

The corridor narrowed. Older section. Stone work from before the last expansion. The ceiling was lower here. The supports thinner.

And cracked. Multiple fractures. The stress pattern showed progressive failure. This section was going to collapse.

The screaming came from around the next bend. Jhace moved faster. His kit banged against his leg. Tools rattling.

He turned the corner.

Chloen was pinned.

A beam had fallen. Caught her across the legs. She was on her back. Hands pushing against the beam. Trying to lift. Couldn't. Too heavy. Too much weight.

Blood on the floor. Coming from under the beam. Her blood.

Jhace's throat closed. His vision tunneled. Junction Seven. Twenty-three names. Bodies pulled from rubble. The exact moment when filing reports stopped mattering because the failure had already happened.

"Jhace!" Chloen's voice cracked. "It's crushing. I can't—I can't feel my legs."

He dropped his kit. Moved to the beam. Set his hands on it. Tried to lift.

The beam didn't move. Thousands of weight. More than muscle could shift.

"We need leverage," he said. Voice flat. Automatic. "I'll get—"

"No time." Chloen grabbed his wrist. Her grip shook. "The ceiling's going. Look."

He looked up. New cracks forming. Spreading. The supports were failing in sequence. This section had maybe forty heartbeats before total collapse.

Not enough time to get equipment. Not enough time to organize a crew. Not enough time to do this properly.

Just enough time to watch her die.

No.

Jhace set both hands on the beam. Copper-hot metal. Coated in dust. The copper-gold particulate that had been falling since—since when? He didn't know. Didn't matter.

His hands were coated in it too. Dust and sweat mixing. The residue stuck to his palms. Made the beam slippery.

He pressed harder. Felt the heat. Felt the weight. Felt the absolute certainty that this beam would not move and Chloen would die and he would file another report that cleared him legally while twenty-four names went on a plaque.

*Hold. Just hold long enough.*

The thought arrived clear. Not planning. Not calculation. Just intent. Desperate. Compressed.

Something in his chest answered.

Heat built behind his sternum. Pressure. Gathering. Responding.

Copper-gold light threaded from his palms into the beam.

Jhace's breath stopped. The light was visible. Real. Coming from his hands. Following the stress lines in the metal. Wrapping around fractures. Finding weak points.

The beam shifted.

One finger-width. Then two. Then a hand-width.

Chloen pulled her legs free. Rolled. Kept rolling until she was clear.

The light cut off.

Jhace staggered back. His hands started shaking. Not the ground-shake. Not fear. Tremor inside the joints. Independent vibration that didn't match external movement.

His palms showed burns. Contact burns from copper-hot metal. The skin blistered. But underneath the burns, something else. Faint copper-gold glow. Traces in his skin. In his bones.

What did he just—

The ceiling collapsed.

Jhace grabbed Chloen. Pulled her further down the corridor. Stone crashed behind them. Dust billowed. The beam he'd moved disappeared under rubble.

They kept moving. Away from the collapse. Toward the main junction. Jhace's hands wouldn't stop shaking. The tremor was getting worse. Spreading up his arms.

Chloen limped. Blood trail behind her. Legs damaged but functional. She was alive.

He'd saved her.

With light. With impossible light from his hands that moved metal and left tremor and felt like something inside him had broken and couldn't be fixed.

They reached Junction Fifteen. The crew saw them. Saw Chloen's legs. Saw Jhace's shaking hands.

"Medical," the crew chief said. Started organizing evacuation.

Jhace looked at his hands. At the tremor. At the faint glow under his burned skin.

The doubled harmonic screamed through the grid. The floor kept lurching. The Underface kept collapsing.

And something had changed. Something fundamental. Something that started in his chest and came out as light and left him marked.

He didn't know what he'd done.

He knew it wasn't reversible.

His hands kept shaking.

---

## PART THREE: EXIT

**Eighty-seven sets after ocean entry.**

Tiffani's eyes burned. She'd been at the lens for the full transit. Documenting. Measuring. Counting.

The seismic activity had been continuous. The platform shook. Glass cracked in the observatory dome. But the structure held.

She'd logged it all.

Now the exit was coming.

She checked her projected coordinates. Crown region. Northeastern hemisphere. Four hundred spans from Saint Merro.

Close enough.

The ground vibration changed. Intensified. Different frequency. Sharper. The transit was ending. The object was approaching the far side of planetary mass.

Tiffani grabbed the theodolite mount. Locked her grip.

The object breached.

She didn't see it directly. Wrong angle. But she saw the effect.

The sky erupted.

Light. Thermal bloom. Massive energy release as the object exited into atmosphere. The horizon went white. Too bright to look at directly.

Tiffani turned her head. Squeezed her eyes shut. The light burned through her eyelids. Red. Orange. White.

Heat arrived.

Transmitted through stone itself. The platform went from cool to warm to hot in heartbeats. The air shimmered. Her lungs felt scorched with each breath.

Pressure slammed her.

Wall of force. Acoustic and kinetic. Her ribs compressed. Breath knocked out. She gasped. Couldn't refill. The pressure held.

Then released.

She sucked air. Tasted copper-gold. The contamination was everywhere now. Coating her mouth. Her lungs. Her skin.

The platform lurched.

Not vibration. Gravity changed. Direction-of-down shifted by fractions. Her boots lost contact with stone. Stomach lifted. Weight disappeared.

She counted heartbeats while floating. One. Two. Three. Four.

Gravity returned.

Hard. Wrong angle. The platform wasn't level anymore. Had tilted during the weightless moment. Now down was slightly different.

Tiffani's knees buckled. She caught herself on the mount. Her ankle twisted. Pain shot up her leg.

She ignored it. Opened her eyes. Looked toward the horizon.

The thermal bloom was fading. Residual glow. The object had exited. Was continuing into space. Leaving.

Leaving destruction behind.

The sky seam was massive now. Visible scar running from ocean entry point to Crown exit point. The wound didn't close. Wouldn't close. Permanent atmospheric damage.

And the dust.

Copper-gold particulate falling like snow. Coating everything. The platform. The theodolite. Her hands. Her face.

She spat. The saliva was pure gold now.

The observatory dome cracked.

Stress fracture. Radial pattern. The glass had survived the pressure wave but couldn't handle the thermal stress and gravity shift combined.

Tiffani watched the crack propagate. Calculated failure point. Estimated collapse window.

Thirty heartbeats. Maybe forty.

She grabbed her logbooks. Primary and backup. Shoved them in her documentation case.

The crack reached the dome's crown. The glass shattered.

Fragments fell. Tiffani covered her head. Glass rained down. Sharp edges. Cutting. She felt impacts on her shoulders. Her arms. Something sliced her cheek.

A larger piece hit the platform. Shattered. Sent shrapnel sideways.

Tiffani's leg exploded with pain.

She looked down. Glass shard embedded in her calf. Blood spreading. Red mixing with copper-gold dust. The combination looked wrong. Metallic.

The platform tilted further. The support structure was failing. The gravity shift had changed the load distribution. The mounts couldn't handle the altered stress pattern.

She needed to move. Needed to get off the platform before it collapsed entirely.

Her leg wouldn't hold weight. The glass shard was deep. Major bleeding.

She tried anyway. Put weight on it. The leg buckled. Pain made her vision white out.

She dropped. Caught herself with her hands. Copper-gold dust ground into her palms.

The platform groaned. Metal on stone. Supports giving way.

Tiffani pulled herself forward. Crawling. Documentation case dragged behind her. Each movement sent pain through her leg. She kept moving.

The platform edge was ten spans away. Then eight. Then five.

A support mount snapped.

The platform tilted steeper. Tiffani slid. Grabbed the theodolite mount. Held on.

The metal frame landed on her legs.

Both of them this time. Crushing. Weight she couldn't shift.

She tried to push it off. Her arms shook. The frame didn't move.

Trapped.

Tiffani pressed her forehead to the platform. Breathed. Counted.

Her logbooks were in reach. She pulled the primary. Opened it. Blood on her fingers. Copper-gold dust everywhere.

She wrote: **Exit event confirmed. Thermal bloom massive. Gravity shift detected. Axial tilt change probable. Observatory structural failure in progress. Legs pinned. Extraction unlikely.**

Her handwriting was shaking. Pain or shock. Didn't matter which.

She wrote: **Documentation complete. Transit time: 87 sets. Saint Merro distance from exit: sufficient for structural cascade. Casualties: catastrophic estimated.**

Below them, Saint Merro was breaking. She could hear it. Distant. Continuous. The sound of terrace systems failing in sequence.

She'd been right. Four hundred spans wasn't safe. Four hundred spans was just far enough to die slower.

Tiffani kept writing. Logged the thermal bloom duration. Logged the pressure wave magnitude. Logged the gravity shift characteristics.

Someone would find these logbooks. Someone would read them. Someone would know she'd calculated correctly and filed reports and continued observation through the full event.

Someone would know she'd done the work.

The platform shook. Another support failing. The angle got steeper.

Her leg screamed. The frame was crushing. She felt bone grinding. Felt something inside break.

She wrote: **Personal status: legs crushed. Blood loss significant. Consciousness unstable.**

Her vision blurred. She blinked. Forced focus. Finished the entry.

Then set down her chalk. Closed the logbook. Made sure it was secure in the case.

Documentation complete.

Tiffani laid her head on the tilted platform. Tasted copper-gold. Felt pain and heat and the wrongness of gravity.

Counted her heartbeats.

Waited.

---

Jhace emerged from the Underface into chaos.

Mid-terrace was collapsing. Buildings leaning. Streets cracked. People running. Screaming. No organization. Just panic.

Chloen was with the evacuation crew. Getting medical attention. Alive.

His hands wouldn't stop shaking.

The copper-gold dust fell like snow. Coated everything. Made the air taste metal. Made breathing hurt.

The grid-hum had stopped entirely. System failure. Network collapse. Saint Merro's infrastructure was dead.

Jhace walked through the streets. His tool kit on his shoulder. His hands shaking. The tremor had spread to his arms now. Constant vibration. Independent of external movement.

Cost.

He'd manifested something. Light. Force. Whatever that was. And it cost him. His hands. His motor control. Something fundamental in how his body functioned.

Permanent.

He knew it was permanent the same way he knew metal stress patterns. The change was structural. Not temporary damage. Not recoverable function.

He'd saved Chloen. Cost: his hands would shake forever.

The mathematics were simple. One life. One disability. The exchange was logged.

Jhace looked at his shaking hands. Turned them over. The burns showed. The faint copper-gold glow was fading but the tremor stayed.

He'd done something impossible. Moved weight muscle couldn't move. Generated light from intent. Changed reality through will.

And couldn't do it again. Didn't know how he'd done it the first time. Didn't know what he'd activated. Didn't know how to control it.

Just knew it had cost him. Cost visible. Cost permanent. Cost he'd carry every heartbeat for the rest of his life.

Around him, Saint Merro kept breaking. Buildings falling. People dying. The catastrophe Tiffani had calculated. The disaster no one prevented because reports sat in bins while the threat approached.

Jhace started walking toward Crown heights. Toward where the exit had been. Toward the epicenter.

His hands shook. The copper-gold dust fell. The world had broken and stayed broken.

And something inside him had changed. Something that glowed and cost and left marks.

He didn't know what came next.

He knew counting heartbeats wouldn't be enough anymore.

---

**END CHAPTER THREE**

[ASSISTANT]
# **Chapter Two: Eight Tides**

Tiffani adjusted the theodolite’s rear foot until the bubble centered. Checked the other three. All seated. She wiped glass dust off her fingertips and opened her logbook to a fresh page.

Eighth Tide of observation.

The object held position in her lens field. Still there. Still approaching. Still impossible.

She wrote the header: **Target designation: Unknown-One. Observation sequence continues.**

Her hands were raw. Chalk residue dried the skin. Small cuts from lens edge work decorated her knuckles. She’d stopped bandaging them after the third Tide. The work mattered more than comfort.

The theodolite sight aligned on the object. She took the measurement. Logged the angle. Compared it to yesterday’s reading.

Offset confirmed. Movement consistent with projected trajectory.

She pulled out her calculation slate. Drew the curve. Extended the line. The mathematics held clean. Velocity constant. Path unwavering. No course correction. No deceleration.

Whatever this object was, it wasn’t stopping.

She measured again. Double-checked the reference stars. Vela-Nine stable. Corros-Twelve stable. Control points confirmed instrument accuracy.

The object kept approaching.

Tiffani wrote: **Velocity: 8,200 spans per set. Thermal signature: absent. No heat bloom detected. No combustion trail. No atmospheric interaction visible at current distance.**

That shouldn’t be possible. Objects moving at this velocity should show heat. Should show friction effects when they hit atmosphere. Should show something.

This showed nothing. Just movement. Clean. Precise. Wrong.

She recorded the sky deformation. A narrow distortion formed along the projected path. Barely visible. Measurable only because she’d been watching the same star field for eight Tides straight.

The stars bent. Not much. Fraction of a span displacement. But stars didn’t bend. Stars held position. Stars were fixed reference points.

Except now they weren’t.

She wrote: **Sky deformation detected along projected trajectory. Displacement magnitude increasing. Pattern suggests object mass sufficient to warp local geometry during approach.**

Her throat tightened. She forced the next breath to complete.

This wasn’t meteor behavior. Wasn’t comet behavior. Wasn’t anything in her manuals.

She calculated the entry coordinates. Drew the angle of approach. Projected the impact point.

Open ocean beyond the shelfline. Seaward of Saint Merro. Hundreds of cables from the nearest terrace city.

Relief tried to surface. She pressed it down with calculation.

Entry point determined. But objects this massive didn’t just impact and stop. They had exit vectors. They had force propagation. They had secondary effects.

She ran the numbers again.

If the object maintained current velocity through planetary mass… if it retained structural integrity during passage… if it didn’t fragment or decelerate significantly…

Exit point: Crown region.

Distance from Saint Merro to projected exit line: four hundred spans.

Her jaw locked. She recalculated. Got the same result. Ran it a third time with different geometric assumptions.

Four hundred spans. Maybe four hundred twenty. The uncertainty margin didn’t matter. Saint Merro sat close enough to the exit trajectory that secondary effects would propagate directly through the terrace foundation structure.

Not vaporized. Structurally compromised.

She wrote: **Saint Merro assessment: outside primary impact zone. Within secondary effect radius. Seismic propagation through terrace support systems probable. Structural failure cascade likely.**

Her hands pressed too hard. The chalk snapped.

She set down the broken piece. Pulled a fresh length from her kit. Kept writing.

She’d filed reports. Three of them. One to the Astronomical Guild. One to the Regional Research Office. One to the Regional Emergency Authority.

All stamped. All acknowledged. All requesting continued observation and trajectory refinement.

None ordering evacuation.

The Emergency Authority response sat in her pocket. She pulled it out. Read it again.

*Your calculations are noted. Please continue observation and provide updated trajectory data as available. Regional coordination requires confirmed threat assessment before evacuation protocols activate. Maintain standard reporting schedule.*

Standard reporting schedule. While an object the size of a small mountain approached at velocities that shouldn’t exist.

She folded the response. Put it back in her pocket. Returned to the theodolite.

The object was still there. Still approaching. Still not stopping.

She measured the sky deformation again. The bend had increased. Stars displaced by measurable fractions. The distortion spread wider along the approach path.

Something was tearing. Not the stars. The space between them.

Tiffani logged it. Drew the deformation pattern. Calculated the expansion rate.

By the time the object reached atmosphere, the sky would show a visible wound.

She wrote: **Projected atmospheric entry: Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide two — Kindling phase. Entry window: fourteen to eighteen sets past Kindling start. Transit duration through planetary mass: estimated eighty-four to ninety-two sets. Exit event follows.**

There. Locked. Dated. Inevitable.

She checked her lens array. Six lenses ground to specification. Each one calibrated for different observation needs. She’d prepared them over the last Anneal. Ground the curves herself because the guild lenses weren’t precise enough for the measurements she needed.

Her fingertips bled when she finished the grinding work. Glass dust embedded in the cuts. She washed them. Ground more lenses. The precision mattered more than her hands.

She looked through the observation lens again. The object filled more of the field than it had this same phase yesterday. Approach confirmed. Acceleration constant.

No heat. No trail. No visible combustion.

Just approach.

Tiffani pulled out her district map. Marked Saint Merro’s position. Drew the projected exit line. Calculated the angle of force propagation through terrace structure.

Lower levels would fail first. Underface districts. Then mid-terrace. Crown heights might hold if the foundation anchors survived the initial shear stress.

Might.

She wrote: **Underface collapse: high probability. Mid-terrace structural compromise: confirmed. Crown heights survival: dependent on foundation anchor integrity and seismic force magnitude.**

The mathematics didn’t lie. The institutions did. Or they just didn’t believe the mathematics mattered enough to act.

She filed the eighth report. Walked it to the guild messenger station herself. Watched them stamp it. Watched them file it with the others.

“Still tracking that object?” the clerk asked.

“Correct.”

“Anything changed?”

“It’s closer.”

The clerk nodded. Filed the report. Went back to processing standard observational data.

Tiffani returned to the observatory.

The object was still there. Still approaching.

She logged the Tide end: **Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide one — Quench.**

Next Tide was Tide two. Kindling phase. Entry window.

She prepared her observation array. Checked every lens. Verified every measurement tool. Set backup slates in case primary documentation failed.

She would observe through entry. Through transit. Through exit.

Someone needed to document what happened. Someone needed to record the data. Someone needed to count the cost.

Her hands were raw from glass work. Her eyes strained from continuous observation. Her throat was dry from not drinking enough water because leaving the observatory meant missing measurements.

None of that mattered.

The object was coming.

Saint Merro sat four hundred spans from the exit line.

Far enough to avoid vaporization.

Close enough to die anyway.

Tiffani returned to the theodolite. Took another measurement. Logged it.

The object kept approaching.

The sky kept bending.

The reports kept going into bins where no one read them fast enough to matter.

She pulled her observation chair to the theodolite mount. Sat down. Set her logbook on her lap.

Next Tide, the object would enter atmosphere.

Next Tide, her calculations would become facts.

Next Tide, she would document exactly how right she’d been and exactly how wrong “safe” turned out to be.

She wrote one more line: **Observation continues through entry event. Documentation priority: absolute.**

Then she settled in to wait.

Behind her, Saint Merro went about its normal Quench routines. Workers ending their Watches. Families preparing for rest cycle. Guilds processing their daily quotas.

Four hundred spans from where the world was about to break.

Close enough.

---

**END CHAPTER TWO**

[USER]
# **Chapter Three: The Piercing**

## PART ONE: ENTRY

Tiffani's hands were steady on the theodolite mount.

**Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide two — Kindling phase. Fourteen sets past phase start.**

The object filled her lens field now. Not a distant point. A visible mass. Shape resolving as it closed the final distance.

Spindle. Elongated. Tapered points at both ends. The surface reflected no light. Absorbed everything. A void-shape cutting through star field.

She logged: **Visual confirmation: length approximately 4,400 cables. Diameter at center approximately 670 cables. Surface composition: unknown. Reflectivity: zero.**

The sky deformation had spread. Stars bent in a wide curve along the approach path. The distortion looked wrong. Looked like someone had pulled the sky taut and pressed their thumb into it.

Tiffani's throat was dry. She swallowed. Tasted copper already. Atmospheric contamination beginning.

The object entered upper atmosphere.

No heat bloom. No friction glow. Just passage. Clean. Impossible. The air should have been on fire around it.

She wrote: **Atmospheric entry confirmed. Thermal signature: still absent. Friction effects: none detected. Object maintains structural integrity.**

Pressure changed. Her ears popped. The air felt thinner. Harder to pull into her lungs.

She forced the next breath to complete. Kept her eye to the lens.

The sky tore.

Not metaphor. Actual visible rupture. The atmosphere split along the object's path. A seam opened. Dark line against blue. The edges didn't heal. Didn't close. Just stayed open. Bleeding.

Tiffani's hands shook. She locked them down. Wrote: **Sky seam formation confirmed. Atmospheric wound persistent. Non-closing tear along entry trajectory.**

The object kept descending. Velocity constant. No deceleration. No course change. Just approach.

She checked her clock. Twenty-two sets since Kindling start.

Impact in approximately forty sets.

The pressure kept dropping. Her vision blurred at the edges. She blinked. Forced focus. The lens field stayed clear.

Copper taste intensified. Metallic. Sharp. Coating her tongue and throat. She spat over the platform rail. The saliva came out faintly gold.

Contamination.

She logged it: **Atmospheric particulate detected. Copper-gold coloration. Taste: metallic. Appears in saliva and respiratory system.**

The observatory platform vibrated. Low frequency. Felt through boot soles before heard. The stone trembled.

Tiffani grabbed the theodolite mount. Steadied herself. The vibration increased.

Seismic precursor. Pressure wave traveling through bedrock ahead of impact.

She wrote: **Ground vibration detected. Frequency increasing. Seismic activity commencing.**

The object hit ocean.

She didn't see the impact. Too far. Wrong angle. But she felt it.

The pressure wave arrived first. Slammed into her chest. Knocked breath out. She gasped. Couldn't refill her lungs for three heartbeats.

Then sound.

Not sound. Violence. Acoustic assault that made her jaw ache and her vision shake and her bones rattle inside her skin.

The platform bucked. Stone grinding. Glass cracking somewhere below. The theodolite mount shifted. She grabbed it with both hands. Held on.

The sky seam widened. Spread like a wound tearing further. The darkness inside it pulsed.

Tiffani's hands were shaking now. Not fear. The platform shaking her. The world shaking everything.

She forced her grip to stay locked on the mount. Forced her eye back to the lens.

The object had entered ocean. Started planetary transit.

She checked her clock. Impact confirmed at forty-seven sets past Kindling start.

Transit duration: eighty-four to ninety-two sets estimated.

She had that long to document. That long before exit. That long before Saint Merro found out what four hundred spans from the exit line meant.

The copper taste got worse. She spat again. More gold in the saliva. The particulate was falling. Microscopic. Coating everything.

Tiffani pulled out a clean slate. Set up backup documentation. Primary and secondary recording. Redundancy in case one failed.

The platform kept shaking. The seismic activity wasn't stopping. Wasn't aftershocks. Continuous vibration as the object traveled through planetary mass.

She wrote: **Ocean impact confirmed. Planetary transit commenced. Seismic activity continuous. Atmospheric contamination increasing. Documentation continues.**

Her logbook pages fluttered. Wind rising. Wrong direction. Wrong pressure. Air moving toward the impact site. Being pulled in.

She weighted the pages with her chalk box. Kept writing.

The sky seam pulsed. The edges glowed faintly copper-gold. The wound was bleeding the same contamination the object shed.

Tiffani's hands cramped from gripping the mount. She flexed her fingers. Locked them back down.

Transit had begun.

Saint Merro had eighty-four to ninety-two sets before exit.

She settled in to count them.

---

## PART TWO: TRANSIT

The heat-grid hum changed pitch.

Jhace stopped mid-strike. Hammer raised. The hum that had run constant through every Watch of his eight Castings working this forge was wrong.

Not louder. Not quieter. Different. Harmonic shifted. Secondary vibration riding underneath the baseline.

He set down his hammer. Pressed his palm flat to the workshop floor.

The vibration traveled through stone into his bones. Two frequencies. The normal grid-hum and something else. Something deeper. Something that made his teeth ache.

Chloen looked up from her anvil. "You feel that?"

"Correct." Jhace stood. Moved to the wall. Pressed his ear to the stone.

The doubled harmonic was clearer there. The second pitch carried an edge. Sharp. Wrong.

He pulled back. Looked at the forge. The coals glowed normal. The heat stayed consistent. But the hum underneath everything had changed.

"Probably grid maintenance," Chloen said. "They do valve work sometimes. Changes the resonance."

Jhace's jaw tightened. "Valve work gets scheduled. Gets announced. Gets logged."

"Maybe they—"

The floor lurched.

Not vibration. Movement. Actual displacement. The stone dropped a finger-width and slammed back up.

Jhace's knees flexed. Caught the motion. His hammer fell off the anvil. Hit the floor with a clang that rang wrong in the shifted acoustics.

Chloen grabbed her anvil. Eyes wide. "What was that?"

"Ground-shake." Jhace moved to the doorway. Structural protocol. Stand in the frame. Most stable point during seismic activity.

The floor lurched again. Harder. His shoulder hit the frame. Stone ground against stone somewhere in the walls.

Dust fell from ceiling seams. Thin cascade. Jhace watched it fall through forge light. The pattern was wrong. Falling at an angle. Gravity pulling different.

No. Not gravity. The floor was tilting.

"Chloen. Frame. Now."

She moved. Got to the doorway opposite him. Pressed her back to the frame.

The grinding intensified. Stone on stone. Load shifting. Supports redistributing force they weren't designed to carry.

A crack appeared in the workshop ceiling. Hair-width. Then finger-width. Then hand-width.

Jhace counted. Watched the crack propagate. Followed the stress line across plaster and stone. Tracked where it would fail first.

The eastern support beam. Same failure pattern as Junction Seven.

His chest tightened. Breath stopped.

Not again. Not the same. Not—

The crack reached the beam. Stone fractured. The beam shifted.

"Out." Jhace's voice came flat. "Now. Through the corridor. Not the stairs."

Chloen moved. Fast. Controlled. Good apprentice. Followed direction without questioning.

Jhace grabbed his tool kit. Left the brackets. Left the forge. Followed Chloen into the corridor.

Behind them, the workshop ceiling collapsed.

Stone and plaster and beam supports crashed down onto anvils and cooling racks and fifty-three finished brackets. The sound rolled through the corridor. Dust billowed out the doorway.

Jhace kept moving. Away from the collapse. Toward the main junction. The floor kept lurching. The walls kept grinding.

They reached Junction Fifteen. The valve cluster he'd repaired last Tide was holding. The pressure collar seated properly. No steam leak. Good work under crisis.

A crew huddled near the isolation valves. Same workers who'd called him for the emergency repair. One of them saw Jhace. Relief visible in how their shoulders dropped.

"Workshop collapsed?" the crew chief asked.

"Correct. Eastern beam failed." Jhace set down his kit. Looked at the junction. "Grid status?"

"Pressure's fluctuating. Harmonic's wrong. We've got doubled resonance through the main feed." The chief pointed at a pressure gauge. The needle jumped. Settled. Jumped again. "Never seen it do that."

Jhace pressed his palm to the main pipe. The doubled harmonic vibrated through his hand. Baseline present. Secondary pitch riding over it. The secondary was getting stronger.

"Isolate non-critical branches," he said. "Reduce load on the main feed. We need to see what the baseline does without the full network pulling."

The crew moved. Started closing valve sequences. The grid-hum changed as branches isolated. The baseline stayed. The secondary pitch got louder.

"It's not load-dependent," Jhace said. "It's coming from outside the system."

The floor lurched again. Harder than before. Jhace's knees buckled. He caught himself against the pipe. Hot metal. He pulled back. Palm stinging.

A scream came from deeper in the Underface. High. Sustained. Terror-sound.

Chloen's face went white. "Someone's trapped."

"Correct." Jhace grabbed his kit. Started toward the sound.

The crew chief caught his arm. "Structure's failing. You go in there, you might not come back out."

Jhace looked at the man's hand on his arm. At the grip. At the fear underneath the warning.

"People are trapped," Jhace said. "That means response."

He pulled free. Moved toward the screaming.

The corridor narrowed. Older section. Stone work from before the last expansion. The ceiling was lower here. The supports thinner.

And cracked. Multiple fractures. The stress pattern showed progressive failure. This section was going to collapse.

The screaming came from around the next bend. Jhace moved faster. His kit banged against his leg. Tools rattling.

He turned the corner.

Chloen was pinned.

A beam had fallen. Caught her across the legs. She was on her back. Hands pushing against the beam. Trying to lift. Couldn't. Too heavy. Too much weight.

Blood on the floor. Coming from under the beam. Her blood.

Jhace's throat closed. His vision tunneled. Junction Seven. Twenty-three names. Bodies pulled from rubble. The exact moment when filing reports stopped mattering because the failure had already happened.

"Jhace!" Chloen's voice cracked. "It's crushing. I can't—I can't feel my legs."

He dropped his kit. Moved to the beam. Set his hands on it. Tried to lift.

The beam didn't move. Thousands of weight. More than muscle could shift.

"We need leverage," he said. Voice flat. Automatic. "I'll get—"

"No time." Chloen grabbed his wrist. Her grip shook. "The ceiling's going. Look."

He looked up. New cracks forming. Spreading. The supports were failing in sequence. This section had maybe forty heartbeats before total collapse.

Not enough time to get equipment. Not enough time to organize a crew. Not enough time to do this properly.

Just enough time to watch her die.

No.

Jhace set both hands on the beam. Copper-hot metal. Coated in dust. The copper-gold particulate that had been falling since—since when? He didn't know. Didn't matter.

His hands were coated in it too. Dust and sweat mixing. The residue stuck to his palms. Made the beam slippery.

He pressed harder. Felt the heat. Felt the weight. Felt the absolute certainty that this beam would not move and Chloen would die and he would file another report that cleared him legally while twenty-four names went on a plaque.

*Hold. Just hold long enough.*

The thought arrived clear. Not planning. Not calculation. Just intent. Desperate. Compressed.

Something in his chest answered.

Heat built behind his sternum. Pressure. Gathering. Responding.

Copper-gold light threaded from his palms into the beam.

Jhace's breath stopped. The light was visible. Real. Coming from his hands. Following the stress lines in the metal. Wrapping around fractures. Finding weak points.

The beam shifted.

One finger-width. Then two. Then a hand-width.

Chloen pulled her legs free. Rolled. Kept rolling until she was clear.

The light cut off.

Jhace staggered back. His hands started shaking. Not the ground-shake. Not fear. Tremor inside the joints. Independent vibration that didn't match external movement.

His palms showed burns. Contact burns from copper-hot metal. The skin blistered. But underneath the burns, something else. Faint copper-gold glow. Traces in his skin. In his bones.

What did he just—

The ceiling collapsed.

Jhace grabbed Chloen. Pulled her further down the corridor. Stone crashed behind them. Dust billowed. The beam he'd moved disappeared under rubble.

They kept moving. Away from the collapse. Toward the main junction. Jhace's hands wouldn't stop shaking. The tremor was getting worse. Spreading up his arms.

Chloen limped. Blood trail behind her. Legs damaged but functional. She was alive.

He'd saved her.

With light. With impossible light from his hands that moved metal and left tremor and felt like something inside him had broken and couldn't be fixed.

They reached Junction Fifteen. The crew saw them. Saw Chloen's legs. Saw Jhace's shaking hands.

"Medical," the crew chief said. Started organizing evacuation.

Jhace looked at his hands. At the tremor. At the faint glow under his burned skin.

The doubled harmonic screamed through the grid. The floor kept lurching. The Underface kept collapsing.

And something had changed. Something fundamental. Something that started in his chest and came out as light and left him marked.

He didn't know what he'd done.

He knew it wasn't reversible.

His hands kept shaking.

---

## PART THREE: EXIT

**Eighty-seven sets after ocean entry.**

Tiffani's eyes burned. She'd been at the lens for the full transit. Documenting. Measuring. Counting.

The seismic activity had been continuous. The platform shook. Glass cracked in the observatory dome. But the structure held.

She'd logged it all.

Now the exit was coming.

She checked her projected coordinates. Crown region. Northeastern hemisphere. Four hundred spans from Saint Merro.

Close enough.

The ground vibration changed. Intensified. Different frequency. Sharper. The transit was ending. The object was approaching the far side of planetary mass.

Tiffani grabbed the theodolite mount. Locked her grip.

The object breached.

She didn't see it directly. Wrong angle. But she saw the effect.

The sky erupted.

Light. Thermal bloom. Massive energy release as the object exited into atmosphere. The horizon went white. Too bright to look at directly.

Tiffani turned her head. Squeezed her eyes shut. The light burned through her eyelids. Red. Orange. White.

Heat arrived.

Transmitted through stone itself. The platform went from cool to warm to hot in heartbeats. The air shimmered. Her lungs felt scorched with each breath.

Pressure slammed her.

Wall of force. Acoustic and kinetic. Her ribs compressed. Breath knocked out. She gasped. Couldn't refill. The pressure held.

Then released.

She sucked air. Tasted copper-gold. The contamination was everywhere now. Coating her mouth. Her lungs. Her skin.

The platform lurched.

Not vibration. Gravity changed. Direction-of-down shifted by fractions. Her boots lost contact with stone. Stomach lifted. Weight disappeared.

She counted heartbeats while floating. One. Two. Three. Four.

Gravity returned.

Hard. Wrong angle. The platform wasn't level anymore. Had tilted during the weightless moment. Now down was slightly different.

Tiffani's knees buckled. She caught herself on the mount. Her ankle twisted. Pain shot up her leg.

She ignored it. Opened her eyes. Looked toward the horizon.

The thermal bloom was fading. Residual glow. The object had exited. Was continuing into space. Leaving.

Leaving destruction behind.

The sky seam was massive now. Visible scar running from ocean entry point to Crown exit point. The wound didn't close. Wouldn't close. Permanent atmospheric damage.

And the dust.

Copper-gold particulate falling like snow. Coating everything. The platform. The theodolite. Her hands. Her face.

She spat. The saliva was pure gold now.

The observatory dome cracked.

Stress fracture. Radial pattern. The glass had survived the pressure wave but couldn't handle the thermal stress and gravity shift combined.

Tiffani watched the crack propagate. Calculated failure point. Estimated collapse window.

Thirty heartbeats. Maybe forty.

She grabbed her logbooks. Primary and backup. Shoved them in her documentation case.

The crack reached the dome's crown. The glass shattered.

Fragments fell. Tiffani covered her head. Glass rained down. Sharp edges. Cutting. She felt impacts on her shoulders. Her arms. Something sliced her cheek.

A larger piece hit the platform. Shattered. Sent shrapnel sideways.

Tiffani's leg exploded with pain.

She looked down. Glass shard embedded in her calf. Blood spreading. Red mixing with copper-gold dust. The combination looked wrong. Metallic.

The platform tilted further. The support structure was failing. The gravity shift had changed the load distribution. The mounts couldn't handle the altered stress pattern.

She needed to move. Needed to get off the platform before it collapsed entirely.

Her leg wouldn't hold weight. The glass shard was deep. Major bleeding.

She tried anyway. Put weight on it. The leg buckled. Pain made her vision white out.

She dropped. Caught herself with her hands. Copper-gold dust ground into her palms.

The platform groaned. Metal on stone. Supports giving way.

Tiffani pulled herself forward. Crawling. Documentation case dragged behind her. Each movement sent pain through her leg. She kept moving.

The platform edge was ten spans away. Then eight. Then five.

A support mount snapped.

The platform tilted steeper. Tiffani slid. Grabbed the theodolite mount. Held on.

The metal frame landed on her legs.

Both of them this time. Crushing. Weight she couldn't shift.

She tried to push it off. Her arms shook. The frame didn't move.

Trapped.

Tiffani pressed her forehead to the platform. Breathed. Counted.

Her logbooks were in reach. She pulled the primary. Opened it. Blood on her fingers. Copper-gold dust everywhere.

She wrote: **Exit event confirmed. Thermal bloom massive. Gravity shift detected. Axial tilt change probable. Observatory structural failure in progress. Legs pinned. Extraction unlikely.**

Her handwriting was shaking. Pain or shock. Didn't matter which.

She wrote: **Documentation complete. Transit time: 87 sets. Saint Merro distance from exit: sufficient for structural cascade. Casualties: catastrophic estimated.**

Below them, Saint Merro was breaking. She could hear it. Distant. Continuous. The sound of terrace systems failing in sequence.

She'd been right. Four hundred spans wasn't safe. Four hundred spans was just far enough to die slower.

Tiffani kept writing. Logged the thermal bloom duration. Logged the pressure wave magnitude. Logged the gravity shift characteristics.

Someone would find these logbooks. Someone would read them. Someone would know she'd calculated correctly and filed reports and continued observation through the full event.

Someone would know she'd done the work.

The platform shook. Another support failing. The angle got steeper.

Her leg screamed. The frame was crushing. She felt bone grinding. Felt something inside break.

She wrote: **Personal status: legs crushed. Blood loss significant. Consciousness unstable.**

Her vision blurred. She blinked. Forced focus. Finished the entry.

Then set down her chalk. Closed the logbook. Made sure it was secure in the case.

Documentation complete.

Tiffani laid her head on the tilted platform. Tasted copper-gold. Felt pain and heat and the wrongness of gravity.

Counted her heartbeats.

Waited.

---

Jhace emerged from the Underface into chaos.

Mid-terrace was collapsing. Buildings leaning. Streets cracked. People running. Screaming. No organization. Just panic.

Chloen was with the evacuation crew. Getting medical attention. Alive.

His hands wouldn't stop shaking.

The copper-gold dust fell like snow. Coated everything. Made the air taste metal. Made breathing hurt.

The grid-hum had stopped entirely. System failure. Network collapse. Saint Merro's infrastructure was dead.

Jhace walked through the streets. His tool kit on his shoulder. His hands shaking. The tremor had spread to his arms now. Constant vibration. Independent of external movement.

Cost.

He'd manifested something. Light. Force. Whatever that was. And it cost him. His hands. His motor control. Something fundamental in how his body functioned.

Permanent.

He knew it was permanent the same way he knew metal stress patterns. The change was structural. Not temporary damage. Not recoverable function.

He'd saved Chloen. Cost: his hands would shake forever.

The mathematics were simple. One life. One disability. The exchange was logged.

Jhace looked at his shaking hands. Turned them over. The burns showed. The faint copper-gold glow was fading but the tremor stayed.

He'd done something impossible. Moved weight muscle couldn't move. Generated light from intent. Changed reality through will.

And couldn't do it again. Didn't know how he'd done it the first time. Didn't know what he'd activated. Didn't know how to control it.

Just knew it had cost him. Cost visible. Cost permanent. Cost he'd carry every heartbeat for the rest of his life.

Around him, Saint Merro kept breaking. Buildings falling. People dying. The catastrophe Tiffani had calculated. The disaster no one prevented because reports sat in bins while the threat approached.

Jhace started walking toward Crown heights. Toward where the exit had been. Toward the epicenter.

His hands shook. The copper-gold dust fell. The world had broken and stayed broken.

And something inside him had changed. Something that glowed and cost and left marks.

He didn't know what came next.

He knew counting heartbeats wouldn't be enough anymore.

---

**END CHAPTER THREE**

[ASSISTANT]
## ORIENTATION TERMS (TE’OGA)

- **Crownward**: toward Crown heights / the Crown region  
- **Underfaceward**: toward the Underface / lower terraces  
- **Seaward**: toward open ocean  
- **Shelfward**: toward the coastal shelf / landward edge of sea  
- **Spinward / Counterspinward**: lateral bearings on charts (rotation-aligned), used only when a chart-grade direction matters  
- Prefer **named regions** over any cardinal labeling.

---

# **Chapter Two: Eight Tides**

Tiffani adjusted the theodolite’s rear foot until the bubble centered. Checked the other three. All seated. She wiped glass dust off her fingertips and opened her logbook to a fresh page.

Eighth Tide of observation.

The object held position in her lens field. Still there. Still approaching. Still impossible.

She wrote the header: **Target designation: Unknown-One. Observation sequence continues.**

Her hands were raw. Chalk residue dried the skin. Small cuts from lens edge work decorated her knuckles. She’d stopped bandaging them after the third Tide. The work mattered more than comfort.

The theodolite sight aligned on the object. She took the measurement. Logged the angle. Compared it to yesterday’s reading.

Offset confirmed. Movement consistent with projected trajectory.

She pulled out her calculation slate. Drew the curve. Extended the line. The mathematics held clean. Velocity constant. Path unwavering. No course correction. No deceleration.

Whatever this object was, it wasn’t stopping.

She measured again. Double-checked the reference stars. Vela-Nine stable. Corros-Twelve stable. Control points confirmed instrument accuracy.

The object kept approaching.

Tiffani wrote: **Velocity: 8,200 spans per set. Thermal signature: absent. No heat bloom detected. No combustion trail. No atmospheric interaction visible at current distance.**

That shouldn’t be possible. Objects moving at this velocity should show heat. Should show friction effects when they hit atmosphere. Should show something.

This showed nothing. Just movement. Clean. Precise. Wrong.

She recorded the sky deformation. A narrow distortion formed along the projected path. Barely visible. Measurable only because she’d been watching the same star field for eight Tides straight.

The stars bent. Not much. Fraction of a span displacement. But stars didn’t bend. Stars held position. Stars were fixed reference points.

Except now they weren’t.

She wrote: **Sky deformation detected along projected trajectory. Displacement magnitude increasing. Pattern suggests object mass sufficient to warp local geometry during approach.**

Her throat tightened. She forced the next breath to complete.

This wasn’t meteor behavior. Wasn’t comet behavior. Wasn’t anything in her manuals.

She calculated the entry coordinates. Drew the angle of approach. Projected the impact point.

Open ocean off the shelfline. Seaward of Saint Merro. Hundreds of cables from the nearest terrace city.

Relief tried to surface. She pressed it down with calculation.

Entry point determined. But objects this massive didn’t just impact and stop. They had exit vectors. They had force propagation. They had secondary effects.

She ran the numbers again.

If the object maintained current velocity through planetary mass… if it retained structural integrity during passage… if it didn’t fragment or decelerate significantly…

Exit point: Crown region.

Distance from Saint Merro to projected exit line: four hundred spans.

Her jaw locked. She recalculated. Got the same result. Ran it a third time with different geometric assumptions.

Four hundred spans. Maybe four hundred twenty. The uncertainty margin didn’t matter. Saint Merro sat close enough to the exit trajectory that secondary effects would propagate directly through the terrace foundation structure.

Not vaporized. Structurally compromised.

She wrote: **Saint Merro assessment: outside primary impact zone. Within secondary effect radius. Seismic propagation through terrace support systems probable. Structural failure cascade likely.**

Her hands pressed too hard. The chalk snapped.

She set down the broken piece. Pulled a fresh length from her kit. Kept writing.

She’d filed reports. Three of them. One to the Astronomical Guild. One to the Regional Research Office. One to the Regional Emergency Authority.

All stamped. All acknowledged. All requesting continued observation and trajectory refinement.

None ordering evacuation.

The Emergency Authority response sat in her pocket. She pulled it out. Read it again.

*Your calculations are noted. Please continue observation and provide updated trajectory data as available. Regional coordination requires confirmed threat assessment before evacuation protocols activate. Maintain standard reporting schedule.*

Standard reporting schedule. While an object the size of a small mountain approached at velocities that shouldn’t exist.

She folded the response. Put it back in her pocket. Returned to the theodolite.

The object was still there. Still approaching. Still not stopping.

She measured the sky deformation again. The bend had increased. Stars displaced by measurable fractions. The distortion spread wider along the approach path.

Something was tearing. Not the stars. The space between them.

Tiffani logged it. Drew the deformation pattern. Calculated the expansion rate.

By the time the object reached atmosphere, the sky would show a visible wound.

She wrote: **Projected atmospheric entry: Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide two — Kindling phase. Entry window: fourteen to eighteen sets past Kindling start. Transit duration through planetary mass: estimated eighty-four to ninety-two sets. Exit event follows.**

There. Locked. Dated. Inevitable.

She checked her lens array. Six lenses ground to specification. Each one calibrated for different observation needs. She’d prepared them over the last Anneal. Ground the curves herself because the guild lenses weren’t precise enough for the measurements she needed.

Her fingertips bled when she finished the grinding work. Glass dust embedded in the cuts. She washed them. Ground more lenses. The precision mattered more than her hands.

She looked through the observation lens again. The object filled more of the field than it had this same phase yesterday. Approach confirmed. Acceleration constant.

No heat. No trail. No visible combustion.

Just approach.

Tiffani pulled out her district map. Marked Saint Merro’s position. Drew the projected exit line. Calculated the angle of force propagation through terrace structure.

Lower levels would fail first. Underface districts. Then mid-terrace. Crown heights might hold if the foundation anchors survived the initial shear stress.

Might.

She wrote: **Underface collapse: high probability. Mid-terrace structural compromise: confirmed. Crown heights survival: dependent on foundation anchor integrity and seismic force magnitude.**

The mathematics didn’t lie. The institutions did. Or they just didn’t believe the mathematics mattered enough to act.

She filed the eighth report. Walked it to the guild messenger station herself. Watched them stamp it. Watched them file it with the others.

“Still tracking that object?” the clerk asked.

“Correct.”

“Anything changed?”

“It’s closer.”

The clerk nodded. Filed the report. Went back to processing standard observational data.

Tiffani returned to the observatory.

The object was still there. Still approaching.

She logged the Tide end: **Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide one — Quench.**

Next Tide was Tide two. Kindling phase. Entry window.

She prepared her observation array. Checked every lens. Verified every measurement tool. Set backup slates in case primary documentation failed.

She would observe through entry. Through transit. Through exit.

Someone needed to document what happened. Someone needed to record the data. Someone needed to count the cost.

Her hands were raw from glass work. Her eyes strained from continuous observation. Her throat was dry from not drinking enough water because leaving the observatory meant missing measurements.

None of that mattered.

The object was coming.

Saint Merro sat four hundred spans from the exit line.

Far enough to avoid vaporization.

Close enough to die anyway.

Tiffani returned to the theodolite. Took another measurement. Logged it.

The object kept approaching.

The sky kept bending.

The reports kept going into bins where no one read them fast enough to matter.

She pulled her observation chair to the theodolite mount. Sat down. Set her logbook on her lap.

Next Tide, the object would enter atmosphere.

Next Tide, her calculations would become facts.

Next Tide, she would document exactly how right she’d been and exactly how wrong “safe” turned out to be.

She wrote one more line: **Observation continues through entry event. Documentation priority: absolute.**

Then she settled in to wait.

Behind her, Saint Merro went about its normal Quench routines. Workers ending their Watches. Families preparing for rest cycle. Guilds processing their daily quotas.

Four hundred spans from where the world was about to break.

Close enough.

---

# **Chapter Three: The Piercing** (Directional-clean)

## PART ONE: ENTRY

Tiffani’s hands were steady on the theodolite mount.

**Casting 1305, Crucible eight, Anneal one, Tide two — Kindling phase. Fourteen sets past phase start.**

The object filled her lens field now. Not a distant point. A visible mass. Shape resolving as it closed the final distance.

Spindle. Elongated. Tapered points at both ends. The surface reflected no light. Absorbed everything. A void-shape cutting through star field.

She logged: **Visual confirmation: length approximately 4,400 cables. Diameter at center approximately 670 cables. Surface composition: unknown. Reflectivity: zero.**

The sky deformation had spread. Stars bent in a wide curve along the approach path. The distortion looked wrong. Looked like someone had pulled the sky taut and pressed their thumb into it.

Tiffani’s throat was dry. She swallowed. Tasted copper already. Atmospheric contamination beginning.

The object entered upper atmosphere.

No heat bloom. No friction glow. Just passage. Clean. Impossible. The air should have been on fire around it.

She wrote: **Atmospheric entry confirmed. Thermal signature: still absent. Friction effects: none detected. Object maintains structural integrity.**

Pressure changed. Her ears popped. The air felt thinner. Harder to pull into her lungs.

She forced the next breath to complete. Kept her eye to the lens.

The sky tore.

Not metaphor. Actual visible rupture. The atmosphere split along the object’s path. A seam opened. Dark line against blue. The edges didn’t heal. Didn’t close. Just stayed open. Bleeding.

Tiffani’s hands shook. She locked them down. Wrote: **Sky seam formation confirmed. Atmospheric wound persistent. Non-closing tear along entry trajectory.**

The object kept descending. Velocity constant. No deceleration. No course change. Just approach.

She checked her clock. Twenty-two sets since Kindling start.

Impact in approximately forty sets.

The pressure kept dropping. Her vision blurred at the edges. She blinked. Forced focus. The lens field stayed clear.

Copper taste intensified. Metallic. Sharp. Coating her tongue and throat. She spat over the platform rail. The saliva came out faintly gold.

Contamination.

She logged it: **Atmospheric particulate detected. Copper-gold coloration. Taste: metallic. Appears in saliva and respiratory system.**

The observatory platform vibrated. Low frequency. Felt through boot soles before heard. The stone trembled.

Tiffani grabbed the theodolite mount. Steadied herself. The vibration increased.

Seismic precursor. Pressure wave traveling through bedrock ahead of impact.

She wrote: **Ground vibration detected. Frequency increasing. Seismic activity commencing.**

The object hit ocean.

She didn’t see the impact. Too far. Wrong angle. But she felt it.

The pressure wave arrived first. Slammed into her chest. Knocked breath out. She gasped. Couldn’t refill her lungs for three heartbeats.

Then sound.

Not sound. Violence. Acoustic assault that made her jaw ache and her vision shake and her bones rattle inside her skin.

The platform bucked. Stone grinding. Glass cracking somewhere below. The theodolite mount shifted. She grabbed it with both hands. Held on.

The sky seam widened. Spread like a wound tearing further. The darkness inside it pulsed.

Tiffani’s hands were shaking now. Not fear. The platform shaking her. The world shaking everything.

She forced her grip to stay locked on the mount. Forced her eye back to the lens.

The object had entered ocean. Started planetary transit.

She checked her clock. Impact confirmed at forty-seven sets past Kindling start.

Transit duration: eighty-four to ninety-two sets estimated.

She had that long to document. That long before exit. That long before Saint Merro found out what four hundred spans from the exit line meant.

The copper taste got worse. She spat again. More gold in the saliva. The particulate was falling. Microscopic. Coating everything.

Tiffani pulled out a clean slate. Set up backup documentation. Primary and secondary recording. Redundancy in case one failed.

The platform kept shaking. The seismic activity wasn’t stopping. Wasn’t aftershocks. Continuous vibration as the object traveled through planetary mass.

She wrote: **Ocean impact confirmed. Planetary transit commenced. Seismic activity continuous. Atmospheric contamination increasing. Documentation continues.**

Her logbook pages fluttered. Wind rising. Wrong direction. Wrong pressure. Air moving toward the impact site. Being pulled in.

She weighted the pages with her chalk box. Kept writing.

The sky seam pulsed. The edges glowed faintly copper-gold. The wound was bleeding the same contamination the object shed.

Tiffani’s hands cramped from gripping the mount. She flexed her fingers. Locked them back down.

Transit had begun.

Saint Merro had eighty-four to ninety-two sets before exit.

She settled in to count them.

---

## PART TWO: TRANSIT

[unchanged]

---

## PART THREE: EXIT

**Eighty-seven sets after ocean entry.**

Tiffani’s eyes burned. She’d been at the lens for the full transit. Documenting. Measuring. Counting.

The seismic activity had been continuous. The platform shook. Glass cracked in the observatory dome. But the structure held.

She’d logged it all.

Now the exit was coming.

She checked her projected coordinates. Crown region. Four hundred spans from Saint Merro.

Close enough.
